description: In the area of private houses in gB9I, excavators discovered a small archive of 3 clay tablets and an envelope. The archive, dating between 655 and postcanonical 641* BC, consists of loan documents concerning silver and barley.

name: Assur Archive 50
description: In a private house in gD10I, excavators discovered a small archive 4 fragmentary clay tablets. The archive, dating to the 7th century BC, consists of juridicial settlements and a loan document.

name: Assur Archive 51
description: At the eastern corner of the large ziggurat in Assur, a library, belonging to a family of scribes, was unearthed in a house in area hB4V (Assur Archive 18). In a door opening of a small house nearby, excavators found a clay pot with 13 tablets inside. This separate archive belongs to Aššur-bissu-epuš and other men. It also dates to the 7th-century BCE. and contains various types of documents, including a juridical settlement and a document concerning the deposit of a chair.

name: Assur Archive 52a
description: In a fill from various rooms of a private house (in area eA8V), excavators discovered an archive of 75 texts, belonging to Duri-Aššur and his business partners. The archive consists of letters, purchase and loan documents, divisions of inheritance, and other legal documents, as well as administrative lists. Most of these texts are concerned with trade. The available datings suggest that Duri-Aššur and his business partners were active in the trade north of Assur from 651 to the destruction of the city in 614 BC. Notably, some documents exemplify the financial independency of Egyptian women in Assur.

name: Assur Archive 52b
description: In a fill from private house in Room 1A2 (in area eA8V), excavators discovered an archive of 15 texts, belonging to a group of Egyptians. Some of these people have Egyptian names, others have Assyrian names. Available datings belong to a period of some 30 years in the mid-7th century BC. The archive consists of legal documents.

name: Assur Archive 53
description: In a private house, south of the Parthian Palace, near the western wall of the New City of Assur, excavators discovered an archive of 52 clay tablets. Of the 34 published tablets, most date to the postcanonical period. The only two exceptions date to 679 and 666 BC. and two tablets are undated. The archive, belonging to Aššur-matu-taqqin, contains purchase documents, loan documents, juridicial settlements, a letter, an adoption document, and others.

name: Assyrian rock relief at Yağmur
description: Inscribed relief, dated to the reign of Tiglath-Pileser I, on a large block near the fortified site of Yağmur in the Tur 'Abdin.

name: Atarneus
description: An ancient Greek city in Aeolis that lies on the mainland opposite the island of Lesbos, northeast of the town of Dikili in modern-day Turkey.

name: Ateste
description: Ateste was a city of northern Italy, located in the interior of Venetia.

name: Athena Kranaia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Athena Kranaia, T.

name: Athenaeum of Hadrian (Rome)
description: The Athenaeum of Hadrian was located in regio VII near the Capitoline Hill in Rome. Founded by the Emperor Hadrian, it was designated for the promotion of literary and scientific studies and named after the city of Athens. Excavations connected to the Metro C line revealed a complex in the vicinity of the modern Piazza Venezia that has been identified as the site of the Athenaeum.

name: Athis/Neocaesarea
description: A first- through tenth-century CE settlement located on the right bank of the Euphrates River in Syria's Aleppo Governorate, the site of Dibsi Faraj was excavated in the 1970s ahead of the Tabqa Dam project which eventually inundated the area. The excavations were jointly organized by Dumbarton Oaks and the University of Michigan under Richard Harper, following initial work by the Syrian culture ministry. The site was shown to be a heavily fortified one with Roman and Byzantine phases. It came to be abandoned after ca. 859 CE.

name: Atrium Vestae
description: The Atrium Vestae or House of the Vestals refers to an architectural complex on the northwest slope of the Palatine Hill.

name: Auditorium Maecenatis
description: An early Imperial building on the Esquiline Hill in Rome, the Auditorium Maecenatis has been connected with Maecenas as it stands in the area of his gardens (horti). While its precise function is unknown, the building may have served as a conservatory.

name: Augusteum, Antioch in Pisidia
description: The Augusteum, or Sebasteion, at Antioch in Pisidia was a monumental architectural complex dedicated to the Roman Imperial cult. Its archaeological remains were first excavated in 1913. Approximately 60 fragments of the Res Gestae Divi Augusti were discovered at the site.

name: Augusteum, so-called Basilica at Herculaneum
description: The Augusteum, or so-called Basilica, at Herculaneum, was partially investigated in the eighteenth century. This large complex (68 by 40 meters) had colonnades on three of its sides and faced on to the Decumanus Maximus across from the Basilica Noniana and the College of the Augustales.

name: Augustobriga
description: Augustobriga was an ancient city located on the left bank of the river Tagus, between Emerita Augusta and Toletum.

name: Auld Bourtreebush
description: Auld Bourtreebush is a prehistoric stone circle in Aberdeenshire, Scotland.

name: Aurelium Cananefatium/Forum Hadriani
description: Aurelium Cananefatium/Forum Hadriani (modern Voorburg) was the northernmost Roman city on the European continent. It was destroyed ca. AD 270.

name: Avaris/Ramesses
description: Avaris, located in the Nile delta region of Egypt, served as the capital city of the Hyksos.

name: Avebury stone circle
description: Avebury is a Neolithic henge monument comprised of three stone circles dating to the third millennium BCE.

name: Ayanis
description: Ayanis (or Ayanıs, now Ağartı Kalesi) is an Urartean fortress and settlement located 35 km north of Van on a rocky outcrop 250 m above Lake Van (1867 m above sea level). It was founded in the 7th century BCE under Rusa, son of Argišti, and was named Rusahinili Eidurukai ("The City of Rusa in front of Mount Eiduru"). Here two inscriptions of Rusa, son of Argišti (7th century BCE), have been found which report that Rusa cultivated the barren land, built the fortress and the city as well as a “tower temple” (“susi temple”) and a “Gate for the God Haldi” (A 12-01 and A 12-09).

name: Ayaz-Kala
description: An archaeological site in Northern Uzbekistan, dating between the fourth century BCE and the seventh century CE.

name: Babylon
description: The ancient city of Babylon began as an Akkadian city-state founded by Amorites in 1894 B.C. The city was also the seat of the Neo-Babylonian Empire from 608 to 539 B.C.

name: Bagai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Bagai

name: Bagistanus (mountain)
description: Mount Behistun is located in the Kermanshah Province of Iran. It is the site of the so-called Behistun Inscription, a multilingual inscription and rock relief established by Darius the Great (r. 522–486 B.C.).

name: Bahçecik
description: Urartian site containing a ruined building and some rock inscriptions.

name: Baiae
description: A Roman seaside community on the Bay of Naples that included thermal springs.

name: Bakchias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 E1 Bakchias

name: Bakr Āwā
description: Large mound with citadel in the plain of Shahrizor, occupied from Early Bronze Age to modern time.

name: Balāwāt
description: Balāwāt is the site of the ancient Assyrian city of Imgur-Enlil. A place from the TAVO Index.

name: Baliares/Gymnesiae Inss. (Maior et Minor)
description: Baliares/Gymnesiae Inss. (Maior et Minor) (The Balearic Islands) is an island group of the western Mediterranean Sea, located to the east of the Iberian peninsula. In modern geographical terms, the four largest islands of the group are Majorca, Minorca, Ibiza, and Formentera, but the latter two are also part of the Pityussae island group of antiquity.

name: Balıklı
description: A Neolithic site that is a contemporary of Aşıklı Höyük and located 14km to its north-east. Occupied ca. 8300–7900 BCE, Balıklı provides evidence for early sedentism in Central Anatolia.

name: Ballatha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Ballatha

name: Banasa
description: Colonia Iulia Valentia Banasa was one of the three colonies founded by emperor Augustus between 33 and 25 B.C. in Mauretania Tingitana.

name: Bantia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 D3 Bantia

name: Baou-des-Noirs
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 D2 Baou-des-Noirs

name: Barake (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 6 B5 Barake Inss.

name: Barbalissus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 F2 Barbalissus

name: Barç
description: A hilltop archaeological site in southeastern Albania, presently named after the adjacent modern village, that lies less than 2km northeast of modern Korça. Its precise nature is not yet known, but earlier suggestions that it was a burial mound have been refuted by the discovery of significant stone walls and small finds datable to the Iron Age and possibly the Hellenistic period.

name: Barm-i Dilak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Barm-i Dilak

name: Barous(s)ai? (islands)
description: The Nicobar Islands

name: Barreira Megalithic Complex
description: A prehistoric megalithic complex in the modern Sintra municipality in Portugal's Lisbon district. It was formally identified in 1961 and designated as a cultural heritage site in 1993. The complex consists of at least 24 standing stones.

name: Barzan (Gallo-Roman Site)
description: Site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary and subsequent city of the Roman imperial period, the "Site gallo-romain de Barzan" is spread across the modern French communes of Barzan, Talmont-sur-Gironde and Arces-sur-Gironde in the department of Charente-Maritime. BAtlas equated it with ancient Tannum/Tamnum, but alternatives continue to be offered.

name: Başbulak
description: Find spot for a stone with Urartian inscription.

name: Basilica Argentaria
description: The toponym "Basilica Argentaria" is a descriptor that appears in fourth-century CE sources for a porticoed complex that dates to the reign of Trajan. This complex was built between the slope of the Capitoline Hill and the Forum Iulium during the terraforming of the Quirinal Hill that created space for the construction of the Forum and Markets of Trajan.

name: Basilica Noniana
description: An architectural complex at Herculaneum that was renovated following the earthquake of A.D. 62 thanks to the patronage of Marcus Nonius Balbus. The site is only partly excavated. It faced onto the Decumaus Maximus, and lies directly across Cardo III from the College of the Augustales.

name: Bassiana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 B5 Bassiana

name: Bastam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 G2 Bastam

name: Batas-Harir
description: A first-century CE rock-cut relief at Batas-Harir is likely associated with the Parthian culture and depicts a ruler, perhaps the king Abdissares or Izates II or III of Adiabene. The site is located in Iraqi Kurdistan in the Erbil Governorate.

name: Bathos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Bathos

name: Baths of Faverges
description: The Roman Baths of Faverges date to the first century.

name: Bavian/Khinnis
description: Just a few kilometers upstream of the city, the 7th-century B.C. Assyrian king Sennacherib had an architectural complex built and several stele-like panels (with accompanying inscriptions) carved into a nearby cliff face. The site may have served as a summer residence for the Assyrian king and may have been used as a headquarters to supervise the construction of a vast network of canals and aqueducts that was created specifically for providing water to Nineveh and the Assyrian heartland. The "Khinnis Reliefs" were included on the 2014 World Monuments Watch to increase awareness of cultural heritage in the Khinnis/Bavian area.

name: Baysān
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Beer Sheva/Berosaba
description: Tel Be'er Sheva is an archeological site in southern Israel that is believed to be the location of the remains of the biblical town of Beersheba.

name: Beidha
description: Beidha is a Neolithic archaeological site located just north of Petra near Siq al-Barid in Jordan. Diana Kirkbride (1957) and Brian Byrd excavated the site. Three phases of occupation were identified beginning in the eleventh millennium BCE.

name: Beit Shemesh
description: City 30 km west of Jerusalem inhabited from the Bronze Age until its destruction in 701 CE.

name: Bele(g)ia/Veleia
description: Bele(g)ia/Veleia (Iruña-Veleia) was a Roman town along the ab Asturica Burdigalam road.

name: Belginum
description: An ancient settlement (vicus) of the Treveri. Located near modern Wederath (Bernkastel-Wittlich district) in Germany.

name: Beltany
description: At Beltany in County Donegal, Ireland, is a Neolithic stone circle monument comprised of 64 menhirs. The monument dates ca. 1400-800 BC.

name: Berà
description: A Roman triumphal arch dedicated ca. 13 BC by Lucius Licinius Sura and perhaps dedicated to Augustus.

name: Berenice Panchrysos
description: Berenice Panchrysos was a settlement in the Eastern Desert of Egypt. It was located near Sabae in the Regio Troglodytica (modern Sudan). Its name ("all golden") derives from its proximity to the gold mines Jebel Allaqi (Jebel Ollaki) that were exploited in antiquity.

name: Berenice/Berenike
description: An ancient port city on the west coast of the Red Sea in modern Egypt, Berenice/Berenike was founded in 275 BCE by Ptolemy II Philadelphus, who named it after his mother, Berenice I of Egypt.

name: Bersiba
description: Bersiba was an ancient site in Syria, inhabited as early as the Neolithic period. To the Hittites the site was known as Masuwari.

name: Beta Samati
description: Archaeological investigation at the site of Beta Samati, located between Aksum and the Red Sea, has revealed remains of a significant Aksumite administrative center. The archaeological site is located in the vicinity of Yeha, Ethiopia.

name: Bethar
description: A largely overbuilt ancient village located in the West Bank. Archaeology indicates occupation as early as the 8th century BCE. Herodean fortifications, extended and occupied by Bar Kokhba's forces, were destroyed after a Roman siege in 135 CE.

name: Bethsaida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Bethsaida?/Iulias?

name: Bhir Mound
description: An archaeological site located in Pakistan's Punjab province that includes some of the earliest remains of the city of Taxila, dating ca. 800–525 BCE.

name: Bhirrana
description: Bhirrana is an archaeological site located in the Fatehabad district of the north Indian state of Haryana. The site's initial occupation predates the so-called Indus Valley civilization with activity beginning in the 8th-7th millennium BCE.

name: Bigis/Bestia Desolata/Parabeste
description: An ancient settlement, located at modern Lashkargāh (formerly Bost/Boost) in Helmand province, Afghanistan.

name: Bir Abraq
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 E2 Bir Abraq

name: Bisutun/Bagistana/Vastan?/Baptana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 C2 Bisutun/Bagistana/Vastan?/Baptana

name: Bīt-Adad-erība
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is identified with the modern site of Tell Baqqaq, a ruined settlement mound located ca. 10 km south of Gir-e pan that is now under water.

name: Bithias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G2 Bithias

name: Blanda
description: Blanda was an ancient city of Lucania, although Pliny the Elder and Ptolemy differ as to its placement; the former places it near the coast, the latter inland. Modern scholars are in agreement that Ptolemy was incorrect. It is to be identified with the significant archaeological site at Palecastro di Tortora in modern Italy.

name: Blatobulgium
description: A Roman fort at present-day Birrens, cited: BAtlas 9 D5 Blatobulgium

name: Bolnisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Bolnisi

name: Boncuklu Tarla
description: Boncuklu Tarla is an archaeological site located in the Mardin province of Turkey. The human settlement was occupied from the Late Epipalaeolithic to Pre-Pottery Neolithic B periods.

name: Borsippa/Burs
description: An ancient city of Sumer, southwest of Babylon, on the east bank of the Euphrates River. Modern Birs Nimrūd in Iraq.

name: Borysthenes/Olbia
description: Commonly referred to as "Olbia Pontica" to distinguish it from multiple other ancient sites with the same name, the remains of this multi-period settlement are located immediately south of the modern Ukrainian village of Parutyne on the west bank of the Dnieper–Bug Estuary.

name: Bostel
description: The archaeological site of Bostel is located in Castelletto, municipality of Rotzo, province of Vicenza. The site is located some 850 meters above sea level. This ancient alpine village was inhabited from the fifth to first centuries BCE.

name: Bouleuterion
description: A Bouleuterion complex at Nicopolis in Epirus.

name: Bouleuterion at Akragas/Agrigentum
description: A bouleuterion complex at Akragas (modern Agrigento in Sicily).

name: Bouleuterion at Aspendos
description: The Bouleuterion at Aspendos dates to the early second century.

name: Bouleuterion at Dodona
description: Constructed during the Hellenistic period, this meeting hall served the Epirote league. It was destroyed and rebuilt multiple times before going out of use for this purpose during the early Roman empire.

name: Bouleuterion at Ietas
description: The Bouleuterion at Ietas dates to the later second century B.C.

name: Bouleuterion at Patara/Arsinoe
description: The Bouleuterion at Patara/Arsinoe sits on the agora opposite the theater.

name: Bouleuterion of Onchestos
description: The Bouleuterion of Onchestos has been identified adjacent to the Temple of Poseidon.

name: Braq
description: Khirbet Braq is the modern name of a ruined Nabatean sanctuary at Ayn Braq in Jordan, a spring that, in antiquity, was channeled through an aqueduct to supply ancient Petra. The heavily looted site is located just south of modern Wadi Musa, on the west side of the modern road to Taybeh.

name: Bronze Athena/Athena Promachos
description: A colossal bronze statue of Athena on the Acropolis of Athens, built in the mid-5th century BC by Pheidias. The base was repaired in the time of Augustus. In Late Antiquity, the statue was transferred to Constantinople, where it was destroyed in 1203.

name: Brusselstown Ring
description: A large fortified settlement in modern County Wicklow, Ireland, with evidence of occupation in the Late Bronze and Early Iron Ages.

name: Brykous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E4 Brykous

name: Buddhas of Bamyan
description: Monumental statues of Gautam Buddha were carved into the cliff-face at Bamyan, located in the Bamyan valley in the Hazarajat region of central Afghanistan, The statues were, respectively, 35 and 53 m tall. They were intentionally destroyed by the Taliban in 2001.

name: Building with Three Exedrae
description: The Building with Three Exedrae at Hadrian's Villa, Tivoli, is an architectural complex with a squared central court onto which three exedra with fountains open.

name: Büklükale
description: Hittite city.

name: Bulla Regia
description: A center for Numidian royalty, Bulla Regia first became an oppidum liberum in the reign of Augustus and then a colony under Hadrian. The site, which boasts significant archaeological remains, lies in modern Tunisia, about 5km north of the modern city of Jendouba. The site was reportedly known in the 19th century as Hammam-Darradji. Today, a small modern settlement named Aïn Babouch abuts the site (not to be confused with Babouch, which lies further north along the border with Algeria).

name: Bulls of Guisando
description: A series of Celtiberian sculptures located near El Tiemblo, Ávila, Spain. These statues were perhaps originally created during the second century BC.

name: Burmarina
description: Minor settlement in Upper Syrian Euphrates.

name: Burmarina Archive 1
description: The small town of Burmarina belonged to the area of the city Til-Barsip which was under Neo-Assyrian rule. An archive, consisting of about 130 clay tablets and fragments, including some bullae, was discovered in two rooms, presumably part of a private house, in Area F. Although most texts were written in Assyrian, some were in Aramaic, and some had short Aramaic notes written in ink. While most of the Assyrian texts were loan or purchase documents, the archive also includes short notes and a list. The Aramaic texts concern loans or are short notes written on bullae.

name: Busa delle Vette
description: A hut and some enclosures located in the Dolomites (Veneto, Italy), datable to between the 5th and 11th centuries CE.

name: Byblos
description: Occupied since the Neolithic period, historical Byblos was originally a major Punic center called Gebal.

name: Bylazora
description: Excavated since 2008, a settlement site near the town of Knezje in Macedonia's Sveti Nikole municipality, has been identified as ancient Bylazora.

name: Cabanes
description: The remains of a Roman arch consisting of two columns supported by a square base.

name: Cabeço do Vouga
description: The ancient settlement of Talabriga is probably to be associated with the Portuguese archaeological site known today as Cabeço do Vouga.

name: Caepionis Monu.
description: Caepionis Monumentum (Tower of Caepio) was a tower-cum-lighthouse built by the Roman proconsul Quintus Servilius Caepio in 140 BC. The aim was to make the Salmedina reef at the mouth of the river Baitis (Guadalquivir) navigable. The modern toponym is Chipiona.

name: Caere
description: Caere (also known as Cisra or Cerveteri) was a major Etruscan polis located ca. 50 km north-northwest of Rome in southern Etruria.

name: Camars
description: An Etruscan settlement covering some 17 ha located near the modern village of Gonfienti (Prato).

name: Camp of Diocletian at Palmyra
description: Camp of Diocletian in the Ancient City of Palmyra. Coordinates determined by eye in Google Earth in November 2012, following description in UNESCO World Heritage List.

name: Campochiaro
description: An Italic sanctuary sacred to Hercules located in località Civitella in the comune of Campochiaro. The site is to be connected with the Pentrian Samnites.

name: Canopus at Hadrian's Villa
description: The Canopus garden is set at the principal entrance to Hadrian's Villa. An imposing waterway occupies all its length.

name: Capalbiaccio Tricosto
description: The fortified site of Tricosto di Capalbiaccio is located in southern Tuscany, some 6 km inland from the ancient Latin colony of Cosa (Ansedonia). The archaeological site was initially identified by means of a field survey led by Stephen L. Dyson (1976-1980). Beginning in 2009 the site was again investigated. The site had a long occupational life, beginning as a minor Etruscan center (ca. seventh century BCE) and continuing in use through the Middle Ages (tenth century CE).

name: Capanna dell’Elceto
description: The so-called Capanna dell’Elceto was part of a fortified Protovillanovan settlement located near Monte Rovello, Italy.

name: Capena
description: An ancient urban center in Faliscan territory adjacent to Etruria, Capena was sacked by the Romans in 390 BC.

name: Capera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 E4 Capera

name: Capo Graziano
description: The prehistoric village of Capo Graziano dates to the Middle Bronze Age and is located on the island of Filicudi. The site was excavated between 1952 and 1969. It is situated on a terrace some 100 m above sea level. At least 27 agglomerative huts were identified at the site.

name: Carchemish
description: An ancient city that was part of the Mitanni, Hittite, and Neo-Assyrian Empires (Kargamiš in Hittite; commonly Carchemish). The site was occupied as early as the Neolithic and Chalcolithic periods; evidence for Early Bronze Age cist tombs begins ca. 2400 BC. It was a key commercial center during the time of Ebla and Mari. The Mitanni controlled the site from the sixteenth to the fourteenth centuries and was conquered by Šuppiluliuma ca. 1350 B.C. A famous battle, attested in the Bible, was fought at the site in 605 BC between the Babylonian army under Nebuchadnezzar II and that of the Egyptian Pharaoh Necho II.

name: Careiae
description: An ancient town on the Via Clodia.

name: Carmarthen
description: A Roman fort in Wales occupied from about 75 CE to at least 100 CE.

name: Carnac megaliths
description: Carnac (Breton: Karnag) is a site in southern Brittany known for the so-called Carnac stones, a massive complex of Neolithic menhir stones. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2025.

name: Casa dei Crescenzi
description: A structure in Rome built between 1040 and 1065 by one Niccolò, son of Crescenzio and Teodora, in order to control the Ponte Emilio, on whose transit the family charged a toll. The building incorporates ancient spolia.

name: Casa del Obispo
description: The archaeological site of Casa del Obispo has evidence for multiple urban phases, ranging from the eighth century B.C. to the eighteenth century.

name: Casae
description: Modern Henchir el Beguar/Begar, an archaeological site located in the Tunisian governorate of Kasserine. Inscriptions of the second century CE found here in the 19th century authorized the holding of regular markets at Casae and demonstrate that it was probably the principal settlement of the Saltus Beguensis, a Roman senatorial estate.

name: Casal Bernocchi-Malafede
description: An archaeological area along the Via Ostiensis with evidence of human activity stretching from the Archaic period to the time of the Roman Empire. SIgnificant archaeological features include an extensive necropolis and a Roman bathing facility.

name: Casas del Turuñuelo
description: An archaeological site located in Spain's Extremadura region, Casas del Turuñuelo is associated with the Tartessian culture and dates from the fifth century BCE.

name: Case Romane del Celio
description: An archaeological area beneath the basilica of the martyrs John and Paul that has been explored by means of excavation, revealing a series of Imperial period domestic remains dating between the second and fourth centuries A.D.

name: Caseggiato di Bacco e Arianna
description: The House of Bacchus and Ariadne at Ostia dates to the Hadrianic period and adjoined the Serapeum.

name: Caselle in Pittari
description: An ancient Lucanian settlement located within the territory of the comune of Caselle in Pittari has been explored via archaeological excavation since 2014. This site occupies parts of a large plateau. Evidence for an axial road running north-south and several large structures has been documented.

name: Casteddu di Ceccia
description: A stone-built monument of the Torrean civilization located south of Porto-Vecchio, Corsica.

name: Casteddu di Tappa
description: Casteddu di Tappa is a Bronze Age archaeological site in Corsica connected with the Torréen culture.

name: Castel Fiorentino
description: The castle was the site of the Holy Emperor Frederick II's death in 1250.

name: Castell Bryn Gwyn
description: Castell Bryn Gwyn is a circular Neolithic enclosure some 54 m in diameter that shows signs of re-use during the Iron Age and in the Roman period. The site is located on the Isle of Anglesey.

name: Castellazzo di Taneto
description: A Medieval fort dating from the mid-ninth to the eleventh century CE.

name: Castelliere di Monte Acuto
description: A multi-period site on Monte Acuto with evidence for habitation (Late Bronze Age) and cult activities (mid-first millennium BCE).

name: Castelvecchio di San Gimignano
description: The site of Castelvecchio di San Gimignano likely had sacred significance in the first millennium BCE. The area was occupied in the late sixth to early seventh centuries CE as it provided a location to control movement between southern Tuscany and Piedmont. The site was fortified in the twelfth century.

name: Castrum Invi
description: An Italic site located at the mouth of the Incastro river and in the territory of Ardea.

name: Castrum Minervae/Athenaion/Limen Aphrodites
description: An ancient settlement of the Sallentini in Apulia. Legendary history held that this was the spot where Aeneas first landed in Italy, thus the assignation 'Limen Aphrodites' or 'Portus Veneris'.

name: Castrum Novum
description: Castrum Novum was a colonia maritima established on the coast of Etruria.

name: Castrum Perticae
description: A fortified site of the later Byzantine period located at Finale Ligure.

name: Castulo
description: An Ibero-Roman settlement of Baetica that was inhabited beginning in the Neolithic period. It is noted for the silver mines of the Sierra Morena.

name: Cat Stane
description: An irregular standing stone bearing a fragmentary Latin inscription, the Cat Stane is likely a Neolithic monument re-used for a burial of the fifth or sixth century AD.

name: Çatıören
description: Çatıören is the modern name for ruins of the Hellenistic through Byzantine period in eastern Rough Cilicia

name: Caunus
description: An ancient city of Caria in southwest Asia Minor.

name: Caveirac milestone
description: A Roman milestone - (CIL 17-02, no. 256).

name: Celadussae (islands)
description: Celadussae Inss., a Dalmatian archipelago in the Adriatic Sea.

name: Cellarulo
description: An area in the Contrada Cellarulo of Benevento that is designated as an archaeological area with evidence for the later phases of ancient Benevento's history.

name: Cento Camerelle (Bacoli)
description: The so-called Cento Camerelle at Bacoli is an archaeological site that dates initially to the late republican period. A multi-story structure with provisions for a water system and cisterns, the building is linked initially to the Roman consul Quintus Hortensius Hortalus and later to Antonia Minor, the mother of the emperor Claudius.

name: Cercadilla
description: A Late Roman palace complex connected with the emperor Maximian.

name: Cercina (island)
description: A group of islands lying off the east coast of Tunisia in the Gulf of Gabès, the islands were a place of exile in Roman antiquity.

name: Cessati Spiriti
description: A ruined Roman mausoleum on the grounds of the Caffarella regional park. The so-called Cessati Spiriti mausoleum was once clad in travertine.

name: Chalasmenos
description: Chalasmenos is an archaeological site that preserves remains of a Late Minoan (LM) IIIC settlement. This site is located on a low hill (240 m asl) immediately to the south of the Cha Gorge near Monastiraki in eastern Crete.

name: Chalcidicum
description: Augustus uses the generic term Chalcidicum to describe a porch he built as an approach to the Curia Iulia.

name: Champlieu
description: A sanctuary located at the boundary of the civitas of the Suessiones and that of the Silvanectes.

name: Chanār-i Pāyn
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Chanctonbury Ring
description: Chanctonbury Ring is a prehistoric hill fort located on Chanctonbury Hill in West Sussex, England. It is part of the present-day South Downs Way.

name: Chani Kokkonas
description: Chani Kokkonas is an archaeological site in Tempe valley, Thessaly, Greece. The excavations revealed remains of a Hellenistic complex with both ritual and workshop areas, as well as a Byzantine church and guesthouse (khan).

name: Chapel of Wadjmose
description: Chapel dedicated to the prince Wadjmose, son of Pharaoh Thutmose I.

name: Charcha/‘Arcaiapis’/Karcharoman
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Charcha/‘Arcaiapis’/Karcharoman

name: Charterhouse Warren Farm Shaft
description: The Charterhouse Warren Farm Shaft is a vertical shaft (approximately 20 meters deep) in the Carboniferous limestone plateau of the Mendip Hills, Somerset, southwest England. Originally identified as a swallet, this cave feature was the site of archaeological excavations in 1972-6 and again in 1983-6. The site has produced evidence of several stratigraphic horizons and, notably, a number of disarticulated human and faunal remains. The human remains come from at least 37 Bronze Age individuals and their demise seems to have been the result of deliberate violence.

name: Chatal Höyük (Amuq)
description: Mound site in the Amuq Plain inhabited from the late 4th millennium BCE-mid-1st millennium BCE. In the Late Bronze Age Chatal Höyük was the site of a sizeable town with connections to the Hittite Empire. In the Iron Age the town was within the sphere of the Syro-Hittite state of Unqi, based at Tell Tayinat/Kinaliya. The town gradually declined, but remained inhabited without interruption until at least the fifth century BCE.

name: Chega Sofla
description: An archaeological site located in the Zeydun plain south of the provincial city of Behbahan, Iran.

name: Chelidoniae (islands)
description: A group of islands located off Cape Gelidonya on the south coast of Anatolia, modern Beşadalar (Five Islands), Turkey. Ancient commentators vary on the number of islands in the group, some counting only the largest two.

name: Chersonesos
description: Chersonesos was an ancient Greek colony founded in the southwestern part of the Crimean Peninsula, ancient Taurica. Settlers from Heraclea Pontica established the site in the sixth century B.C.

name: Chervony Mayak
description: Chervony Mayak (Kherson Oblast, Ukraine) is the site of a necropolis associated with the Late Scythian culture (second and mid-third centuries CE).

name: Chil Zena
description: A mountainous outcropping located to the west of Kandahar, Afghanistan. A flight of forty stone steps leads to the top of the outcropping. The bilingual inscription of the third century BCE known as the Kandahar Bilingual Rock Inscription is found here.

name: Chiyāpuykah
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Choburak-I
description: Cemetery site in the Altai Mountains containing burials associated with the Bulan-Koba culture (4th century CE).

name: Chogha Bonut
description: Chogha Bonut is an archaeological site located in the Khuzistan Province of Iran. It lies approximately 5km to the west of Chogha Mish. The tell was the site of a settlement of the Elamite civilization and was settled by ca. 7200 BCE.

name: Chogha Mish
description: The Chalcolithic settlement site of Chogha Mish, also known as Tappeh-ye Choghā Mīsh, dates originally to the early seventh millennium BCE and is located on the Susiana Plain of western Iran. The site was occupied continuously from the Neolithic period to the Proto-Literate period. It was eventually eclipsed by nearby Susa, but BAtlDir maintains its occupation through at least the end of the early Roman imperial period, and possibly later.

name: Choirades Nesoi
description: A small archipelago composed of two islands - San Pietro and San Paolo - in the Gulf of Taranto.

name: Choragic Monument of Lysicrates
description: The Choragic Monument of Lysicrates is a round structure erected in 335/4 BC in Athens to honor the victory of a choregos in a dramatic competition.

name: Chorazin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Chorazin

name: Chrysopolitissa complex
description: The Chrysopolitissa complex in Paphos is an archaeological area that includes various ruins. Among these are those of an early Christian basilica (fourth to seventh centuries), a medieval Franciscan church (thirteenth century), and the so-called Pillar of St. Paul.

name: Chryssi-Gaidouronisi
description: The archaeological remains of a small Minoan settlement of approximately 12-20 houses on the island of Chryssi. Evidence for murex processing was discovered during excavations conducted in 2008.

name: Chughā Gāwānah
description: Large mound in the middle of Shahabad-e Gharb (Islamabad) in Western Iran, possibly ancient Palum.

name: Chughā Pahn East
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Chughā Pahn West
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Chughā Sabz
description: A mound where a cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Cillium
description: Cillium (modern Kasserine, Tunisia) occupies a strategic location in the gap between the Semmama and Chambi mountains, along the road from Gafsa.

name: Çine Tepecik Höyük
description: Human occupation at Çine-Tepecik begins in the Chalcolithic period. The site is located in a fertile alluvial plain near the Meander River in western Anatolia. In the Late Bronze Age, this place was located in the Arzawa lands.

name: City Eleusinion at Athens
description: The City Eleusinion was one of the most important sanctuaries in the religious life of ancient Athens. It was the urban “branch” of the sanctuary of Demeter and Kore at Eleusis in use during the Archaic and Classical periods.

name: Cividade de Bagunte
description: The Cividade de Bagunte is a fortified Iron Age site that became Romanized. It is located in the municipality of Vila do Conde in the Porto district of Portugal.

name: Civita d'Arlena
description: Civita d'Arlena (also known as Civita del Fosso di Arlena) is an archaeological site that occupies a hilltop near Lake Bolsena. It was the site of Etruscan occupation until the third century BCE. Excavations carried out by the École française de Rome (1952 to 1958) revealed parts of a fortification wall and a sacred building, along with evidence for a city plan.

name: Claterna
description: Claterna was a Roman a town of Gallia Cispadana, located along the Via Aemilia between Bononia and Forum Cornelii.

name: Clickimin broch
description: A Broch tower with other associated Iron Age structures.

name: Co(n)silinum/Cosilianum
description: Co(n)silinum/Cosilianum was a statio on the Via Popilia (Annia) in ancient Calabria.

name: Col di Marzo, Perugia
description: Survey and excavation at Montelabate overlooking the upper Tiber valley in the territory of Perugia have revealed evidence for an Etruscan settlement on the hill of Col di Marzo (Perugia). This place is located some 10 km from Arna and rises to 645 meters above sea level.

name: Col. Clunia (Sulpicia)
description: Col. Clunia (Sulpicia) was a Roman city of Hispania Tarraconensis. The site was besieged by Cn. Pompeius Magnus during the war against Sertorius.

name: Col. Ulpia Traiana/Tricensimae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G1 Col. Ulpia Traiana/Tricensimae

name: Colle delle Fate
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Colle delle Fate

name: Colle Madore
description: Colle Madore is an archaeological site connected with the Sicanians.

name: College of the Augustales
description: The so-called College of the Augustales at Herculaneum was built at the end of the first century BC. The brothers A. Lucius Proculus and A. Lucius Iulianus served as patrons of the project.

name: Collegium at Italica
description: A building that has been identified as the seat of a Collegium because of its internal structure and size, was previously known as the"House of the Exedra".

name: Colonnacce
description: Le Colonnacce refers to preserved elements of the lateral, north-south Corinthian-order colonnades that flanked the Forum Transitorium.

name: Colossus Neronis
description: The "Colossus Neronis" or "Colossal Statue of Nero" was a monumental bronze statue of the first-century CE emperor reportedly created by Zenodorus. The statue was originally an element of the sprawling Domus Aurea of Nero but the monument endured long after Nero's reign. The statue's original position likely marked the summit of the Velian Hill. The Flavians refashioned the statue to resemble Sol and Hadrian relocated the statue nearer the Flavian amphitheater as part of the work to create the Temple of Venus and Roma.

name: Column of Julian
description: The Column of Julianus, ca. 362 CE, honors the military exploits of the Roman Emperor Julian.

name: Column of Marcus Aurelius
description: The Column of Marcus Aurelius is a monumental victory column erected along the Via Lata between 176 and 193 CE. The monument celebrated victories realized against the Marcomanni and the Sarmatians (172-175 CE).

name: Column of Trajan
description: The Column of Trajan (or Trajan's Column) is a victory column that was erected in the Forum of Trajan at Rome ca. 113 in order to commemorate Trajan's victories in the Dacian Wars. The column is particularly noted for its helical historiated bas relief frieze decoration.

name: Comana Pontica/Hierocaesarea
description: An ancient city of Pontus.

name: Comitium of Pompeii
description: The complex referred to as the Comitium of Pompeii is located in the southeast corner of the square of the Forum and was built during the second century BC.

name: Comitium of Poseidonia/Paestum
description: The comitium is a component of a monumental forum complex constructed at Paestum after the city became a Latin colony in 273 B.C.

name: Complesso dei Riti Magici
description: The so-called "complex of the magic rites" is a building located in Regio II of the archaeological site of Pompeii. Excavations were conducted in 1954, 1957, 1963, and 1985. The modern toponym is derived from various ritual items discovered in the complex, which may have been dedicated to the cult of Sabazios.

name: Complesso episcopale
description: The Late Antique to early Medieval "Episcopal" complex of the Santissima Trinità located at Venosa.

name: Complutum
description: Complutum (modern Alcalá de Henares in Spain) was an ancient oppidum founded in the first century CE and largely re-fashioned in the third century.

name: Conimbriga
description: Conimbriga was a Celtic and Roman settlement in Portugal that was inhabited between the ninth century BC and the seventh century AD.

name: Constantinian Columbarium
description: The so-called Constantinian Columbarium is actually a second-century AD tomb on the Via Appia.

name: Coptos
description: An ancient settlement on the Nile river (modern Quft/Qift in Egypt) that flourished from pre-dynastic times through late antiquity.

name: Corent
description: Corent was the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman oppidum.

name: Corfinium
description: Corfinium was the principal city of the Paeligni in the valley of the Aternus river. The site becomes the capital of the breakaway Italic groups that oppose Rome during the Social War.

name: Corinthus/Korinthos
description: The ancient Greek and Roman city of Corinth, located in the Peloponnese, Greece. Also known today as Archaia Korinthos and not to be confused with the nearby modern town.

name: Cornus
description: Cornus was an ancient settlement on the west coast of Sardinia. The site is located in the modern municipal territory of S. Caterina di Pittinuri.

name: Cortaillod-Est
description: Settlement on the northern shore of Lake Neuchâtel in Switzerland containing the inundated remains of a Bronze Age village, inhabited ca. 1010-955 BC.

name: Corviano
description: Corviano (Bomarzo) is a protected landscape with evidence of Etruscan, Roman, and Medieval activity.

name: Costia dei Lippi
description: The archaeological area of Costia dei Lippi is located near the contemporary cemetery of Vetulonia. Excavations in this area began in the 1960s and revealed a series of stone walls built in a squared technique, as well as a system of terraces and a paved road. The area has been interpreted as a domestic quarter dating ca. the third to the first century BCE.

name: Cowley Roundabout Roman Settlement
description: Roman settlement, with evidence for agricultural, quarrying, and settlement activity during the second to fourth centuries CE, together with a possible mutatio (horse-changing station) along the Roman road linking Glevum (modern Gloucestershire) and Corinium (modern Cirencester).

name: Crawford
description: Site of a small Roman fort between Castledykes and Tassiesholm. The fort was discovered in 1938 and excavated in 1961-66.

name: Cremna/Col. Iulia Augusta Felix
description: A significant Hellenistic settlement of Pisidia that received an Augustan colony, taking the name Colonia Iulia Augusta Felix Cremnensium.

name: Crnobuki
description: An ancient site, known locally as "Gradishte", located near the modern North Macedonian village of Crnobuki has so far yielded evidence for occupation from the Bronze Age at least through the Hellenistic period. BAtlDir repeated conjecture that it might be identifiable with the Macedonian-era "Perseis" attested in literature.

name: Cross dyke and bowl barrow on the northern spur of Beacon Hill
description: A cross dyke in Burghclere, Basingstoke and Deane, England, United Kingdom.

name: Crucuno stone rectangle
description: A rectangle of 21 menhirs ranging in height from 0.91 m to 2.4 m.

name: Cryptoporticus (Alife)
description: An aisled Cryptoporticus at Alife that dates to the Augustan period.

name: Cuccuru Nuraxi
description: A nuragic site near Cagliari, Sardinia.

name: Cuicul
description: A Berbero-Roman town of North Africa (modern Djémila in Algeria). Sometimes labeled on maps and in travel information as "Curculum".

name: Cullompton Forts
description: Two Roman forts and two Roman camps at St Andrew's Hill, Cullompton, Devon, England.

name: Cuneiform findspot close to the citadel of ‘Ammān
description: Location on a slope south-east of the citadel of ‘Ammān (modern Jordan) where cuneiform texts were discovered.

name: Cuniculariae (islands)
description: The modern archipelago of Maddalena off northern Sardinia is comprised of seven main islands and numerous other small islets.

name: Curia Iulia
description: Construction of the Curia Julia was begun by Julius Caesar and completed by Augustus in 29 BCE. It replaced the former senate house, the Curia Hostilia.

name: Cutha
description: Kutha or Cutha (modern Tell Ibrahim) is located on the Upper Euphrates, north of Nippur and northeast of Babylon. The archaeological site consists of two tells. Archaeological evidence of the Neo-Babylonian period has been revealed by excavation.

name: Cyclades Islands
description: An island group in the Aegean Sea composed of over 200 islands.

name: Cyclopum Scopuli Tres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G3 Cyclopum Scopuli Tres

name: Cypriae (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 E5 Cypriae Inss.

name: Cyrrus/Hagioupolis
description: Seleucid kings (either Seleucus I Nicator or Antiochus I Soter), likely founded Cyrrhus in the first half of the third century B.C.

name: D(o)ura/Europos
description: An ancient settlement on the Euphrates river in modern Syria.

name: Dah-i Nū
description: Mound of Elamite city.

name: Dahan-i Ghulaman/Zra(n)ka?
description: A mud brick urban site in Sīstān, Iran, with a main period of occupation around the fifth century BCE. It was probably the Achaemenid provincial capital of Zarin or Zranka in Drangiana until it was buried by sand or dried out by movements of the Helmand endorheic basin.

name: Dal'verzin tepe
description: Dal'verzin tepe is an archaeological site located in Uzbekistan. The site is located near the Surkhan­darya River, approximately 60 km northeast of Termez. The settlement has its origins as a Greco-Bactrian town that developed into a larger, fortified settlement.

name: Dan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Dan

name: Daphnai
description: Ancient town of Defenneh in the Egyptian Nile Delta, primarily of the Late Period and Ptolemaic Era.

name: Dara/Anastasioupolis
description: Dara/Anastasioupolis was a fortified Roman city of northern Mesopotamia located on the border with the Sassanid Empire.

name: Dard(...)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 C6 Dard(...)

name: Daskyleion
description: Daskyleion (modern Hisartepe at Ergili in Turkey) was the capital of the Persian satrapy of Phrygia from the sixth century BC until the conquests of Alexander the Great.

name: Davti-blur
description: Davti-blur is the Armenian name of an Urartian settlement situated on a natural hill near the modern village Nor-Armavir. Just like the settlement on the immediately adjacent hill, Armavir, which in Urartian was called Argištihinili ("city of Argišti"), it was founded by Argišti I, son of Minua (8th century BCE). In Davti-Blur, a column base with an inscription of Argišti I was found, which reports the construction of a building. Furthermore, several inscriptions on clay pithoi by Argišti have been found there, which attest to the administrative activity at this place. A stone block of the Urartian king Sarduri II, son of Argišti I (8th century BCE), reveals that the place was further developed and extended by Sarduri II.

name: Dawali
description: Dawali is a site in the middle of the Euphrates region and was part of the kingdom of Suhu. The city was situated on the east bank of the Euphrates, a few kilometres downstream of Anat (Anatho). The modern city of the same name (Ad Dawālī) is on the river's west bank. The city's ancient name is not known.

name: Değirmenköy
description: A findspot of an Urartian inscription.

name: Deir Abu Sha'ar el-Qibli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 D3 Deir Abu Sha'ar el-Qibli

name: Deir el-Bahari
description: Deir el-Bahari is a complex of mortuary temples and tombs that constitute a part of the Theban necropolis located on the west bank of the Nile river, opposite Luxor.

name: Delos (settlement)
description: The ancient settlement of Delos, located on the Aegean island of the same name.

name: Dema House
description: A country house in Attica dating to the fourth century BCE located near the fort and wall at Dema.

name: Demeter Sanctuary (Eleusis)
description: The Sanctuary of Demeter at Eleusis in Attica was the site of the celebration of the Eleusinian mysteries, observed from the Archaic period to Late Antiquity.

name: Demeter Temple (Aegae - Yuntdağı)
description: A temple complex at Aegae-Yuntdağı, associated with the cult of Demeter and Kore based on epigraphic evidence, may also have been sacred to other divinities. The site is located to the west of the Scaenae frons of the theater complex.

name: Deorum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 unlocated Deorum Inss.

name: Dereüstü
description: Find spot for fragmentary stele with Urartian inscription.

name: Deru/Beth Daraya/[Badra]
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 A3 Deru/Beth Daraya/[Badra]

name: Devade (islands)
description: The modern Farasan Islands.

name: Diabetai (islands)
description: A small island group off the west coast of Syme.

name: Dibbin
description: A settlement mound where a cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Didymae (islands)
description: An ancient group of islands, identified by scholars with the modern Diliktaş Adaları in the Gulf of Fethiye on Turkey's southern coast.

name: Didymai? (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 B2 Didymai? Inss.

name: Didymoi (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 G3 Didymoi Inss.

name: Dilbat
description: Dilbat (modern Tell ed-Duleym), a small city southeast of Babylon on the eastern bank of the Euphrates River, was the cult center of the god Uraš and the goddess Ninegal. The site consists of two mounds of ruins: the larger, eastern mound contains the remains of earlier building phases (going back to the city’s founding in the Early Dynastic Period, ca. 2700 BC), while the smaller, western ruin hill contains the first-millennium-BC and later building occupations (down to the early Islamic Period). Little is known about the cultic topography of this small Babylonian city. Two first-millennium-BC ziggurat lists and a few Neo-Assyrian and Neo-Babylonian royal inscriptions record that Dilbat’s main temple was E-ibbi-Anum and its ziggurat was Eguba’anki, both were dedicated to Uraš. The temple of Ninegal (Bēlet-ēkalli) might have been named Esapar. Recent excavations on the eastern mound have unearthed the Kassite-Period remains of E-ibbi-Anum.

name: Dilkaya Höyüğü
description: An ancient tell located approximately 24 km from the city center of Van. Dilkaya Höyüğü is located just west of Dilkaya Village in the southwest of the Edremit district. It lies on the northern side of Hoşap Stream, on the shores of Lake Van.

name: Dimum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 C5 Dimum

name: Diomedeae (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D7 Diomedeae Inss.

name: Diomedeae Islands
description: An Adriatic archipelago north of Mons Garganus. Modern Isole Tremiti.

name: Dion
description: Dion was a settlement of Pieria located on the border between Macedonia and Thessaly. It was held, traditionally, as the place of Orpheus' death and burial. In the Roman Imperial period, it became a Roman colonia. The adjacent modern village, now known as Dion, is sometimes also referenced by its earlier modern name Malathria.

name: Dionysiades (islands)
description: An island grouping off the north coast of Crete, opposite Siteia. Known today also as the Yanisades Islands.

name: Diospolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 unlocated Diospolis

name: Divorum
description: A porticus constructed by Domitian in A.D. 80 on the eastern Campus Martius on the site of the Villa Publica, whose northern entry—a triumphal arch—was flanked by two small temples in honor of Titus and Vespasian.

name: Djebel Taya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 G3 Djebel Taya

name: Doclea
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 B1 Doclea

name: Dokimeion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Dokimeion

name: Doliche
description: An ancient city of Asia Minor (modern Dülük in Turkey).

name: Dolmen de Kerhenry
description: A Neolithic tomb.

name: Dolmen of Guadalperal
description: A megalithic stone circle dating to the third millennium BCE located in Peraleda de la Mata in eastern Extremadura, Spain. The monument is usually submerged beneath the waters of the Embalse de Valdecañas, a reservoir of the Tagus river.

name: Domus and Episcopal Palace
description: Domus and Episcopal Palace at Aquileia. The second phase of the Episcopal Palace at Aquileia belongs to the fourth and fifth centuries CE.

name: Domus and Mitreo delle Pareti Dipinte
description: The complex known as "Domus and Mitreo delle Pareti Dipinte" began its life in the later second century BCE and was remodeled during the time of Augustus. A mithraeum is built in the northwest part of the building in the later second century CE.

name: Domus Aurea
description: A sprawling palace complex begun by Nero after A.D. 64 that aimed to connect the Esquiline, Caelian, and Palatine hills.

name: Domus di Vigna Guidi
description: A well-appointed domus of the mid-second century CE that was consumed by the construction works to create the Thermae Antoninianae, resulting in its being buried to a depth of some 10 m in construction fill. Heritage officials describe the house as a sort of 'condominium', comparing it to the contemporary insulae of Ostia.

name: Domus Faustae
description: The Domus Faustae refers to a part of the Lateran Palace. In about 312 CE, Constantine razed the barracks of the imperial horse-guards barracks that adjoined the palace. At that time, the palace was known as Domus Faustae. Since the equites singulares Augusti had supported Maxentius, Constantine destroys the barracks and orders the beginnings of the construction of the Basilica di San Giovanni in Laterano on the site.

name: Donuktaş
description: An archaeological site in Tarsus, Turkey, that has been interpreted in various ways and assigned to various periods. The current consensus suggests that the ruined concrete structure is of a sacred nature and dates to the second century CE.

name: Dorset Cursus
description: A Neolithic cursus monument spanning some 10 km of Cranborne Chase in east Dorset, United Kingdom.

name: Doukki Gel
description: An ancient city founded by Egyptian pharaohs ca. 1450 BCE, Doukki Gel succeeds the earlier site of Kerma.

name: Dreros
description: An ancient Greek settlement near modern Neapoli in the Lasithi region of Crete (about 15km northwest of Agios Nikolaos).

name: Drombeg stone circle
description: A megalithic stone circle monument, also known as the Druid's Altar, consisting of seventeen closely spaced stones with a diameter of 9.3 m. Thirteen stones survive today.

name: Drususstein (Drususstone) at Mogontiacum
description: The remains of a Roman monument at Mainz, perhaps a cenotaph erected in 9 B.C.

name: Dugirdkhan
description: Mound where cuneiform inscription has been found, periodically inundated.

name: Dur-(Kuri)galzu
description: Dur-(Kuri)galzu was founded by the Kassite ruler Kurigalzu ca. 1400 B.C. At its peak the site covered some 225 ha.

name: Dur-Aššur-ketti-lešir
description: A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is identified with modern Tell Bderi.

name: Dur-Bel-Harran-belu-uṣur
description: A city founded by the Assyrian palace herald Bēl-Harrān-bēlu-uṣur in the mid-8th century B.C. The discovery of a stele there identifies ancient Dūr-Bēl-Harrān-bēlu-uṣur with modern Tell ʿAbṭa (Tell Abtah).

name: Dur-Katlimmu Archive 3
description: In several rooms of the Red House, located in the central part of the lower town of Dur-Katlimmu, an archive of ca. 150 clay tablets inscribed in Assyrian cuneiform and ca. 50 tablets inscribed in Aramaic alphabetic script, along with many more small fragments, was discovered. The texts found at in the Red House date between 828 and 600 BC, after the fall of the Assyrian Empire. Many of the texts can be connected with a man named Šulmu-šarri, a contemporary of king Ashurbanipal (r. 668-ca. 631 BC). The texts pertaining to him were found in multiple rooms of the house (Rooms YV, CW, IW and XW), although texts were also found in other rooms. Several of the people who appear in the tablets found at the Red House bear military titles: a garrison was located at Dur-Katlimmu The four latest texts, Neo-Babylonian tablets dating from 603 to 600 BC, were found in Room XX. The texts include purchase documents, loans and juridicial documents.

name: Dur-Šarrukin Archive 1
description: Three tiers of niches were found in Room H 5 (on the northwestern side of Forecourt I) of the Nabû temple at Dur-Šarrukin, the short-lived capital of the Assyrian king Sargon II (721–705 BC). Fragments of clay tablets and inscribed prisms in these niches suggest that a temple library was located here. The empty niches might be a result of the move of the capital to Nineveh under Sennacherib (704–681 BC) or of the use of perishable writing materials.

name: Dur-Šarrukin Archive 2
description: Two tiers of niches (similar to Dur-Šarrukin 1) were found in Room H 15 (on the northwestern side of Courtyard II) of the Nabû temple at Dur-Šarrukin, the short-lived capital of the Assyrian king Sargon II (721–705 BC). Although no inscribed objects were found inside the niches, the existence of a temple library is suggested by a similar construction in Room H 5 (on the northwestern side of Forecourt I). The empty niches might be the result of the move of the capital to Nineveh under Sennacherib (704–681 BC) or of the use of perishable writing materials.

name: Dur-Sharrukin
description: Dūr-Šarrukīn (“Fort Sargon”), modern Khorsabad, became the capital of the Assyrian Empire during the reign of the eighth-century-BC ruler Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC). According to the Assyrian Eponym Chronicle, the foundations of the city were laid in 717 BC and the city itself was completed in early 706 BC, one year before Sargon was killed on the battlefield. Sargon’s newly-constructed, rectangular-shaped capital, which he modelled on the general plan on Babylon, was abandoned upon his death in 705 BC; his son and successor Sennacherib (r. 704–681 BC) made Nineveh Assyria’s principal administrative center.

name: Durrington Walls
description: Durrington Walls is an archaeological site located 2 miles (3.2 km) north-east of Stonehenge (parish of Durrington, Amesbury,Wiltshire). A large Neolithic settlement and a later henge enclosure were located at Durrington Walls and used in the later third millennium BCE. This site is considered to be part of the Stonehenge World Heritage Site in England.

name: Ebla
description: An important Bronze Age city and the capital of the three Eblaite Kingdoms.

name: Ecbatana/Hagmatana/Achmeta/Epiphaneia/Ahmadan
description: The capital of Astyages (Istuvegü), captured by Cyrus the Great in 549 BC. The site served as a summer residence for both Persian and Parthian kings.

name: Ecclesiastical complex, Port of Sanitja
description: Archaeological excavations in 2008 and 2010 revealed an ecclesiastical complex at the west side of the port of Sanitja on the Spanish island of Menorca. This complex may date to the sixth century CE.

name: Echinades (islands)
description: A group of islands in the Ionian Sea off the coast of Acarnania.

name: Eetioneia Pr.
description: A fortified promontory located on the western side of the main port of Piraeus.

name: Egnatia/Gnathia
description: Egnatia/Gnathia was an ancient city of the Messapii in southern Italy.

name: Eilsleben
description: Archaeological investigation and geophysical prospection at Eilsleben, Germany, have documented the presence of a notable Early Neolithic settlement dating to the later sixth millennium BCE.

name: Eining
description: A Roman vexillation fort of the Upper Germanic-Rhaetian Limes.

name: El Carambolo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D4 El Carambolo

name: El Chuche (settlement)
description: A Roman-period hilltop site, partially excavated in the 20th century, that lies just west of the modern Spanish village of El Chuche in the municipal district of Benahadux, Almeria. It is probably to be associated with the attested Roman name "Urci". The archaeological record of human habitation on and near the site stretches back to the Chalcolithic.

name: El-‘Iraq
description: At modern El-‘Iraq in Jordan's Karak region, archaeologists have identified Byzantine pottery, architecture (including a church), and inscriptions. They argue, based on the archaeology and on the relative placement of other contemporary settlements on the Madaba map, that this site should be identified as ancient Tharais.

name: el-Lahun
description: el-Lahun was a planned 'pyramid town' that supported a royal mortuary complex and other activities associated with the Pyramid of Senusret II, which was located less than a kilometer west of the settlement.

name: Elaioussa/Sebaste
description: Elaioussa/Sebaste was an ancient Roman city in Asia Minor founded in the second century BCE.

name: Elaphites (islands)
description: An island group off the coast of Illyricum.

name: Elateia
description: Elateia was an ancient Greek city of Phocis, and numbered as the most important place in that region after Delphi. The remains of the ancient site are located to the northeast of the modern town.

name: Elephas herbarius
description: Elephas herbarius (Elefantum herbarium), a place included in the Notitia, is mentioned only once and must lie at the southern limit of Regio VIII. Coarelli concludes that this place must have been located on the lower slopes of the Capitoline Hill facing the Forum Holitorium. Its coincidence with Late Antique and Medieval toponyms suggests that it refers to an ancient statue of an elephant, perhaps one shown eating plants, in the area of the Forum Holitorium.

name: Elina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B3 Elina

name: Elis (city)
description: Elis was an ancient Greek city of the northwest Peloponnese that served as the capital of the district of the same name.

name: Elyros
description: An ancient polis in the mountainous Selino area of western Crete. Its site is located near modern Rodovani.

name: Emar
description: An important Bronze Age trading center in northern Syria. The site is known today as Tell Meskene.

name: Emirzeli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 E3 Emirzeli

name: Emmaus/Nicopolis
description: An ancient settlement of Judea that is mentioned in numerous ancient sources including the Gospel of Luke. Now situated within the modern Canada Park in the Israeli-occupied West Bank, the site was known in modern times as Imwas and was populated until 1967.

name: Emporio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C5 Emporio

name: Ennea Hodoi
description: An ancient Thracian hilltop settlement whose site is located on a hill 2 kilometers north of the later site of Amphipolis.

name: Ephesus/Arsinoe(ia)
description: Ephesus/Arsinoe(ia) was the most important ancient metropolis of Ionian Asia Minor.

name: Ephyra/Kichyros
description: An ancient settlement located on the hill immediately north of modern Mesopotamo in the modern Greek regional unit of Preveza (Epirus).

name: Episkopi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 D5 Episkopi

name: Erebuni
description: Erebuni was a fortified city of the ancient kingdom of Urartu located in what is now Yerevan, Armenia.

name: Erechtheion
description: The Erechtheion is a temple on the north side of the Athenian Acropolis dedicated to Athena Polias and other deities and heroes; the temple is of the Ionic order with several distinctive features, including its famous caryatid porch.

name: Eretria
description: Eretria was an ancient polis located in Euboea.

name: Eridu
description: Eridu was an ancient city of Sumeria and perhaps one of the first true cities. The site is settled from ca. 5300 B.C. onward. Eridu was located approximately 12 km southwest of Ur and was the southernmost of a conglomeration of Sumerian cities that grew up around temple complexes.

name: Eskiyapar
description: Hittite city with fortification.

name: Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo
description: The so-called Etruscan Pyramid of Bomarzo is a megalithic, rock-cut monument at Bomarzo. It likely dates to the late Archaic period.

name: Euesperides
description: Euesperides was the westernmost Greek colony of Cyrenaica. The remains of the site are found on the outskirts of modern Benghazi near the north Libyan coast. Under Ptolemy III Euergetes, the new city of Berenice was built a short distance southwest of Euesperides and the original site was subsequently abandoned.

name: Euhydrion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C2 Euhydrion

name: Eumachia Building
description: Located on the east side of Pompeii's forum (VII, 9, 1), the so-called Eumachia building is a large, public building financed by Eumachia, a public priestess of Venus in Pompeii during the middle of the first century AD.

name: Europos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B3 Europos

name: Europos
description: An ancient city in Macedonia. The known components of the site (acropolis, extensive necropolis) are located immediately south of the modern town.

name: Euthymios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Euthymios

name: Exusta/Katakekaumene (islands)
description: Exusta/Katakekaumene Inss. are a group of islands located in the Red Sea. The ancient names Exusta and Katakekaumene refer to the volcanic nature of these islands, one of which is identifiable as the modern Jabal al-Tair Island.

name: Faesulae
description: Faesulae (modern Fiesole) was an Etruscan foundation called Viesul, Viśl, or Vipsul. The city was conquered by Romans in 283 B.C. Sulla would settle Roman veterans there. Citizens of the town supported the conspiracy of Catiline.

name: Fag el-Gamous Cemetery
description: A large (300 acres), multiperiod (Old Kingdom through late Roman) cemetery located just beyond the arable land of the eastern Fayoum in Egypt. It likely served the nearby ancient settlements of Tanis, Philadelphia, and Selia.

name: Falerii
description: A fortified town located some 6 km from Falerii Veteres and established by the ancient Romans ca. 241 B.C.

name: Faroe Islands
description: An archipelago made up of 18 islands situated between the Norwegian Sea and the North Atlantic Ocean.

name: Ferentium
description: Ferentium (modern Ferento) was a city of Etruria first mentioned in the Liber Coloniarum ca. 123 BC that became a Roman municipium after the Social War. Substantial remains of the city remain; the site was the birthplace of Otho and noted for its fine stone monuments and statues.

name: Ferme d'Ithe
description: In the modern Jouars-Pontchartrain commune of the Yvelines department in France, a ruined medieval farmstead (the Cistercian Ferme d'Ithe, established in the 12th century and occupied until the 20th) sits atop part of the site of a Gallo-Roman vicus identified by crop marks and rescue excavations in the 20th century. A "Divodurum" mentioned in the Antonine Itinerary may refer to this settlement.

name: Ficana
description: An ancient city of Latium that was said to have been conquered by Ancus Marcius, Ficana was located near the eleventh mile of the Via Ostiensis. Excavation at the site has produced important evidence for the urban center beginning from the eighth century BC. Pliny the Elder mentions Ficana as one of the lost cities of Latium.

name: Fifth century baths at Lebadeia
description: Foundations of a bath facility with mosaic pavement and other structures were discovered during rescue excavations in 2006 at the corner of Daidalou and Kadmou streets in modern Levadeia, Greece.

name: Filitosa
description: Filitosa is a megalithic site in southern Corsica that was in use from the Neolithic period until Roman times. A number of menhirs have been discovered at the site.

name: Fines Africae et Cyrenensium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 D2 Fines Africae et Cyrenensium

name: Fioccaglia di Flumeri
description: Fioccaglia di Flumeri was the site of a Gracchan colony. Some scholars identify the archaeological site at Fioccaglia di Flumeri with the ancient Forum Aemilii.

name: Flaminio Obelisk (Rome)
description: The so-called "Flaminio Obelisk" at Rome was created in Egypt during the Nineteenth Dynasty under Pharaoh Seti I. The obelisk was erected at Heliopolis under Ramesses II. In 1 BCE, the obelisk was transported to Rome on the orders of Augustus. It was transported with the Obelisk of Montecitorio. The Flaminio Obelisk was re-erected on the spina of the Circus Maximus. It was discovered in a fragmentary state in 1587 along with the so-called Lateran Obelisk. Following the orders of Pope Sixtus V, Domenico Fontana restored the obelisk and erected it as the centerpiece of the Piazza del Popolo in 1589. It is supported by a travertine podium that was constituted from fragments of the Severan Septizodium on the flank of the Palatine Hill, a monument that had been demolished on papal orders.

name: Flavia Solva
description: Flavia Solva was a Celtic-Roman settlement located on the banks of the Mur river in Noricum.

name: Floga Sculpture Workshop (Paros)
description: Located at modern Floga in the town of Paros, this residential structure, dating to the 5th/4th centuries BCE, underwent a number of subsequent modifications and took on the additional role of a sculpture workshop in the Hellenistic period.

name: Forau de la Tuta
description: A previously unknown settlement with Roman-period remains was identified near the village of Artieda, Spain.

name: Fortified Port Settlement on Cape St. Athanasius
description: An ancient settlement with fortifications and port facilities located on the modern Cape St. Athanasius near Byala in Bulgaria.

name: Fortunatae Insulae (Canary Islands)
description: The "Blessed Islands", equivalent in most ancient literature to the modern Canary Islands (Islas Canarias).

name: Fortunatae Insulae? (Madeira Islands)
description: The modern, Portugeuse archipelago of Madeira seems to have been identified by some ancient authors with the "Fortunatae Insulae" or "Blessed Islands".

name: Forum antique de Bavay
description: The Gallo-Roman forum of ancient Bagacum (Bavay) covers more than 2.5 ha.

name: Forum granary
description: Roman granaries of the forum in Pompeii. (VII,7,29).

name: Forum Holitorium
description: The Forum Holitorium was the vegetable market of ancient Rome, situated outside the Porta Carmentalis in the southern Campus Martius. This area was bounded by the Forum Boarium and the Circus Flaminius.

name: Forum Romanum
description: The ancient Roman forum.

name: Fufluna/Populonium
description: Populonia likely derives its name from the Etruscan god 'Fufluns' and was an important maritime and commercial center of Etruria located in the Colline Metallifere.

name: Gabia La Grande
description: Gabia La Grande (Granada, Spain) is the findspot of a subterranean nymphaeum approached by a dromos that may have dated to the fourth century CE.

name: Gali Zardak
description: A group of badly preserved rock-cut sculptures located along a watercourse of the same name that drains the northern face of Jebel Maqlub.

name: Gallo-Roman Settlement at Mamer
description: A settlement of the Treveri (ancient name unknown) at modern Mamer in Luxembourg.

name: Ganj Nameh
description: Ganj Nameh (or Gandj Nameh) is a site located approximately 12 km southwest of Ecbatana. Two trilingual Achaemenid royal inscriptions are to be found here. One of these was ordered by Darius the Great (r.  522–486 BCE) and the other by his son, Xerxes the Great (r.  486–465 BCE).

name: Garden Houses
description: A large residential complex, built in ca. 128 CE as part of the Hadrianic remodeling of Ostia. Its central area was occupied by two blocks, each comprising four houses, surrounded by a garden furnished with six fountains.

name: Garden of Cybele
description: An archaeological area in Vienne that includes remains of municipal and domestic architecture.

name: Garni temple
description: The Garni Temple dates to the first century A.D. and was sacred to the Armenian sun god Mihr.

name: Garšana
description: A Babylonian town mentioned in a collection of 1500 unprovenienced tablets. It is possibly to be identified with the archaeological site of Tall Baridiyah.

name: Gasr Banat
description: Gasr Banat (also Qasr Banat or Qasr Isawi) was the site of a Roman-period fortified farmstead referred to as a "centenarium".

name: Gasr Doga
description: The mausoleum of Gasr Doga is a monumental, elite Libico-Punic tomb built in the Tarhuna area.

name: Gate of Athena Archegetis
description: The Gate of Athena Archegetis is the Doric-order gateway at the west of the Roman Agora in Athens. It was dedicated to Athena Archigetis in 11/10 BCE.

name: Gavurkale
description: Gavurkale is a hill in central Anatolia with remains of both Hittite rock-cut reliefs and architecture dating from various periods.

name: Gazara
description: Gazara is a fortified ancient city dating to the early second millennium BCE.

name: Gentinos?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C2 Gentinos?

name: Gerasa/Antiochia ad Chrysorhoam
description: Gerasa (modern Jerash in Jordan) became a substantial center under Antiochos IV Epiphanes, at which time it became known as Antioch on the Chrysorhoas. It became part of the territory of Iudaea and in 63 B.C. Pompeius Magnus made it part of the Decapolis. It received the status of a Roman colony in the third century.

name: Geridu
description: Geridu, located near the comune of Sorso in Sardinia, was the site of a Medieval village active from the twelfth to fourteenth centuries.

name: Germanikeia
description: An ancient city located in the northern Syrian region of Commagene mentioned. Modern Maraş.

name: Geziret el Marakeb
description: A long and confused tradition of the archaeology of Geziret el Marakeb or "Seal Island" is reviewed by Carter who debunks many of the earlier claims. The island is the site of curious stone enclosures as well as evidence for Late Roman activity.

name: Ghar El Kebir settlement
description: Settlement associated with Punic and Roman quarries known today as Ghar El Kebir (previously and perhaps in error: Rhar-el-Kebir). The complex is perhaps to be associated with the ancient name Latomiai.

name: Gheriat el-Garbia
description: Gheriat el-Garbia was the site of a Roman fort in Libya that was part of the Limes Tripolitanus.

name: Ghiaccio Forte
description: Ghiaccio Forte was an Etruscan settlement, most likely founded in the fourth century B.C., that was sacked by the Romans ca. 280 B.C.

name: Ghirza
description: Ghirza is the site of a Roman town in Libya.

name: Gholaia
description: Gholaia (modern Bu Njem in Libya) was the site of a settlement and Roman fort along the Limes Tripolitanus.

name: Ghuzza
description: A multi-period complex in Egypt's eastern desert comprising a fort, settlement(s?), and production areas for gold-washing of quartz collected from the wadi alluvium. Most visible structures date to the Ptolemaic and Roman periods, but there is evidence for earlier activity during the New Kingdom period.

name: Gianola
description: The site of a villa associated with Lucius Vitruvius Mamurra.

name: Gigthis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C1 Gigthis

name: Gird-i Gulak
description: A high mound located on the edge of Lake Dukan and now threatened by innundation.

name: Gird-i Rostam
description: Tall multiperiod settlement mound in Kurdistan Iraq. Preliminary excavations have yielded evidence for occupation and construction ranging from the Chalcolithic to the Iron Age.

name: Giricano
description: Modern Giricano, site of the ancient Dunnu-ša-Uzībiis, is located in the Upper Tigris region of south-eastern Turkey, in the province of Diyarbakir, about 11 km southeast of modern Bismil, ca. 5 km northwest of Ziyaret Tepe, on the north bank of the Tigris River.

name: Girumu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 F5 Girumu

name: Giza
description: The site of Giza encompasses the Giza Necropolis, a large archaeological site famous for its pyramid complexes and monumental sculpture.

name: Gladiatorial School
description: The gladiatorial school of Carnuntum was located to the west of the amphitheater.

name: Gladiators' barracks
description: A training ground for gladiators in Pompeii noted for the more than 100 inscriptions connected to gladiating. (V, 5, 3)

name: Gleieh
description: Gleieh (also Gleiʾeh, Glaiʿā) is a small site in the middle Euphrates region and was part of the kingdom of Suhu. The city, which is probably to be identified with ancient Kār-Apladad, was situated on the south bank of the Euphrates, opposite Sur Jureh (which lies on the northern bank). Like many towns and cities of the ancient kingdom of Suhu, Gleieh/Kār-Apladad is now underwater; the area was flooded after the construction of the Haditha dam.

name: Göbekli Tepe
description: The site of Göbekli Tepe, located in the Germuş mountains of south-eastern Anatolia, was the location of a Neolithic hilltop sanctuary. This sanctuary, thought to be a series of stone enclosures with T-shaped pillars carrying carving of wild animals, was built in the Pre-Pottery Neolithic and ranges in date from 9,600 to 8,200 B.C.

name: Gobustan Rock Art Cultural Landscape
