description: The Gobustan Rock Art Cultural Landscape is a UNESCO World Heritage Site located in central Azerbaijan. The 537 ha site comprises more than 6,000 rock engravings covering a period of 40,000 years.

name: Godin tepe
description: Godin tepe is an archaeological site located in western Iran.

name: Gonur Tepe
description: A city in the Murghab River delta inhabited in the third-second millennia BCE. Gonur Tepe is located ca. 60 km north of Mary, Turkmenistan.

name: Goritsa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D2 Goritsa

name: Goritsa Neromyloi site
description: A hill to the southeast of Pella with fortifications and settlement remains from the late Classical and later periods.

name: Gorkhatri
description: A complex in Peshawar, constructed as a caravanserai in the 16th century CE. The site itself seems to go back much earlier (to perhaps the 3rd century CE) and may be mentioned in early travelers' accounts.

name: Gors Fawr stone circle
description: Gors Fawr Stone Circle is located in Mynachlog-Ddu, Pembrokeshire, Wales, United Kingdom.

name: Gorsium/Herculia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 F2 Gorsium/Herculia

name: Gourimadi Prehistoric Settlement
description: Survey and site excavation have provided evidence for a likely prehistoric settlement (possibly fortified) on the Gourimadi hill in the Katsaronio plain in the Karystia, southern Euboea, Greece.

name: Gournia
description: A Minoan palace complex and settlement on Crete, finally abandoned ca. 1200 BCE. The original toponym is unknown, and the modern name is derived from stone vessels found at the site.

name: Grand Lagneux Roman Bath
description: At Grand Lagneux, a small village in the French commune of Yenne, a rescue excavation in 2020 revealed part of a Roman-era bathing establishment. The excavated site is now buried under a modern home. This find constitutes another possible locality in the commune for attested ancient name "Etanna".

name: Grange stone circle
description: Grange stone circle or Lios na Grainsi is the largest stone circle in Ireland, consisting of 113 stones and was built ca. 2100 BC.

name: Graviscae
description: The port of the Etruscan city of Tarquinia.

name: Gre Fılla
description: Gre Fılla is a Pre-Pottery Neolithic B (PPNB) period site located in Turkey's Diyarbakır Province. The site has been compared to Göbekli Tepe in some respects. Gre Fılla has produced evidence of very early copper working.

name: Great Colonnade at Palmyra
description: The primary colonnaded avenue of the ancient city of Palmyra was constructed during the second and third centuries CE. It stretched for more than one kilometer (approximately 0.75 miles).

name: Great Palace of Constantinople
description: Palace complex constructed by Constantine I in 330 CE that served as the primary residence of the Eastern Roman/Byzantine Emperors until 1081 CE. Use of the palace declined beginning in the 11th century, and it had been abandoned by the time of the Ottoman conquest of Constantinople in 1453. Most of the structure was demolished in the early Ottoman period. The Sultan Ahmed Mosque was built on the primary site of the palace in the early 17th century.

name: Gremoulias ancient sanctuary
description: A sanctuary -- with remains of at least two successive temples (6th C BCE and 4th C BCE), a great altar, and votive deposits -- located on the modern Gremoulias saddle, 3.5km SE of Kalavryta in the northern Peloponnese. The site is not known from ancient literature. Scholars speculate that the deity worshiped may have been Artemis and the site may have been an extra-urban sanctuary of nearby Kynaitha.

name: Greolieres Roman Milestone
description: A Roman milestone located in France, in the town of Gréolières in the Alpes-Maritimes department in the Provence-Alpes-Côte d'Azur region. The inscription on the milestone honors the Roman emperor Caracalla and is dated to 213.

name: Grimspound
description: A prehistoric settlement of the late Bronze Age located in the Dartmoor area of south Devon, England. The remains of 24 stone houses survive within a massive boundary wall.

name: Grotta Paglicci
description: The Paglicci cave or Grotta Paglicci is located near Rignano Garganico, Apulia, in southern Italy. Discovered in the 1950s, the cave is an important archaeological site documenting life in the Paleolithic period. In addition to artifact finds and zooarchaeological information, the cave provides evidence of the harvesting of wild oats ca. 36,000 BCE. The cave also includes Paleolithic mural art.

name: Grotte dell'Acqua
description: Architectural remains near Lago Fusaro that may have been associated with a so-called "villa maritima".

name: Grotte di Seiano a Pietramelara
description: A ruined, terraced megalithic complex covering an area of some 900 square meters. The complex likely dates to the second century B.C. and was destroyed in the time of Sulla. The complex is interpreted as a Samnite sanctuary.

name: Grumentum
description: Grumentum was an ancient settlement of Lucania, likely inhabited since the sixth century B.C. The site played a role in the Second Punic War and later became a bishopric in the fourth century A.D.

name: Grutenhäuschen
description: The Grutenhäuschen is a Roman funereal temple of the third or fourth century A.D. It has been reconstructed on site.

name: Güglingen Mithraeum
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mithras located near modern Heilbronn, Germany, in use during the second-third centuries CE.

name: Guild Temple and Mithraeum of Fructosus
description: An architectural complex excavated in 1938 at the intersection of Via del Pomerio and Via del Tempio Rotondo in Ostia. The guild temple dates to the reign of Alexander Severus (222 - 235 CE). Based on epigraphic evidence, this shrine may belong to the guild of the stuppatores. A mithraic shrine with a patron named Fructosus is to be found in the substructure of the podium.

name: Guyum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Guyum

name: Guzana (Tell Halaf)
description: An Aramaean principality and Neo-Assyrian provincial capital founded on the site of an extinct Neolithic settlement. Settled through Hellenistic times, it was abandoned at the end of the first century BCE.

name: Guzana Archive 1
description: Approximately 85 clay tablets were discovered in the eastern room of a house adjoining the tarrace of the northeastern palace of Guzana. The texts were found in a secondary context, probably as fill, and belonged to Mannu-ki-Aššur, Assyrian governor in Guzana during the first quarter of the 8th century BC. The archive consists of letters to and from the governor, as well as administrative lists concerning persons, animals, and objects.

name: Guzana Archive 2
description: Inside a clay pot, found in a private house east of the citadel gate of Guzana, excavators discovered the private archive of a man with an Aramaic name, Il-manani, dating to the last years of the Assyrian Empire. The archive consists of five Aramaic dockets concerning loans of barley, previously attached to perishable material, and five clay tablets written in Assyrian cuneiform, specifically three loan documents, one purchase document concerning a slave, and one legal settlement.

name: Güzelhisar
description: Mosque in the village of Güzelhisar/Avnik where a Urartian inscription was found in 1965.

name: Gymnasium at Akragas
description: An architectural complex in the upper agora of Akragas (modern Agrigento) dedicated to Hermes and Heracles that was probably used as gymnasium. It appears to have been built in Hellenistic time and renovated in the Augustan period.

name: Hadid/Ad(d)ida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 F2 Hadid/Ad(d)ida

name: Hadrianopolis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B2 Hadrianopolis

name: Haghartsin
description: Armenian monastery complex constructed between the 9th-13th centuries CE.

name: Hala Sultan Tekke
description: A major Late Bronze Age settlement occupied the site of Hala Sultan Tekke, which later became an important Sufi Islamic shrine associated with Umm Haram.

name: Halaesa
description: Halaesa is located on the north coast of Sicily between between Kalakta and Kephaloidion. The city was founded in 403 B.C. by Archonides of Herbita. The city sided with Rome during the First Punic War and was made "libera et immunis". By the Augustan period it had become a municipium.

name: Halicarnassus/Halikarnassos
description: An ancient city in Caria, now known as Bodrum in Turkey.

name: Haltern
description: A sequence of occupational phases at Haltern, Germany, results from the presence of the Roman military following from the earliest period of Roman occupation in the principate of Augustus.

name: Hamadab
description: Hamadab is an important archaeological site of the middle Nile River valley. It is the site of a ruined Kushite city located in what is now Sudan. The settlement was established by the third century BCE and remained active until the fourth century CE.

name: Hamamkarahisar Höyüğü
description: An Early Bronze Age settlement located in Central Anatolia to the southwest of Hamam Karahisar Village.

name: Ḥammām
description: Graves near the village Ḥammām (now inundated), find place for inscribed cylinder seal.

name: Haradu
description: Haradu (the modern Khirbit ed-Diniye, Iraq) was a small settlement southeast of Mari during the First Dynasty of Babylon.

name: Harmozike
description: Harmozike was an ancient site in Georgia and the dwelling of the royalty of Caucasian Iberia. The site is located on the Bagineti hill in modern Mccheta. Pompeius Magnus captured the site in 65 BC.

name: Harran/Carrhae
description: A major settlement of Upper Mesopotamia from the Bronze Age onward. The earliest mentions of the site are found in the Ebla tablets (ca. 2300 B.C.). Notably the location of the Battle of Carrhae between the Roman Republic and Parthian Empire in 53 B.C., which proved to be a devastating defeat for the Romans. Modern-day Ḥarrān, Turkey.

name: Hattusa
description: The Late Bronze Age capital of the Hittite empire (near modern Boğazkale in Turkey). The site was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.

name: Hayling Island Saxon Settlement
description: Between ca. 570-900 CE (radio-carbon dates), a Saxon-era settlement and possible ritual site flourished on and around the location of an earlier Iron-Age/Roman sanctuary in the northern part of modern Hayling Island (borough of Havant, Hampshire, England).

name: Hazine Piri Kapısı
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Hebron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Hebron

name: Heidentor
description: A triumphal monument built at Carnuntum between A.D. 354 and 361, perhaps by Constantius II.

name: Hekatonnesoi (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 D3 Hekatonnesoi Inss.

name: Helike
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 C1 Helike

name: Heliopolis
description: Ancient Heliopolis was the capital of the 13th or Heliopolite Nome of Lower Egypt and served as a major religious center. The site is now located in Ayn Shams, a suburb of Cairo.

name: Heliopolis/Col. Iulia Augusta Felix
description: An ancient settlement in Lebanon's Beqaa Valley, named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site because "with its colossal structures, [it] is one of the finest examples of Imperial Roman architecture at its apogee."

name: Hellenistic Arsenal
description: The Hellenistic Arsenal is the modern name for a rectangular building on the Kolonos Agoraios in Athens, built in the 270s or 260s BC and destroyed during the Sullan Sack of Athens in 86 BC. It appears to have been modelled on the Naval Arsenal at Piraeus and was probably used for the storage of weapons and armour.

name: Hellenistic Funerary Monument (Keryneia)
description: Also frequently referred to as "the Heroon" in archaeological literature, this structure at the site of Keryneia in Achaea was excavated in the mid-20th century.

name: Henchir Hnich
description: Discovered in 2013, this site in the modern Oued Ellouz sector of Tunisia lies 3.5km west of ancient Musti (modern Henchir Mest). Excavators interpret the site as a rural settlement of an imperial estate. The only known copy of an inscribed "lex de agris rudibus" of the emperor Hadrian was discovered here (unpublished as of January 2022).

name: Henchir-el-Oust
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 Henchir-el-Oust

name: Heraion
description: The sanctuary of Hera on Samos.

name: Heraion (Teichos)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 B2 Heraion (Teichos)

name: Herakleion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 C2 Herakleion

name: Herakleopolis
description: Herakleopolis (also called Herakleopolis Magna) was the capital of the Twentieth nome of Egypt.

name: Herakleopolis Mikra/Sethroe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 G2 Herakleopolis Mikra/Sethroe

name: Heraklion
description: Heraklion was the harbor city of ancient Knossos, and was later re-founded by the Arab conquerors.

name: Herculaneum
description: An ancient Roman town of Campania destroyed by the eruption of Mt. Vesuvius in A.D. 79. Named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997.

name: Herculis (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A1 Herculis Inss.

name: Hermes and Aphrodite Sanctuary
description: This large, open-air sanctuary near Syme Viannou in southern Crete was in use from 2000 BCE until at least the sixth century CE. It attracted pilgrims who are assumed to have camped at the site, which is far from any contemporary settlement. Monumental construction began in the early second millennium BCE and multiple phases and changes are evident, including the construction of Christian chapels in late antiquity. Although the identities of the deities worshiped here at the earliest periods are uncertain (as are the saints venerated in the Christian period), the association with Hermes and Aphrodite seems to have begun as early as the 10th century BCE.

name: Hermeskeil Roman Camp
description: A late-Republican Roman army camp, located on the eastern outskirts of modern Hermeskeil in the German county of Trier-Saarburg (Rheinland-Pfalz). The site is not visible today.

name: Hermopolis Magna/Schmun
description: The capital city of the Hermopolite nome (the fifteenth Nome of Upper Egypt). The ancient Egyptians called the site Khmun after the group of eight divinities (Ogdoad) that represented the primordial world.

name: Herodeion
description: Herodeion or Herodium refers to an archaeological site located in what is now the West Bank, some 12 km south of Jerusalem and 5 km southeast of Bethlehem. In antiquity, Herod the Great established the fortress after defeating his rival Antigonus II Mattathias in 40 BCE.

name: Heroonpolis
description: Heroonpolis, located at Tell el-Maskhuta, was an ancient Egyptian city of the Nile Delta.

name: Hibis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Hibis

name: Himera
description: A colony on the north shore of Sicily founded in 649/8 BC by the Myletidai.

name: Hippo Regius
description: Hippo Regius (modern Annaba in Algeria) was a colony established by Phoenicians from Tyre as early as the twelfth century BC.

name: Hippoi (islands)
description: Hippoi Inss., an island group off the coast of Ionia near the port of Erythrai.

name: Hipposthenis Monu.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hipposthenis Monu.

name: Hitan Rayan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 F2 Hitan Rayan (1)

name: Honorific arch
description: An honorific arch built in Pompeii near the Temple of Jupiter.

name: Horologium Augusti
description: The Horologium Augusti or Solarium Augusti was an ancient monument of the Campus Martius built during the reign of Augustus. In theory, it functioned as a giant solar marker. An obelisk relocated to Rome from Heliopolis served as the gnomon. The obelisk today stands in the Piazza di Monte Citorio.

name: Horrea Agrippiana
description: A set of warehouses in Regio VIII.

name: Horrea Galbana
description: Horrea (warehouses) located between the southern part of the Aventine Hill and Monte Testaccio.

name: Horrea Piperataria
description: A portion of a warehouse complex dating to the Flavian period, the Horrea Piperataria, was likely destroyed in the fire of 192 CE. Its remains were later incorporated into the substructions of the Basilica Nova (a.k.a. Basilica of Maxentius).

name: Horrea Vespasiani
description: A warehouse complex on the Palatine slope adjacent to the Sacra Via.

name: Horta
description: The Etruscan settlement of *Hurta (the modern Orte) was close to the Vadimonis Lacus where the Romans fought, and won, two engagements against Etruscan armies.

name: Ḥorvat Tevet
description: A late Iron Age IIa (ca. ninth century BCE) site located in the north-eastern part of the Jezreel Valley, Ḥorvat Tevet has produced archaeological evidence of a large, pillared building that has been interpreted as an administrative site belonging to early monarchic Israel.

name: Hospitium of Hermes
description: A Roman hospitium located near the Porta Stabia in Pompeii (I, 1, 8).

name: Hot Baths
description: Remains of a large building and baths uncovered between Bilbury Lane and Lower Borough Walls, beneath what is now known as the Gainsborough Spa in Aquae Sulis, modern-day Bath.

name: House of Julia Felix
description: The so-called House of Iulia Felix (II, 4, 3) is situated on the south side of the Via dell'Abbondanza, near the Palaestra and the Amphitheater. The estate was formed from two complete insulae that were merged. About one-third of the area was occupied by the house, the remainder by a large garden. Following the AD 62 earthquake the proprietress seems to have leased out portions of the property.

name: House of the Chaste Lovers
description: The so-called House of the Chaste Lovers at Pompeii is located on the north side of the Via dell'Abbondanza. The site has been the subject of ongoing archaeological excavation since 1987. It is located in insula 12 of Regio IX. It adjoins the so-called House of the Painters at Work which lies to the north.

name: House of the Tiles
description: The so-called "House of the Tiles" at Lerna belongs to the Korakou culture in the Early Helladic II period (ca. 2500–2300 BCE). It was a monumental two-story building (12 x 25 meters) and has been interpreted as either a "proto-palace" or administrative center.

name: Hung-i Kamalvand
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Kamalvand

name: Hung-i Nauruzi/Hung-i Azdar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Nauruzi/Hung-i Azdar

name: Hung-i Yar-i 'Alivand
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 F2 Hung-i Yar-i 'Alivand

name: Ḥūr Sulaymān
description: A Bronze Age site, comprised of a low settlement mound, in Lorestan where a cuneiform brick inscription has been found.

name: Huzirina
description: The ancient Huzirina, modern Sultantepe, is a Late Neo-Assyrian archeological site, now in Şanlıurfa Province, Turkey.

name: Huzirina Archive 1
description: In Huzirina, about 400 clay tablets were discovered in a secondary position outside a private house with a central courtyard in area F. Likely, this library was previously located in the private house since a similar type of clay tablet was also found in the central courtyard. The library dates from 718 BC to the end of the Assyrian Empire and belonged to a family of šangû-priests: Qurdi-Nergal, his son Mušallim-Baba and others. The library consists of incantations, incantation rituals, medical texts, prayers, hymns and lexical lists, including god-lists.

name: Hypereia (spring)
description: Strabo locates a Hypereia krēnē, mentioned in the Iliad's "Catalog of Ships", at Pherai in Thessaly (modern Velestino) and geographically criticizes its assignment by Homer to the contingent led by Eurypylos. Without comment, Pliny places "fons Hyperia" immediately before "oppida Pherae" in a list of places in Thessaly (E. Kirsten in RE accepts this as a reference to the spring at Pherai, but BAtlDir lists it as an "unlocated spring in Thessaly"). Archaeological work confirms a perennial spring at Velestino was a locus of settlement activity from the Neolithic period onwards, and a structure monumentalizing the spring was built and modified during the Classical and Hellenistic periods.

name: Hyphaloi (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B3 Hyphaloi Inss.

name: Hypsele
description: A fortified settlement of the geometric period, located at modern Ypsili on the island of Andros. Its ancient name is not known.

name: Ianus Augustus
description: The remains of a Roman arch may mark the boundary between the Roman provinces of Hispania Baetica and Hispania Tarraconensis.

name: Ianus Quadrifons
description: A quadrifrons triumphal arch at Rome that is located at a crossroads in the area of the Velabrum and the Forum Boarium. It dates to the early fourth century AD; its construction employed spolia, and it likely honored either Constantine I or Constantius II.

name: Iconium/Claudiconium
description: Iconium/Claudiconium (modern Konya) was an important city in Lycaonia that later became part of the Roman province of Galatia.

name: Idebessos
description: Idebessos was an ancient city of Lycia.

name: Idu
description: Idu or Idum, Iron age town by Lower Zab.

name: Ierabriga
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A2 Ierabriga

name: Ierusalem/Hierosolyma/Col. Aelia Capitolina
description: The city of Jerusalem.

name: Igel
description: The village of Igel is known for its funerary monument of the Secundinii dating ca. A.D. 250.

name: Iklaina
description: An important Late Bronze Age administrative center located in the territory of Pylos in Messenia.

name: Il Barco
description: The so-called "Barco Borghese" is an archaeological site located in Monte Porzio Catone (Roma) in Italy. This multiperiod site began as a Roman terracing or substructure, perhaps originally meant to support a villa (basis villae). Subsequent modification and reuse converted the Roman structure into a "barco", an enclosure for animal hunts.

name: Ilici/Helike
description: The site of Ilici began as a pre-Roman settlement that eventually developed into a Roman municipium. The site continued into the Visigothic and Byzantine periods as a minor urban settlement, controlling a vast territory which reached old Lucentum. The settlement was relocated to the modern site of Elche in the Islamic period.

name: Imgur-Enlil Archive 1
description: The Mamu temple of Imgur-Enlil was located on the northeastern side of the citadel. In Room 8, behind the secondary cella, an archive of 40 clay tablets, belonging to Šumma-ilu and Mamu-iqbi, was discovered. While most texts date to the first half of the 7th century, three texts are older – dating from 734 to 710 BC. The archive consists of debt-notes, leases, one purchase agreement and other legal documents.

name: Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti Definitio Silvarum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti Definitio Silvarum

name: İnandıktepe
description: An important cult center of the Old Hittite period.

name: Incoronata
description: A protohistoric site located near Metaponto in Italy, Incorata provides important evidence -- especially between the 8th and 6th centuries BCE -- for both indigenous Oenotrian and early Greek colonial physical culture in Italy.

name: Industria/Bodincomagum?
description: Industria/Bodincomagum? (Monteu da Po) was a Ligurian settlement in the Po valley. Under Roman influence the site became known as Industria, a colonial establishment, in regio IX of the Augustan organization of Italy. The colony is likely founded ca. 124 B.C.

name: Insula M1 (Ephesus)
description: A richly appointed insula in Ephesus includes a latrine, which has previously been referred to in scholarship as a brothel.

name: Insula Volusiana
description: The Insula Volusiana in the Forum Boarium is an archaeological site with evidence for multiphase occupation, including craftworking evidence from the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Investiture relief of Ardashir I
description: A rock-cut relief depicting the investiture of Ardashir I, ca. A.D. 226-242. This relief is the earliest Sasanian monument at Naqš-e Rustam.

name: Iomnium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 30 H3 Iomnium

name: Iotapata
description: A Hellenistic village that later became a key site during the Great Jewish Revolt.

name: Ipogeo dei Cristallini
description: A hypogeum tomb of the Hellenistic period in Neapolis (now Naples, Italy).

name: Iri-sagrig
description: A city of southern Mesopotamia attested from the Fara period (Early Dynastic IIIa) through the Old Babylonian period, located on the Tigris river near Nippur, about 60-80km north of Umma. The site has been heavily looted.

name: Is Calas
description: A nuragic complex located near Monte Nai, Sardinia.

name: Is Calas menhir
description: A menhir located near the Is Calas nuragic complex near Monte Nai, Sardinia.

name: Ischalis?
description: Ischalis? is mentioned by Ptolemy, but its precise location remains debated. One possible location is the 'Charterhouse Roman town' site, while another is at Gatcombe, Somerset.

name: Ishān Abū Kumbārah
description: An archaeological site and the findspot for an inscribed cylinder seal.

name: Ishān al-Khāzna
description: Group of mounds where cuneiform inscriptions have been found.

name: Ishān Dhahak
description: Find place for inscribed bricks.

name: Ishān Dulaiyhim
description: Mound, sometimes associated with the ancient city Tummal, which is located in the modern Afak subdistrict of Iraq's Al-Qādisiyyah governorate.

name: Ishān Hāfudh
description: Small mound where a stamped brick is said to have been found.

name: Ishān Khālifa
description: Group of mounds where cuneiform inscriptions have been found.

name: Ishān Miziyād
description: Large mound north of Kish, Akkadian settlement and find place for cuneiform inscriptions.

name: Ishkaft Salmān
description: Location for Elamite rock-cut reliefs.

name: Isin
description: Isin (modern Ishan al-Bahriyat), a city located 107 km southeast of Babylon and 27 km south of Nippur, was an important cult center of the healing goddess Gula (who was also called Ninisinna, the “Lady of Isin”). Its principal religious structure was called Egalmah, which was rebuilt during the long reign of the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC). The Sumerian ceremonial names of a few other temples in this city are known. Although Isin was founded as late as the Early Dynastic Period, possibly already in the Ubaid period, and its main cult remained active until at least the end of the Neo-Babylonian Empire (625–539 BC), the city held brief authority over southern Mesopotamia only twice in its long history. The First Dynasty of Isin, under its fifteen kings, was the dominant power from the collapse of the Ur III state until Isin’s chief rival Larsa became the most dominate city-state (ca. 2017–1924 BC) The Second Dynasty of Isin, under its eleven kings, held authority over Babylonia from ca. 1157 BC to 1026 BC, when it was supplanted by the Second Dynasty of the Sealand.

name: Isinda (Lycian)
description: Ruins of the walled acropolis of ancient Isinda in Lycia are variously said by modern scholars to be located either "above" or 2-3km south of the modern Turkish village of Belenli, which is in Antalya province. BAtlas and OpenStreetMap generally agree (within their accuracy and precision limits) with the location further south.

name: Isinda (Pisidian)
description: An ancient settlement of Pisidia, located at modern Korkuteli in Turkey's Antalya province. The acropolis hill was heavily damaged by the construction of the modern dam in the 20th century. The majority of the ancient settlement probably lay to the east of the acropolis and is now covered by the neighborhood of Alaaddin (Alaeddin Mahalessi).

name: Ision
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 D5 Ision

name: Iskandar Tepa
description: A settlement site in modern Uzbekistan's Pamir-Alay mountain range dating probably between the second century BCE and the first century CE.

name: Isole della Marmorata
description: The Isole della Marmorata off the coast of Sardinia provided the grey granite (also known as granito della Sardegna) that was exported to Rome during the Imperial period.

name: Issus/Nikopolis
description: An ancient port city of Cilicia at the apex of the gulf that bore its name (Issicus Sinus).

name: Itanos
description: An ancient coastal settlement of east Crete. The site is located north of the palm forest at Vai and has provided evidence for occupation from the archaic period through late antiquity.

name: Iulia Concordia
description: Iulia Concordia (Concordia Sagittaria) was established by the Romans in 42 BC at the intersection of the Via Annia and the Via Postumia.

name: Iuvanum
description: A Roman municipium founded in 87 BC after the Social War, Iuvanum sits on the site of a pre-existing Samnite settlement that occupies an upland plateau.

name: Iyyon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Iyyon

name: Īzah
description: Central mound of the modern town of Īzah where artifacts of Elamite date were found in excavations 1976.

name: Jabal ‘Ārūdah
description: Uruk period settlement located on the bank of the Euphrates river.

name: Jamjamāl
description: Mound on which surface cuneiform inscription has been found.

name: Jawānqal‘h
description: Site for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Jebel Moraba
description: The Roman-period name of Jebel Moraba is not known.

name: Jebel Moya
description: Jebel Moya is an archaeological site located in the southern part of the Gezira Plain in Sudan. The 25-acre site was the locus of the largest-known pastoralist necropolis in Africa, with more than 3,000 burials dating between 5000 BCE and 500 CE. Sir Henry Wellcome first excavated the site from 1911 to 1914.

name: Jebel Oust
description: At Jebel Oust [Jbel al Wost] (Tunisia) are the remains of a large Roman bathing complex with a number of built cisterns.

name: Jebel Shaqadud
description: Jebel Shaqadud (Sudan) is an archaeological site located 115 km northeast of Khartoum. It preserves at least 7 meters of in situ stratified deposits that date from ca. 8,200 to 4,000 cal BP.

name: Jericho
description: An ancient site in the Jordan valley with occupation beginning ca. 11,000 years BP. The tell of Hierichous (Jericho) has more than 20 settlement horizons, ranging from Natufian Hunter-Gatherers to modern.

name: Jmr-t
description: An ancient settlement, centered on the temple to Amun Nakht, located at modern Ain Birbiyeh/Birbibya, in Egypt's Dakhleh Oasis.

name: Judeideh
description: Judeideh (also Jedīdē, Judaide) is a site in the middle Euphrates region in what was part of the ancient kingdom of Suhu. The city was situated on the east bank of the Euphrates, about ten kilometers downstream of Anat (Anatho). Like many towns and cities of the ancient kingdom of Suhu, Judeideh is now underwater; the area was flooded after the construction of the Haditha dam. Its ancient name is not known.

name: Jumeirah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 3 G4 Jumeirah

name: Ka'ba-ye Zartosht
description: A stone monument located in the Naqsh-e Rustam compound.

name: Kabeirion
description: A sanctuary near Thebes where the mystery cult of the god Kabeiros and his son, Pais, was celebrated.

name: Kabnak
description: An important town of the Middle Elamite period discovered at the archaeological site of Haft Tepe in southwestern Iran. The site was an important center of the Elamite kingdom in the 15th century BCE, and also shows signs of inhabitation from the Parthian to the Early Islamic periods.

name: Kadembas/Katepants
description: Kadembas (Katepants) is a modern settlement in the Van Province in the Southeast of Turkey. Here several rock inscriptions of the Urartian king Minua (9th / 8th century BCE) were found which report the construction of the “Canal of Minua” which is still in use today. Furthermore a rock inscription of Tariria, wife of Minua, (9th / 8th century BCE) is located here which attributes a vineyard to her belongings.

name: Kadıkalesi/Anaia
description: The modern Turkish name Kadıkalesi denotes a modern coastal neighborhood in Turkey's Aydın province, the extensive Byzantine fortified settlement (sometimes "Kadı Kalesi") that gave the neighborhood its name, and the multi-period mound on which the remains of the Byzantine castle stand wherein there is evidence for human settlement extending back to the Calcolithic. The mound and vicinity were clearly important in Hittite, Greek, Roman, and Byzantine periods.

name: Kainua
description: An Etruscan town in the plain of the Po River at modern Marzabotto. It was founded in the early fifth century B.C.

name: Kaisaran Rock Relief
description: Kaisaran (Kaissaran, Kayseran Ekece) is a modern mountain village in the mountains east of the Keşiş Gölü ("Priest Lake"), an artificial lake created by Rusa, son of Erimena (probably 8th century BCE). At an elevation of 2,780 m above the village (more than an hour’s difficult ascent) a rock inscription of probably the same ruler was found in the late 19th century. Another rock inscription and a stela of Rusa, son of Erimena have been found in other places in the vicinity of the lake. These are the rock inscriptions situated in Erek Dağ (CTU I A 14-3) and the so-called Keşiş Göl stela (CTU I A 14-1) whose upper part has been found at Gövelek.

name: Kalaba(n)tia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 B5 Kalaba(n)tia

name: Kalaiou Nesoi
description: Kalaiou Nesoi, a group of nine islands in the Gulf of Oman.

name: Kalhu Archive 1
description: Two eighth-century-B.C. archives were excavated in Fort Shalmaneser, a building complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu. One archive was located in Rooms NE 47–50 at the northern side of the northeast courtyard. It consists of about eighty unsealed administrative texts, including twenty-two horse lists and forty-eight wine lists. A few clay tablets were also found in other rooms nearby.

name: Kalhu Archive 10
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). Inside the well of Room AB, British excavators found the leaves of approximately thirty ivory and wooden writing boards, thus constituting the largest and best-preserved discovery of writing boards in Mesopotamia. According to the Akkadian text preserved on one of the objects, some of the writing boards contain copies of the astrological series Enūma Anu Enlil belonging to “the palace of Sargon (II).”

name: Kalhu Archive 11
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). The archive found in Rooms ZT 13–17 was written between 687 BC and 614 BC, thus postdating this move. This repository contained about sixty clay tablets, specifically loan documents, the sale of slaves and land, juridical texts, marriage contracts, and administrative lists.

name: Kalhu Archive 12
description: An archive repository was discovered in Rooms K, M, and S of the Governor's Palace at Kalhu. This group of 217 clay tablets belongs to five successive governors of the city and dates from 835 BC to 710 BC. Apart from one literary text and a perfume recipe, the tablets discovered in Rooms K and M are generally legal documents, whereas the tablets in Room S are administrative texts. and letters (usually addressed to the governors).

name: Kalhu Archive 13
description: In Room 8 (the throne room) of the Burnt Palace, as well as in adjoining rooms, British excavators found letters addressed to and from the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (721–705 BC).

name: Kalhu Archive 14
description: The remains of a library containing about 280 clay tablets were found in and around Room NT12 the Nabû Temple (Ezida). These text date from 800 BC to 614 BC. The majority of the texts assigned to this archive repository were discovered in Room NT12 and the adjoining courtyard. Some texts, including fragments of seven wooden and ivory writing boards, were found in Rooms NT13 and NT14. This Neo-Assyrian ‘library’ house omen texts (79 tablets), incantation and medical texts (75 tablets), lexical lists (37 tablets), as well as some administrative texts, epics, hemerological texts, hymns, prayers, rituals, and royal inscriptions.

name: Kalhu Archive 15
description: British excavators found fragments of at least eight or nine large clay tablets in the Throne Room of the Nabû Temple (Ezida). These now-fragmentarily-preserved tablets, all of which date to 672 BC, contain copies of treaties that seventh-century-BC Assyrian king Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) made with several Median client rulers. These ‘covenant’ documents — which are often referred to as “Esarhaddon’s Succession Treaty” or “Esarhaddon’s Vassal Treaty” — were written up to ensure loyalty to Ashurbanipal, Esarhaddon’s fourth eldest son, when he ascended the Assyrian throne upon the death of his father.

name: Kalhu Archive 16
description: In Room NT16, on the southern side of the inner courtyard of the Nabû Temple (Ezida) at Kalhu, British excavators found twenty-one cuneiform clay dockets; these date from 699 to 652 BC. The texts in this archival repository are principal corn loan documents. Some might have been attached to Aramaic texts that were written on perishable materials, such as papyrus.

name: Kalhu Archive 17
description: Among private houses in the north-east of the citadel, in Room 19 of House III, British excavators found forty-seven clay tablets, including two dockets, which might have been once attached to Aramaic texts written on perishable material (e.g., papyrus). This private archive belonged to the eunuch Šamaš-šarru-uṣur and a few other influential men. While most of the texts are loans of silver, corn, or birds, eleven of the texts are sale documents. There is also a list of silver and birds connected to different citizens of Kalhu.

name: Kalhu Archive 2
description: Two eighth-century-B.C. archives were excavated in Fort Shalmaneser, a building complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu. Eleven tablets were found in Room SW 6, a wine store. The archive contains wine lists, as well as notes about the delivery of wine to the palace.

name: Kalhu Archive 3
description: Three seventh-century-B.C. archives were excavated in Fort Shalmaneser, a building complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu. In the northern corner of the south courtyard, mostly in Rooms SE 1 and SE 10, British excavators discovered the archive of the rab ēkalli (“palace manager”). The archive consists of twenty-seven tablets, most of which are sealed. These documents are concerned with the rab ēkalli’s work as manager. The sealed clay bulla were attached to wooden objects such as boxes or writing boards and secured with a string.

name: Kalhu Archive 4
description: Three seventh-century-B.C. archives were excavated in Fort Shalmaneser, a building complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu. The archive from Rooms SE 14–15 consists of nineteen clay tablets, mostly purchase documents but also some loan documents, a release documents, a legal settlement, and a letter. One of the loan documents is written in Neo-Babylonian dialect, instead of the usual Neo-Assyrian dialect.

name: Kalhu Archive 5
description: Three seventh-century-B.C. archives were excavated in Fort Shalmaneser, a building complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu. In Room S 10, in the inner southern part of the palace, British excavators found the archive of the queen's household and of her šakintu (“woman manager”). The archive consists of eighteen clay tablets; most of the documents are sealed. The principal text categories found here are purchase documents, loans, and lawsuits.

name: Kalhu Archive 6
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). Four hundred clay tablets dating to the reign of Sargon II and some of his predecessors were found in Room ZT 4, on the northern side of the northernmost courtyard of the palace. That archive consists of letters to and from the Assyrian king, administrative texts, lists, short notes, records of payments, and one literary text. There were also two rows of open brick boxes in that room of the Northwest Palace; these might have been intended for the storage of clay tablets.

name: Kalhu Archive 7
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). A few tablets were found in Rooms ZT 30 and ZT 31, on the eastern side of the northernmost courtyard of the palace. The administrative texts found in this archive repository include lists of quantities of oil (delivered to the palace), which were presumably stored in the large jars that were also found inside both rooms.

name: Kalhu Archive 8
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). Fourteen clay tablets and sealings, dating from 719 to 715 BC, were found in Room HH, in the southeastern part of the palace.

name: Kalhu Archive 9
description: Most documents found in the Northwest Palace, built by the Assyrian king Ashurnarsipal II (883–859 BC), predate the move of the new Assyrian capital to Dur-Šarrukin under Sargon II (721–705 BC). An archive repository of 180 clay tablets were found in Room 57. These texts, which date from 800 to 736 BC, are mostly sale documents; there is also a list of oxen. The older documents in the archive generally deal with the purchase of slaves and houses, whereas the younger documents record the purchase of fields, some by servants of the queen.

name: Kalindoia
description: An ancient settlement in Macedonia, located near modern Kalamoto.

name: Kalkriese
description: The presumptive archaeological site of the Battle of the Teutoburg Forest (A.D. 9).

name: Kalliroe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B2 Kalliroe

name: Kalloni
description: A Middle Helladic to Late Helladic settlement located near Kalloni, Troizenia, Attiki, the Kalloni site was excavated in 2005.

name: Kalydnai/Lagusae (islands)
description: A small island group of the northern Aegean Sea.

name: Kalydnioi (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Kalydnioi Inss.

name: Kamiros
description: An ancient city of Rhodes, with evidence for Mycenaean occupation. From the eighth century BC, a Greek settlement flourished, with a temple dedicated to the goddess Athena on its acropolis. The Hellenistic phase of the site followed the tenets of Hippodamian planning. The city was twice destroyed by earthquakes (226 and 142 BC).

name: Kammenyi Log I
description: Settlement in the Middle Yenisei containing finds dated to the 13th century BCE.

name: Kamouh el Hermel
description: An ancient pyramidal monument located 6 km south of Hermel in the Baalbek-Hermel Governorate, Lebanon. The monument likely dates to the second century BCE.

name: Kanī Jūnī
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found, Kanī Jūnī (I).

name: Kanī Jūnī
description: Bronze Age site, low mound in Lorestan where cuneiform brick inscription has been found, Kanī Jūnī (II).

name: Kānī Shāyah
description: A small site in Kurdistan, Iraq, consisting of a high and low mound with remains from the fourth and early third millennium BCE.

name: Kaphyai
description: Kaphyai or Caphyae was an ancient settlement of Arcadia in Greece, located northwest of the lake of Orchomenus.

name: Kar-Assurnazirpal
description: Town founded by Assurnazipal II, mound (Tall Masaikh) in the Lower Middle Euphrates valley, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Kar-Mullissi
description: Assyrian city.

name: Kar-Mullissu
description: Kar-Mullissu (Akkadian "Quay of the goddess Mullissu") is an Assyrian town in northern Iraq. Modern Karemles (also Karemlash and Karemlish) is located less than twenty-nine km southeast of Mosul (ancient Nineveh). Inscribed bricks of the seventh-century-BCE Assyrian king Sargon II have been discovered there.

name: Kār-Tukultī-Ninurta
description: A place from the TAVO Index

name: Karabel Reliefs
description: A monument consisting of multiple Hittite rock reliefs with Luwian inscriptions, probably dating to the 13th century BCE, located in the Karabel pass on the southern slopes of Mount Nif in Turkey's Izmir province. Most of the reliefs and inscriptions known to western scholars from the 19th and 20th centuries have been lost to road construction and vandalism.

name: Karaftu
description: At Karaftu is a series of rock-cut chambers with a Greek inscription, dating to the fourth or third century BCE. The site is located in Kordestān Province, Iran.

name: Karakala
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Karataş
description: Karataş is a modern settlement situated at the northern bank of Lake Van and 2 km in the east of Erciş in the southeast of Turkey. Here two rock inscriptions of the Urartian king Sarduri II., son of Argišti (8th century BCE) have been found. One of them is of annalistic contents (CTU A 9-10), the other reports the plantation of a vineyard in this region (CTU A 9-11).

name: Karban-I
description: Burial site in the Northern Altai region containing remains associated with the Bulan-Koby culture (2nd-5th centuries CE).

name: Kārībahīn
description: Find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Karmir-blur
description: Karmir Blur (or "Red Hill") is the ancient Urartian site of Teishebaini, a fortified city of the seventh century BC built by Rusa II.

name: Karmylessos?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 B4 Karmylessos?

name: Karnak
description: A large temple complex in Egypt, associated with the ancient site of Thebes and Luxor and inscribed together with them on the UNESCO World Heritage list.

name: Karpasiai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E1 Karpasiai Inss.

name: Karthaia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Karthaia

name: Kastri 2
description: "Kastri 2" is the site of a Late Minoan settlement that is located to the west of Apodoulou, in Rethymno, Crete.

name: Kastro Phaneromenis/Kastro Kochylou
description: One of the two known Genovese fortresses on Andros.

name: Kavousi Vronda
description: At Kavousi Vronda in Eastern Crete (also Vronda or Βροντάς or Βρόντας) is an archaeological site located approximately 1.25 km south of the modern village of Kavousi, in the municipality of Ierapetra, prefecture of Lasithi. The site was in use from the Final Neolithic period (later fourth millennium BCE) onwards.

name: Kawala Kandal
description: Assyrian site near a pass in the Chiya-î Qerechugh hill.

name: Kazane Höyük
description: Mound with remains of fortification, close to modern city Şanlıurfa.

name: Kebili (formerly Babil)
description: The small village of Babil (now Kebili) is located about 25 km southwest of Cizre, near the Tigris river, directly on the Turkish-Syrian border. The name of the ancient site whose ruins lay beneath the modern village is not known.

name: Kekropeion
description: The tomb and shrine of Kekrops, first king of Athens, located on the Acropolis. In the sixth century BC it was marked by a giant Ionic column, which was destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC. The site was partially covered by the southwestern corner of the Erechtheion in the second half of the fifth century BC.

name: Kellis
description: A Roman to Late Antique settlement with substantial remains of public religious buildings, including a large temple, residential areas, tombs, and three churches.

name: Kenan Tepe
description: Ancient site on the Tigris river in Turkey

name: Kenk Boğazı
description: The site of an Assyrian relief of Shalmaneser III, which is located 10 km away from the Karasu relief.

name: Kerameikos
description: A district of ancient Athens located northwest of the Acropolis, the Kerameikos takes its name from the potters' workshops in the area; the portion of the district lying outside the Dipylon Gate served as a necropolis.

name: Kermario alignments
description: A complex comprised of 1029 megaliths arranged in 10 rows.

name: Keryneia
description: An ancient city located on a hill immediately north of the modern Greek village of Mamousia (Achaea).

name: Keskese
description: The archaeological site of Keskese is located approximately 8 km north of Matara. It is the site of numerous stelai dating ca. 500 BCE.

name: Khabiṣ
description: Neolithic site located about 5 km east of the town of Shadad on the edge of the Dasht-i Lut (Lut desert) in southeastern Iran.

name: Khalandritsa
description: A fortified Mycenaean settlement occupies a hilltop near modern Khalandritsa, Greece.

name: Kharaneh IV
description: Kharaneh IV is an archaeological site located in Jordan. This site has produced evidence of human occupation during the Late Pleistocene, ca. 18,900 to 16,600 BCE.

name: Kharg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G4 Kharg

name: Khirbat al-Mudayna
description: A small Moabite sanctuary within a walled-in enclosure was discovered on the Wadi ath-Thamad in Jordan.

name: Khirbet ed Deir
description: The early Byzantine monastery located at Khirbet ed-Deir in the Judean desert was excavated from 1981-1987 by Yizhar Hirschfeld. The remote site yielded important evidence about monastic life from the fourth to seventh centuries CE.

name: Khirbet el-Mastarah
description: An archaeological site located in the middle Jordan Valley in the West Bank that includes the largest known of the complex oval compound type habitation sites connected with the Israelite Settlement period.

name: Khufu's ship
description: The sealed burial pit adjacent to the Great Pyramid contained a remarkably well preserved Old Kingdom ship that has been interpreted as the solar barge of the pharaoh Khufu.

name: Khurha
description: A partially-excavated complex near modern Qum in Iran. The site was possibly a sanctuary and likely dates to the Seleucid age. There is some evidence that the site continued in use into the Parthian period.

name: Khwaja Ali Sehyaka/Sehyak
description: This "Mesopotamian-style temple" site located near modern Dishu in Afghanistan's Helmand province was excavated in the 1970s. Carbon-14 dates and "Greek-style architectural fragments and fine red pottery" led the excavators to date the use of the site from the late Hellenistic and Parthian period to "the beginning of the Sasanian period in the 3rd century" CE. Finds included a fragmentary bilingual (Greek/Aramaic) inscription possibly mentioning a Parthian king Phraates and the Iranian deity Ahuramazda.

name: Kierion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C2 Kierion

name: Kimūnah
description: A site with a Bronze Age palace, periodically submerged in the Tigris River.

name: Kinneret
description: Early Iron Age settlement on the Sea of Galilee covering about 25 acres of a hilltop.

name: Kirikongo
description: Archaeological site in Burkina Faso occupied from the early first millennium CE through c. 1700. The site consists of a mound spread over approximately 37 hectares. Evidence for ironworking is present along with household structures.

name: Kirra
description: An ancient port settlement located at modern Magoula Xeropigadas in the Greek municipality of Itea near where the Pleistos river debouches into the Corinthian Gulf. Classical Kirra has earlier, Helladic antecedents in this area.

name: Kish
description: Kish was an ancient city of Sumer located east of Babylon in Mesopotamia. Kish was occupied from the Jemdet Nasr period (ca. 3100 BC) until its abandonment under the Seleucids.

name: Kisurra
description: Kisurra (modern Tell Abu Hatab) was established in the Early Dynastic II period (ca. 2700 BCE). The importance of this settlement, which was located only a few kilometers north of Šuruppak (modern Fārā) and which was situated on the western bank of the Euphrates River, declined significantly during the Old Babylonian Period. Numerous cuneiform tablets have been unearthed at Tell Abu Hatab since 1902.

name: Kition
description: Kition was an ancient city founded on the southern coast of Cyprus in the thirteenth century B.C.

name: Kition: Military Port and Shipsheds
description: Covered sheds (neoria) in the ancient harbor of Kition used in the construction and repair of warships by the Phoenician rulers of the city during the fifth century BCE.

name: Kizkapanli
description: A modern village near the Pazarcik dam in Turkey. The Pazarcik Stele, which bears inscriptions of the Assyrian kings Adad-nerari III and Shalmaneser IV, was discovered here.

name: Kleides (islands)
description: An archipelago extending from the northeast tip of the Karpas Peninsula on the island of Cyprus.

name: Kleidí
description: An archaeological site located on a coastal hill near Samikon, Kleidi presents evidence for both prehistoric settlement and a cemetery.

name: Kleonai
description: An ancient city located in the mountainous area southwest of Corinth. The acropolis of ancient Kleonai lies about two kilometers east of the modern town named after it in the 1960s (Archaies Kleones, formerly Kontostavlos).

name: Klimataria
description: The so-called "Minoan Villa" of Klimataria is located on the 80-meter-high hill of Manares (Μάναρες) on the road from Piskokefalo (Πισκοκέφαλο) to Sitia (Σητεία).

name: Knocknakilla megaliths
description: A megalithic complex located between Macroom and Millstreet, in County Cork, Ireland, and thought to have been constructed ca. 3,500 years ago.

name: Koçyatağı
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription.

name: Kodrigai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C3 Kodrigai

name: Koh Ker
description: An ancient temple complex of the Khmer Empire, Koh Ker is also known as Lingapura or Chok Gargyar. The site is located in the Srayong commune of Preah, Vihear province's Kulen district. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in September 2023 and covers some 1,187 ha.

name: Kokkonatou (islands)
description: The Socotra archipelago in the Indian Ocean was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 2008 on account of its biodiversity.

name: Kom el-Dikka
description: Part of the ancient city of Alexandria where the Roman theater and baths are located.

name: Konār Sandal
description: Konār Sandal is a Bronze Age archaeological site associated with the "Jiroft culture" (third millennium BCE). The site is located in the valley of the Halil River just south of Jiroft, Kermān Province, Iran. There are, in fact, two tells situated a few kilometers apart. By convention, these mounds are referred to as Konar Sandal A and B.

name: Korsiai (islands)
description: The Aegean archipelago lying between Ikaros and Samos, known today as the Fourni Korseon. Its two main islands are known today as Thymena (to the west) and Fourni (to the east).

name: Korykiai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 A1 Korykiai Inss.

name: Kouloures at Phaistos
description: Circular, stone, pit-like structures built into the ground at the edge of the West Court of the Minoan palace at Phaistos.

name: Koumbi Saleh
description: The ruined city of Koumbi Saleh in south-east Mauritania may have been the capital of the Ghana Empire.

name: Kourion
description: An ancient settlement in the southern part of the island of Cyprus, located southwest of Episkopi in the Lemesos district.

name: Koyuk Shahri
description: An ancient fortified site known as either Koyuk Shahri or Kuiyukexiehai'er that is located in the Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region of north-western China preserves evidence of urban occupation in the Tarim Basin during the first millennium BCE. The site consists of a walled area of some 60,000 m² with an artificial mound at the center of the walled space.

name: Krane
description: An ancient settlement near modern Argostoli on the island of Kefalonia.

name: Krasnyĭ mai͡ak
description: Late Scythian burial site on the Lower Dnieper River containing burials dated to the 3rd century BCE-2nd century CE.

name: Krenides/Philippi/Col. Augusta Iulia Philippensis
description: An ancient city founded as Philippi by the king of Macedon, Philip II of Macedon on the site of the Thasian colony of Krinides.

name: Krokodilo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 C2 Krokodilo

name: Kromna
description: An ancient settlement located between Kenchreai and Korinth. Archaeological survey and excavation reveal a rich record of human occupation from the Neolithic period onward in the area around modern Hexamilia. The Archaic through Hellenistic center seems to have been concentrated east of Hexamilia in the area of modern Kesimia (just west of Ag. Demetrios), with a possible subsequent Roman-era shift to the southwest around Monastiraki.

name: Kul'chukskoye
description: A fortified settlement in the chora of Chersonesos Taurike, dating from the Late Classical and Hellenistic periods, and reused in the early Medieval period.

name: Kūlfaraḥ
description: Place for Neo-elamite rock inscriptions.

name: Küllüoba Höyüğü
description: Küllüoba Höyüğü is the site of a large settlement mound dating from the Chalcolithic period and continuing to the Early Bronze Age.

name: Kumidi
description: Bronze age settlement.

name: Kūnārā
description: Bronze Age site with upper and lower towns and remains of a monumental building.

name: Kurşun Kalesi
description: Remains of an ancient hilltop settlement, with houses, agricultural processing areas, a stoa, a temple, and some ancient Greek inscriptions located within the modern Turkish municipality of Yeğenli (Mersin province). The site's ancient name is not known, but it has also been discussed under other modern names including Asar, İsmailli, and Kurşunlu.

name: Kuşsaray
description: A Bronze Age archaeological site consisting of a mound with finds of cuneiform tablets. The site was only briefly excavated.

name: Kyaneai
description: An ancient city of Lycia and the chief settlement in the region between Antiphellos and Myra.

name: Kyaneai (islands)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Kyaneai Inss.

name: Kythnos
description: The chief, homonymous city on the Cycladic island of Kythnos (modern Vriokastro/Episkopi).

name: L-Shaped Palace at Gla
description: A complex of multifunctional rooms at Gla with two connected wings that extend from a right angle to form two ʺmegaronsʺ that lacked hearths and throne rooms. The palace was built around 1300 BCE.

name: L’Ecluse
description: Remains of fortifications dating to the Late Roman period.

name: La Almoloya
description: La Almoloya, located in south-eastern Spain, is an archaeological site connected with the El Argar society of the Early Bronze Age. The site includes especially wealthy burials and a sprawling architectural complex that some scholars have equated with a sort of meeting hall or parliament.

name: La Cabañeta
description: A Roman-style settlement at modern El Burgo de Ebro, located on the south bank of the Ebro river, southeast of Zaragoza, Spain. The settlement appears to have been founded in the second century BCE and permanently destroyed in the first century, likely during the Sertorian War (82-72 BCE).

name: La Chapelle-des-Fougeretz
description: A Gallo-Roman settlement that persisted until the fourth century of the modern era at modern La Chapelle-des-Fougeretz. Known since the 20th century, rescue excavations ahead of a housing development on the southwest edge of the modern town were conducted in 2022 and revealed a sanctuary of Mars, a civic bath complex, and a late-antique necropolis. The site's ancient name(s) are unknown.

name: La Fonteta
description: Phoenician port located at the mouth of the Segura River on Spain's Mediterranean coast.

name: La Giostra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 La Giostra

name: La Hougue Bie
description: A Neolithic ritual site that includes a passage grave dating ca. 4000-3500 BCE.

name: La Montjoie
description: A monumental Gallo-Roman column.

name: Lacippo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 E5 Lacippo

name: Lacus Curtius
description: The Lacus Curtius is an area in medio foro connected with the earliest phases of Rome's occupation. By the late Republican period, it had become a small basin set inside a paved area. Livy relates the famous episode of the Sabine Mettius Curtius in relation to his description of the site.

name: Lagash
description: Lagash was an important Sumerian city of the late third millennium B.C.

name: Lagina
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 G3 Lagina

name: Lamā Cemetery
description: Cemetery site near Lama village, Iran with almost 60 graves dating to the late 2nd-early 1st millennium CE. Most of the graves were destroyed by bulldozing in the area for a road in December 1999.

name: Lanuéjols
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A1 Lanuéjols

name: Laodicea ad Libanum
description: Laodicea ad Libanum was a Hellenistic city located on the Orontes River in Coele-Syria.

name: Lapethos/Lepethis Phoinikon
description: Located on the north coast of Cyprus 10 km west of Keryneia, Lapethos/Lepethis Phoinikon is known through archaeological remains for the most part. Archaeological evidence shows settlement already in the Late Bronze Age, continuing to the first millennium BCE and beyond. The tradition of Lapethos holds that the kingdom was founded by Praxandros from Lakonia in the Peloponnese.

name: Lapis Niger
description: A shrine in the Forum Romanum adjacent to the Republican comitium, recognizable by the distinctive black paving stones that covered it in the later republican period. The shrine has been associated, in tradition, with the tomb of Romulus and/or of Faustulus. Giacomo Boni excavated the site between 1899 and 1905.

name: Largo di Torre Argentina
description: A square in the Campus Martius of Rome, at the center of which lies an archaeological site that includes the remains of four temples of the Republican period, as well as elements of the first century B.C. theater complex of Cnaeus Pompeius.

name: Larsa
description: Larsa (modern Tell as-Senkereh), a city located ca. 21 km east of Uruk, was an important southern Mesopotamian cult center of the sun-god (Utu/Šamaš). Its principal religious structure was called Ebabbar, just like the Šamaš temple in the northern Babylonian city of Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial names of some of Larsa’s other religious buildings are mentioned in two first-millennium-BC lists of ziggurats and some royal inscriptions, especially from the Isin-Larsa Period (ca. 2025–1763 BC) — when the city of Larsa temporarily became a dominant political power (ca. 1924–1763 BC) — and the Neo-Babylonian Period. The most important of these, at least in the sixth century BC, was the temple tower Edurana, which sometimes went by the name Eduranki. At the beginning of the second millennium BC (ca. 1924 BC), under a man called Gungunum (r. 1932–1906 BC), Larsa broke free of Isin’s dominance over it and established its political dynasty. Although it grew more powerful than its former overlord, Larsa never accumulated a significant amount of territory. At its peak, under the authority of Rīm-Sîn I (r. 1822–1763 BC), it controlled only ten to fifteen other city-states. This city’s political dominance, as well as its dynastic line, was brought to an end by Hammu-rāpi of Babylon (r. 1792–1750 BC). After 1763 BC, Larsa was never again a major political player in Babylonia.

name: Las Médulas
description: Las Médulas is the site of substantial Roman gold mines that have been listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Lateran Obelisk
description: The Lateran Obelisk is a monumental Egyptian obelisk, completed during the reign of Thutmose IV (ca. 1400 BCE) and looted from Karnak in the early fourth century CE.

name: Lattara
description: A walled port city inhabited by Gauls, Etruscans, and Massalian Greeks.

name: Laureion
description: A mining region of Attika famous in antiquity for its silver mines. These mines were one of the chief sources of revenue for the Athenian state.

name: Lchashen
description: Urartian rock inscription near the town Lchashen.

name: Lederata
description: A Roman auxiliary fort on the Danube river at modern Ram (Serbia). It was reduced in size by the time of Diocletian and then renovated and expanded under Justinian.

name: Leibethra
description: An ancient settlement whose acropolis has been located at Kanalia, which is near the modern towns of Skotina and Leptokarya in the Greek Dion-Olympus municipality.

name: Leontini
description: A Greek colonial city founded by Naxians in 729 B.C., Leontini was reduced by Hippocrates of Gela in 498 B.C. Following the Sicilian Expedition, the city became subject to Syracuse. In Roman times it may have been of little importance and was destroyed by the Saracens in A.D. 848.

name: Leontion
description: An ancient settlement of Achaea in the northern Pelopnnese, the site is located on a hillside known today as Kastritsi at the northern foot of mount Erymanthos. It is not to be confused with the modern settlement of Leontio, which is located about 10 km north.

name: Lerna
description: Lerna was an ancient settlement on the east coast of the Peloponnesus. Extensive Bronze Age layers were excavated in the twentieth century by J. L. Caskey.

name: Les Cars
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H3 Les Cars

name: Les Tours-Mirandes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F2 Les Tours-Mirandes

name: Les Vaux de la Celle
description: An ancient Gallo-Roman sanctuary near modern Genainville (France), with excavated remains of a temple, nymphaeum, and theater: BAtlas 11 B3 Génainville (sic)

name: Letoa (island)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C2 Letoa Ins.

name: Letoon
description: Sanctuary of Leto and center of the Lycian federation.

name: Leuctra tropaion
description: A trophy monument erected by the Thebans following their defeat of the Spartans in 371 BC.

name: Leukai Inss.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 B2 Leukai Inss.

name: Leukos Limen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A2 Leukos Limen

name: Leuktron
description: Leuktron was an ancient settlement in the southern Peloponnese. Pausanias places it twenty stades from Pephnus.

name: Li Castelli
description: An ancient settlement of the Messapians located near the modern town of San Pancrazio Salentino on the Salento peninsula of Italy, the site was later used as a Roman military installation. The ancient name of the site is unknown.

name: Li Schiavoni (Nardò, Lecce)
description: Li Schiavoni is the site of a fortified Messapic center that flourished in the mid-first millennium BCE.

name: library at Nysa on the Maeander
description: A second-century CE library located at Nysa on the Maeander.

name: Library of Celsus
description: The Library of Celsus is a library and monumental tomb in Ephesus which was constructed ca. 110-120 CE.

name: Library of Hadrian at Athens
description: The Library of Hadrian is located on the north side of the Athenian Acropolis, immediately to the north of the Roman Agora. The Roman emperor Hadrian built the complex in A.D. 131/2. It was subsequently destroyed during the Herulian sack of Athens in A.D. 267.

name: Library of Pergamum
description: The Attalid ruler Eumenes ll built and filled a library at Pergamum between 197 and 159 BCE. Nineteenth century excavators argued for the identification of a series of rooms behind the north stoa of the temple of Athena on the acropolis as housing this library, but more recent scholarship has called this identification into significant doubt (Coqueugniot 2013).

name: Lichades Islands
description: An island group off North Euboea, Greece. Near the Malian Gulf.

name: Lidar
description: Lidar or Lidar Höyük is an archaeological site located in southeastern Anatolia, to the northwest of Sanliurfa, Türkiye, and just southwest of the village of Lidar (district of Bozova). This settlement mound measured roughly 200x240 meters and stood 25 m high. Its remains are now inundated by the Atatürk Dam.

name: Limnaion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C1 Limnaion

name: Limyra
description: An ancient settlement located at modern Yuvalılar in the district of Finike, Antalya province, Turkey.

name: Lindinis
description: A Roman fort is thought to have been established at Lindinis (modern Ilchester) ca. 60 CE. The site protected the crossing of the Fosse Way over the River Yeo. The settlement developed in phases from the first to the fourth centuries CE. It was eventually one of only two known settlements with a stone wall in the territory of the Durotriges. The inhabitants of the town seem to have been reasonably wealthy, as a number of domestic complexes have fine mosaic pavements.

name: Lion of Amphipolis (monument)
description: The Lion of Amphipolis is a fourth-century BC tomb sculpture from Amphipolis in northern Greece. It may originally have been connected with the Kasta Tomb.

name: Little Palace at Knossos
description: The so-called "Little Palace at Knossos" lies some 200 m northwest of the main Minoan "palace" center and to the east of the Unexplored Mansion to which it was once linked.

name: Lixus
description: Lixus was an ancient settlement of Mauretania Tingitana. It was founded as a Punic port settlement as early as the eighth century B.C. Under the Roman emperor Claudius Lixus was elevated to the rank of colonia.

name: Lochmaben Stone
description: A standing megalith located near the mouth on the Solway Firth and dating ca. 3000 B.C.

name: Locmariaquer megaliths
description: At Locmariaquer, Brittany, are the remains of a complex of Neolithic structures.

name: Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar)
description: Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar) is an archaeological site located in the community of Los Villares (Huétor-Tájar). Here remains of a significant settlement dating to the third to sixth centuries CE have been discovered.

name: Lucone di Polpenazze
description: The archaeological site of Polpenazze del Garda (BS) Lucone is situated in a morainic basin in northern Italy. It is the site of several prehistoric settlements, which have been documented since archaeological excavations began in 2007. The Early Bronze Age settlement was active from ca. 2034 BCE until 1967 BCE. The site is included in the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Prehistoric Pile Dwellings around the Alps".

name: Ludus Magnus
description: A gladiatorial training school adjacent to the Flavian Amphitheater in Rome. The structure is attested on the Severan marble plan of Rome.

name: Luna
description: Luna (the modern Luni) is an ancient settlement of Liguria. It was re-founded by the Romans as a colonial settlement in 177 BC.

name: Luxmanda archaeological site
description: A large settlement in Tanzania dating to the Pastoral Neolithic period in eastern Africa.

name: Lyceum
description: A sanctuary of Apollo Lyceios in Athens, which served as the seat of Aristotle's Peripatetic school of philosophy founded in 335/4 BCE. The Lyceum was destroyed by Sulla in 86 BCE.

name: Macedonian palace at Pella
description: The Macedonian palace at Pella, now only partially excavated, occupied an area of perhaps 60,000 square meters. The palace consisted of up to seven large architectural groupings.

name: Macellum (Pompeii)
description: The Macellum of Pompeii is located just outside the northeast corner of the Forum of the city.

name: Macellum of Puteoli
description: A macellum of Roman Puteoli. The discovery of a statue of Serapis at the site led to a misidentification of the building as a Serapeum.

name: Macellum of Sagalassos
description: The Roman Macellum of Sagalassos.

name: Maen Llia
description: Maen Llia is a standing stone located in the Brecon Beacons, Wales.

name: Magidû
description: Magidû or Megiddo (modern Tell al-Mutesellim, "The Tell of the Governor") is an ancient settlement mound located in northern Israel near Kibbutz Megiddo, about 30 km southeast of Haifa. Bronze Age Megiddo was an important Canaanite city-state and during the Iron Age, it was a royal city in the Kingdom of Israel. The site is mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources.

name: Magnesia ad Maeandrum/Leukophrys
description: Magnesia ad Maeandrum was an ancient city on the Maeander River in Ionia, site of an important sanctuary of Artemis Leukophryene ("of the white eyebrow"). Its extensive remains are located near the modern village of Tekin in Turkey's Aydın Province.

name: Mahabalipuram
description: This group of sanctuaries was carved out of rock along the Coromandel coast in the seventh and eighth centuries. The Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram is a UNESCO World Heritage site.

name: Māhān
description: Tomb of Shah Nematollah Vali, find place for cuneiform inscription.

name: Maḥmūd Ābād
description: Mound and find place for a stone block with Urartian inscription.

name: Maiden Tower (Baku)
description: A twelfth-century monument in the Old City, Baku, Azerbaijan.

name: Makistos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B2 Makistos

name: Makri St. peristyle
description: Remains of a Hellenistic stoa initially identified via salvage excavation in the 1960s and further explored ca. 2012. J. Travlos identified the remains as those of the Palladion court.

name: Malichou islands
description: A group of 10 major volcanic islands located in the Red Sea.

name: Mallia
description: Mallia is a site on the northern coast of Crete that was the location of a major Minoan palatial center of the Middle Bronze Age.

name: Malloura
description: Located at Malloura, Cyprus, are the remains of human settlement activity ranging from the Archaic period in the eastern Mediterranean to the time of the Roman Empire. The site has yielded evidence of a sanctuary with votive activity as well as funereal evidence. Modern exploration of the site began in 1990 under the direction of Michael K. Toumazou.

name: Mampsis
description: The Nabataean city of Mampsis or Memphis (Ancient Greek: Μέμφις) was an important station on the Incense Road.

name: Mandra (Despotiko)
description: A substantial multiperiod sanctuary and associated complex on the Aegean island of Despotiko. Structures and finds date from the early Iron Age through Roman times, with some evidence for use or reuse into the Byzantine period.

name: Maniolai (islands)
description: Islands mentioned in Ptolemy. They may correspond to the modern Andaman islands in the Bay of Bengal.

name: mansio, via Tiburtina
description: A mansio located at the seventh mile of the Via Tiburtina.

name: Mantai
description: Mantai was an ancient coastal town and harbor of northern Sri Lanka.

name: Mardaman
description: Early and Middle Bronze Age city in northern Mesopotamia.

name: Māri
description: Mari (Tell Hariri) was an ancient Semitic city that flourished as a trade center and hegemonic state between 2900 BC until 1759 BC. The site was submitted as a tentative candidate for the UNESCO World Heritage list in 1999.

name: Mariana
description: Mariana was a Roman city on the east coast of Corsica founded by Caius Marius ca. 93 BC.

name: Marion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A2 Marion

name: Marlik
description: Burial site in northern Iran containing over fifty burials dated to the mid-2nd millennium BCE.

name: Marmachyn
description: Place for Urartian rock inscription, 1km from Marmashen Monastery.

name: Marmara sanctuary
description: An ancient sanctuary, perhaps sacred to Demeter, that was discovered during tunneling for the Corinth to Patras highway. The terraced site is located at Marmara, SSE of Aigeira. It dates from the seventh century BCE to the third century BCE.

name: Marmoutier Abbey
description: The Marmoutier Abbey located near Tours, Indre-et-Loire, France is also known as the Abbey of Marmoutier or Marmoutiers. Saint Martin of Tours (316-397) founded the abbey in 372 after he became Bishop of Tours in 371.

name: Maşat Höyük
description: A Bronze Age Hittite center ca. 100 km east of Boğazkale/Hattusa.

name: Mashkan-Shapir
description: Mashkan-Shapir, covering 56 ha, was occupied during the Uruk period and becomes more prominent during the Akkadian period and then during the Ur III period. The site is known today as Tall Abū Duwārī.

name: Matara
description: Matara, in Eritrea, is an archaeological site located along the coast road to Adulis and dating to pre-Aksumite or early Aksumite times. It is possibly to be identified as the Koloe mentioned in a Roman imperial source.

name: Matzanni
description: A sanctuary that includes a Punic temple built in white limestone, a nuraghe, and a sacred well.

name: Mausoleo di Monte dell'Incastro
description: A circular, domed, apsidal subterranean structure of the Hadrianic period that is referred to as a tomb but may have had a cultic function.

name: Mausoleum Augusti
description: A monumental tomb built in the Campus Martius at Rome by Augustus and dedicated in 28 B.C. The mausoleum served as a family tomb for the Iulio-Claudians.

name: Mausoleum of the First Qin Emperor
description: The mausoleum of Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor of the Qin dynasty of China, was built between 246 and 208 BCE. The mausoleum itself was outfitted with numerous ancillary features, including the famed "terracotta army" discovered in 1974. The site was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1987.

name: Me-Turnat
description: Me-Turnat was a Bronze Age and Iron Age settlement located on the Diyala river.

name: Medeon
description: Medeon was a polis of ancient Greece with occupation stretching from the Archaic period to Late Antiquity. It is located southeast of Aspra Spitia, Viotia, Central Greece.

name: Mediana
description: An extensive late-Roman villa used as a temporary imperial residence in the suburbs of ancient Naissus. Located in the modern village of Brzi brod, within the municipality of Niš, Serbia (BAtlas 21 E6 Mediana).

name: Medina Azahara
description: The Caliphate City of Medina Azahara is a city built in the mid-tenth century CE by the Umayyad dynasty as the seat of the Caliphate of Cordoba.

name: Medinet Bu Hindi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Medinet Bu Hindi

name: Megali Koryfi
description: Megali Koryfi, an archaeological site on the island of Aegina in proximity to the Christian chapel of Ag. Antonios that is noted for the presence of substantial remains of drystone fortifications dating to the Mycenaean period.

name: Megali Magoula
description: A Middle to Late Bronze Age settlement located on the hill of Megali Magoula that includes Mycenaean chamber tombs.

name: Megaloi Theoi (sanctuary, Samothrace)
description: The "Sanctuary of the great gods" on the island of Samothrace, one of the chief Panhellenic sanctuaries of the ancient Greek world.
