description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 unnamed feature ()

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 unnamed feature ()

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 C2 unnamed feature ()

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 F1 unnamed monastery (on Gallinaria Ins.)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 F4 unnamed unlabeled ((1 monastery))

name: Vega del Mar
description: A paleochristian basilica.

name: Victoria Church
description: A paleochristian church located at Thugga.

name: Villa gallo-romaine de Barat de Vin
description: Rediscovered in the 19th century and partially excavated in the 1960s, this Roman-era complex east of modern Sorde-l'Abbaye (Landes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine in France) has been variously identified as a villa and a road station. Discoveries at the site included stone walls, a courtyard, a portico, baths, a mosaic floor, and a bordering Roman-era roadway. A medieval church indicated in documents and early maps seems to have used some of the ancient structures. The site appears to be largely overgrown and inaccessible today.

name: Villa Pamphili
description: The church of San Pancrazio was founded in the sixth century A.D. by Pope Symmachus. It stands beyond the Porta San Pancrazio on the Ianiculum. Below the church is a massive catacomb referred to as S. Pancrazio or Ottavilla.

name: Villa S. Silvestro
description: The remains of a Republican temple podium now support a Christian church at Villa S. Silvestro in Umbria. The temple's podium likely dates, in its first phase, to the third century B.C.

name: Visigothic basilica at Segobriga
description: The basilica of Cabeza del Griego at Segobriga dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

name: Wadi Crema
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Wadi Crema

name: Wadi Nagat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Wadi Nagat

name: Zacchaeus?, Mon.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 G2 Zacchaeus?, Mon.

name: Άγιος Νικόλαος
description: A paleochristian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE, located near the harbor of Limani Khersonesou, Pediada District, northern Crete.

name: *Aquae
description: *Aquae or pagus Aquensis was a bath site located along the Strei river in the territory of Ulpia Traiana Sarmizegetusa.

name: *Evaunum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Evaunum

name: *Thermae Privatae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 *Thermae Privatae

name: Ad Aquas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 B3 Ad Aquas

name: Ad Aquas Caesaris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 A2 Ad Aquas Caesaris

name: Ad Mediam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Ad Mediam

name: Agamemnoniai Thermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Agamemnoniai Thermai

name: Aidepsos (spa)
description: A famous Euboean spa city in the Hellenistic and Roman periods, located at modern Loutra Aidepsou. The polis' original location may have been 3-4 km north in the area of a modern town that carries the modern name Aidepsos (previously Lipso).

name: Ain el Hammam
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C2 Ain el Hammam

name: Aix-en-Diois
description: Among various features at Aix-en-Diois are the remains of baths that could either pertain to a sanctuary or a villa.

name: Amélie-les-Bains
description: Remains of a Gallo-Roman bath complex are now incorporated in a modern spa at Amélie-les-Bains-Palada in the French department of Pyrénées-Orientales.

name: Aponus
description: An ancient thermal site known as Aponi fons or Aquae Patavinae.

name: Aquae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D1 Aquae

name: Aquae *Arnemetiae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F1 Aquae *Arnemetiae

name: Aquae Albulae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Aquae Albulae

name: Aquae Albulae
description: A group of springs located west of Tibur (modern Tivoli). The main spring, known as Lago della Regina, preserves traces of an ancient Roman bath.

name: Aquae Apollinares
description: The Aquae Apollinares were thermal springs located between Careiae and Tarquinii, perhaps to be associated with the Bagni di Stigliano or Bagni di Vicarello.

name: Aquae Apollinares Novae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Aquae Apollinares Novae

name: Aquae Aptuccensium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 C4 Aquae Aptuccensium

name: Aquae Bormonis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 A3 Aquae Bormonis?

name: Aquae Caeretanae
description: Therapeutic springs located near Caere.

name: Aquae Calidae
description: Aquae Calidae was an ancient spa depicted on the Peutinger Map. Modern scholarship associates it with archaeological remains found in modern Vichy (France).

name: Aquae Calidae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 E6 Aquae Calidae

name: Aquae Calidae
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Malavella

name: Aquae Calidae Neapolitanorum
description: A site of settlement connected to thermal springs, according to Ptolemy.

name: Aquae Carpitanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Aquae Carpitanae

name: Aquae Convenarum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F2 Aquae Convenarum

name: Aquae Dacicae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C5 Aquae Dacicae

name: Aquae Helveticae
description: Aquae Helveticae was a Roman vicus on the river Limmat.

name: Aquae Herculis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 Aquae Herculis

name: Aquae Iasae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D3 Aquae Iasae

name: Aquae Onesiorum
description: Aquae Onesiorum (modern Bagnères-de-Luchon, France)

name: Aquae Originae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Aquae Originae

name: Aquae Passeris
description: Located in Etruria, Aquae Passeris was frequented in antiquity for its thermal springs, as noted by Martial. The site is included on the Tabula Peutingeriana, placing it on the road from Rome to Volsinii. Its location likely corresponds to that of Bacucco, a comune to the north of Viterbo.

name: Aquae Populoniae
description: Remains of an Etrusco-Roman thermal complex, dating from the third century B.C. to late antiquity, were documented between 2000 and 2003.

name: Aquae Rusellanae
description: A thermal site located near Roman Rusellae in regular use and reuse since at least Roman times. Its ancient name is not known. The first modern excavations and mapping efforts date to the 18th century of the modern era.

name: Aquae Segestanae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 B3 Aquae Segestanae

name: Aquae Segetae
description: An ancient settlement at modern Sceaux-du-Gâtinais in France (BAtlas 11 C4 Sceaux-du-Gâtinais)

name: Aquae Siccae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 Aquae Siccae

name: Aquae Sulis
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa and site of the Temple of Sulis Minerva.

name: Aquae Tauri
description: A thermal site in Etruria northeast of Centumcellae.

name: Aquae Terebellicae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D2 Aquae Terebellicae

name: Aquae Traianae?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Aquae Traianae?

name: Aquae Vescinae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Aquae Vescinae

name: Aquae?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 Aquae?

name: Aquas volaternas
description: A place named on the Peutinger map, possibly to be associated with modern Monte Cerboli, Italy.

name: Aque Ange
description: An ancient road station and/or spa mentioned on the Peutinger Map. It's precise location cannot be determined today; however, some scholars have suggested that it be identified with the modern Terme di Caronte at Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province.

name: Arc de Diane
description: The ruins of a Roman bath complex at Divona Cadurcorum.

name: Arieldela
description: A Roman site in modern Jordan, known today as ‘Ayn Gharandal. It includes remains of a late Roman fort and bathhouse.

name: Arriana
description: A Roman-era bathing establishment was identified at modern Arriana in Greece during the 1970s. The facility dates to the second century CE and was destroyed in the third century. Subsequently, the site served as a cemetery through the fourth century.

name: Asklepieion
description: The Asklepieion at Pergamon is located 3 km south of the Acropolis.

name: Bacino Termale Euganeo
description: A key element of a well-known area of thermal spas and springs already in use in Roman antiquity, located in modern Italy's Veneto region.

name: Bad Bertrich
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H2 Bad Bertrich

name: Bagni di Nerone
description: The so-called "Baths of Nero" are located near the Porta a Lucca in Pisa.

name: Balesmes-sur-Marne
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Balesmes-sur-Marne

name: balneum Caesaris
description: A bath complex known from the Severan marble plan and perhaps also referred to in the Historia Augusta.

name: Balneum Regis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C3 Balneum Regis

name: Bar Hill Fort Bathhouse
description: The Bar Hill Fort Bathhouse was built around 140 CE, at the same time as the Antonine wall, and is a long and narrow stone structure with latrines and two heated rooms.

name: Barbarathermen at Trier
description: Dating to the second century, the so-called "Barbara Baths" (Barbarathermen in German) are the largest Roman bath complex known north of the Alps. The bath complex was reused first as a Medieval castle and then as a seventeenth century Jesuit college. The ruins were designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier" UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.

name: Batanaia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B5 Batanaia

name: Bath at northwest foot of the Areopagus
description: A bathhouse located northwest of the Areopagus Hill and southwest of the Agora in Athens, first built in the late second century BC and remaining in use until the late sixth century AD.

name: Bath C3
description: Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block C3.

name: Bath E3 Phase 1
description: Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaced by Bath E3 Phase 2.

name: Bath E3 Phase 2
description: Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaces Bath E3 Phase 1.

name: Bath F3
description: Bath building originally occupying all of Block F3, located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos. Excavations reveal that the bath likely had two phases of renovation (one perhaps after 165 CE, and another around c. 200 CE), before it was destroyed by fire. Dura-Europos' amphitheater was built over the remains of Bath F3, and thus the amphitheater's dedicatory inscription, dated to 216 CE, serves as a terminus ante quem for the bath building.

name: Bath M7
description: Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block M7.

name: Bath-Gymnasium Complex at Sardis
description: Bath/gymnasium complex containing a palaestra and a synagogue that was developed in the 3rd century CE.

name: Baths at Cremna
description: Public baths at Cremna.

name: Baths of Antoninus at Carthage
description: The Baths of Antoninus at Carthage date to the middle of the second century and are the largest of the Roman public bath complexes in North Africa.

name: Baths of Caracalla (Albano Laziale)
description: Public baths generally dated to the reign of Caracalla.

name: Baths of Cilurnum
description: The Roman military bathhouse at Cilurnum.

name: Baths of Diocletian (Palmyra)
description: A public bath complex built at Palmyra toward the end of the second century CE and perhaps renovated during the reign of Diocletian.

name: Baths of Pozzo Pantaleo
description: A public bath (thermae) of the Roman Imperial period.

name: Baths of the Old Convent, Tharros
description: Roman baths at Tharros known as the "Baths of the Old Convent".

name: Baths of the Philosopher
description: The so-called "Baths of the Philosopher" or "Terme del Filosofo" in region V of the ancient city of Ostia take their modern sobriquet from a bust of Plotinus dating to the middle of the third century CE.

name: Bearsden Roman bath house
description: The remains of a stone-built Roman bath at Bearsden on the Antonine Wall.

name: Beauport Park Archaeological Site
description: The largest known Roman iron-making site in the Weald, with the remains of a substantial bath house.

name: Billingsgate Roman House and Baths
description: A Roman balneum attached either to a Roman townhouse or mansio.

name: Bothwellhaugh
description: The remains of a Roman legionary fort located in North Lanarkshire near Glasgow, Scotland, that was built in connection with the Antonine Wall (ca. A.D. 142-165).

name: Bourbon-l’Archambault
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I2 Bourbon-l’Archambault

name: Bourbonne-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Bourbonne-les-Bains

name: Brestovačka Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Brestovačka Banja

name: Buridava Roman Fort and Vicus
description: A Roman fort and associated structures at modern Stolniceni, Romania. An indigenous settlement was likely located a few kilometers to the north.

name: Buziaş
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D4 Buziaş

name: Byzantine Bath
description: A thirteenth/fourteenth century Byzantine bath at Thessalonica; listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.

name: Caldes de Montbui
description: An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Montbui.

name: Caldoval
description: A Roman settlement with public baths that was occupied from the later republican period until the fifth or sixth century CE.

name: Campo Valdés Roman Baths, Gijón
description: Roman baths dating from the first to fourth centuries.

name: Capitol Baths
description: A bath complex dating to the later second century that were subsequently repurposed as a Christian basilica.

name: Capreae (island)
description: An island of the Bay of Naples off the Sorrentine Peninsula that has, since the time of the Roman Republic, been a popular seaside resort area. The emperor Tiberius maintained twelve villas on the island.

name: Central Baths
description: A bath complex (IX, 4, 5-18) located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and Via di Nola that was built following the earthquake of AD 62.

name: Central Baths
description: A public bath complex at Herculaneum, dating to the first century A.D. It was subdivided into "male" and "female" sectors.

name: Chassenon Roman baths
description: The thermal baths of Chassenon are among the best preserved in the Gallo-Roman world. They are part of the ancient city of Cassinomagus.

name: Cortinal de los Baños
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cortinal de los Baños

name: Deir Abou Menas
description: An ancient Christian pilgrimage site that grew into an active town centered around the monastery complex devoted to Saint Menas who is said to have been buried there. The town reached its peak as the most visited pilgrimage site in Egypt during the sixth century AD.

name: Duntocher
description: Roman fortlet and fort on the Antonine Wall in West Dunbartonshire, Scotland.

name: Exeter Roman Bathhouse
description: A stone-built bathhouse at Exeter connected with the Neronian fortress.

name: Finca del Secretario
description: An archaeological site that comprises remains of an urban area of the Roman period ranging in date from the first to the fifth centuries. Among the complexes in evidence are a salting factory, a pottery works, and thermal baths.

name: Fontaines Salées
description: Salt springs continuously exploited from the Neolithic period through the early modern era. Also the site of a Roman bath complex.

name: Forum baths
description: A bath complex adjacent to Pompeii's forum that was built after 80 BC (Reg VII, Ins 5, 2, 5, 7, 24). The baths are publicly financed by the duovir Lucius Caesius and the aediles Caius Occius and Lucius Niraemius.

name: Freilichtmuseum Römerbad
description: A Roman bath complex located at Rottweil, Germany.

name: Gallo-Roman bath complex at Mackwiller
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex located at the modern town of Mackwiller in the French commune of Waldhambach.

name: Gallo-Roman baths
description: A suite of Roman-period public baths in Lyon, France. The site is located in the rue des Farges on the slope of the Fourvière hill. The baths may have been linked to the cult of Apollo.

name: Great City Baths at Salona
description: The so-called Great City Baths at Salona or Great Thermae were built in the second or beginning of third century A.D.

name: Great Southern baths at Conimbriga
description: The Roman baths at Conimbriga.

name: Hadrianic Baths
description: The largest of the public bath complexes at Aphrodisias dating to the early second century CE.

name: Hammam Beni Guecha
description: The so-called Hammam Beni Guecha is a ruined Roman spa located just north and east of the modern Algerian village of Chouara Torche (Beni Guecha municipality). The site may be associated with a road station recorded on the Peutinger Map: Fons Carmerata.

name: Hammath/Ammathous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Hammath/Ammathous

name: Harbour Thermae, Ephesos
description: A large bath complex adjacent to the ancient harbor of Ephesus that measures ca. 160 by 170 meters and was originally built in the late first century CE.

name: Hellenistic Balaneion, Amathous
description: A circular public bath building dating to the Hellenistic period in the agora of Amathous.

name: Hierapolis/Pamukkale
description: Hierapolis (modern Pamukkale in Turkey's Denizli province) was a major ancient city in Phrygia located at the site of hot springs in southwestern Anatolia. The site was designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.

name: House of the Hotel
description: The so-called House of the Hotel (Casa dell'Albergo) is an opulent Augustan-period domus that occupies the bulk of the southern end of Insula III at Herculaneum.

name: Hydata Lesitana
description: An ancient thermal site with both nuragic and Roman remains. Now the community of Benettuti on the island of Sardinia.

name: Jewry Wall
description: The so-called "Jewry Wall" is a substantial segment of surviving Roman masonry at Ratae.

name: Kastell Op de Hoge Woerd
description: A Roman auxiliary fort with associated vicus and port at modern Vleuten-De Meern in the Dutch province of Utrecht.

name: Kastellbad Schirenhof
description: Extensive Roman bathhouse associated with the Roman fort at modern Schirenhof in the German municipality of Schwäbisch Gmünd, Baden-Württemberg.

name: Kuršumlijska Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Kuršumlijska Banja

name: Le Caldane
description: Ancient thermal baths in use from the Etrusco-Roman period onward.

name: Leontos Kome
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Leontos Kome

name: Les Rubricaire (St-Gemmes-le-Robert)
description: A Roman fort and tower in the modern French commune of St-Gemmes-le-Robert (Pays de la Loire).

name: Menthon-St-Bernard
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Menthon-St-Bernard

name: Military Bath House at Vindolanda
description: One of two Roman Bath Houses known at Vindolanda.

name: Minor baths of Itálica
description: The Minor baths of Itálica date to the reign of Trajan. Under Hadrian, the bath complex was expanded to an area of some 1,500 m².

name: Montoulieu-Saint-Bernard
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex last occupied during the fourth century A.D.

name: Nopigeia
description: Remnants of an ancient settlement have been identified south and east of the modern beach village of Nopigeia on Crete that date to at least the 5th century BCE. The church of Agios Georgios Nopigion, which is located between Nopigia and Drapania, was built on the remains of a Roman-era bath complex. A single, late source (Aelian) provides the name Mithymna, which may have belonged to this settlement. Bronze Age remains have also been identified in the vicinity.

name: Prestatyn
description: The modern-day site of a Romano-British settlement dating from first and second centuries A.D.

name: Public Bath of Pella
description: The Public Bath at Pella is a bathing complex constructed and continuously renovated between the 4th and the 1st centuries BCE.

name: Public baths at Saepinum
description: The public baths at Saepinum are located along the decumanus and date to the first and second centuries.

name: Ravenglass Roman Bath House
description: The Ravenglass Roman Bath House, also known as "Walls Castle", is linked to the second-century CE fort at Glannoventa. English Heritage dates the site ca. 130 and deems it one of the tallest surviving Roman ruins in Britain.

name: Ribchester Roman Bath House
description: Roman baths at Ribchester dating ca. 100 CE.

name: Roman bath at Alange
description: A thermal spring where a Roman bath complex was first built in the third century A.D.

name: Roman bath at Fregellae
description: A two-phase complex of public baths at Fregellae dating to the third and second centuries BCE represents an important point in the transition from "Greek" bathing culture to "Roman" bathing culture.

name: Roman bath at Lugdunum Convenarum
description: The Roman bath at Lugdunum Convenarum is part of the second urban nucleus found at the foot of the Fourvière hill.

name: Roman bath at Volaterrae
description: A bath complex located adjacent to the theater of Volaterrae.

name: Roman bath house, Callendar Park, Falkirk
description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in Callendar Park, Falkirk in 1980.

name: Roman Bath House, Castleford
description: The Roman Bath House, Castleford, consists of the now-buried archaeological remains of a Roman military bath house that was built in the mid to late AD 80s. The site was excavated in 1978. The ancient bath was situated within the site of a former defensible annex that was constructed to the north of Castleford Roman fort.

name: Roman Bath House, Prestatyn
description: At the Prestatyn Roman Site, a bath complex was identified via excavation (1934-7; 1981). The bathhouse measures approximately 11.7 by 4.5 meters.

name: Roman Bath-House at Little Chart, Kent
description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in a field to the west of the village of Little Chart, Kent in 1942. These remains included fragments of mosaic pavement. Excavations in 1942 and later in 1947 identified the site as a Roman bathing complex.

name: Roman Baths (Bath)
description: Bathhouse complex in Aquae Sulis, modern-day Bath. This well-preserved site is now a museum and the bathing complex itself is below modern street-level.

name: Roman Baths (Parium)
description: Public baths at Parium used from the second century BCE and renovated several times in the late Roman and Byzantine periods, likely being used until at least the seventh century CE.

name: Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas
description: The Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas date to the third century CE. They were located outside of the ancient city center in an area that has been categorized as industrial. Their exact nature is difficult to determine.

name: Roman baths at Carnuntum
description: The Roman baths at Carnuntum.

name: Roman baths at Fiesole
description: The Roman baths at Fiesole date to the time of Sulla in the first century B.C.

name: Roman baths at Fundi
description: Imperial baths located on Fundi's decumanus maximus.

name: Roman baths at Gaujac
description: The Roman baths at Gaujac were begun ca. A.D. 20.

name: Roman baths at Hydata Hypsitana/Forum Traiani
description: The Roman baths Fordongianus dates to the first through third centuries.

name: Roman baths at Oescus
description: The Roman baths at Oescus are dated to the middle of the third century.

name: Roman baths at Sagalassos
description: The Roman baths at Sagalassos date to the early part of the first century A.D.

name: Roman baths at Sexi/Saxetanum
description: The Roman baths at Sexi/Saxetanum date to the first century.

name: Roman baths at Trier
description: The Imperial Baths at Trier (also referred to as "Kaiserthermen") are a large Roman bath complex in Trier, Germany. The site was designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier", a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The complex dates to the fourth century A.D.

name: Roman baths at Vertillum
description: The remains of baths at the Gallo-Roman site of Vertillum.

name: Roman baths of Acinipo
description: The Roman baths of Acinipo date to the first century B.C.

name: Roman baths of Aquinum
description: Roman baths at Aquinum.

name: Roman Baths of Como
description: The Roman Baths of Como were in use from the later first century until the third century CE.

name: Roman baths of Coriovallum
description: The site of Coriovallum is known for its well-preserved bath complex. These baths were first built ca. A.D. 40 and then re-built during the reign of Hadrian. They remained in use until the fourth century.

name: Roman baths of Ebora
description: The Roman baths of Ebora were in use during the second and third centuries. The archaeological site was discovered in 1987.

name: Roman baths of Taormina
description: The Roman baths of Taormina are built atop a public building of the Hellenistic period. The Roman baths originally fronted on the forum and were expanded in the second century.

name: Roman baths, Carsulae
description: Baths of the early imperial period at Carsulae.

name: Roman baths, Malvindi
description: The remains of Roman baths located in the ager of Mesagne.

name: Roman baths, Spoleto
description: Roman baths located at Spoletium.

name: Roman Thermae (Varna)
description: A monumental bath complex constructed in the 2nd c. CE in modern Varna, ancient Odessus.

name: Roman Villa at Għajn Tuffieħa, Malta
description: Ruins at this ancient site include a Roman-era bath complex and extensive mosaics. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Romano-British Bath House
description: A bathhouse located at Orpington, Kent was excavated between 1971 and 1975. The site flourished between ca. 270 and 400 CE. Its post-abandonment phase includes Anglo-Saxon graves of the fifth and sixth centuries.

name: Royat Roman Baths
description: Ruins of Roman baths at Royat, France, that were listed as a registered monument in 1889.

name: S. Calogero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F2 S. Calogero

name: S. Casciano dei Bagni
description: The village of San Casciano dei Bagni, located south of Siena, Italy, has been frequented since at least the first millennium BCE on account of its thermal springs with supposedly healing properties.

name: San Basilio
description: A Roman bath complex that was reused first as a monastic complex and then as the Medieval church of San Basilio.

name: Sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios
description: The sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios on Mt. Lykaion.

name: Santa Maria Nova
description: A multi-period site with medieval and modern remains atop Roman levels, including a medieval farmhouse built atop a Roman cistern. Excavations revealed multi-phase evidence, including a bath building decorated with mosaics. The structures may have been related to the nearby Villa dei Quintilli.

name: Sarno Baths
description: The so-called "Sarno Baths" (Regio VIII, Insula 2, nos. 17–21) at Pompeii resulted from the merger and remodeling of two atrium houses, no. 18 and no. 21. There is considerable debate in the scholarly literature as to the architectural phasing.

name: Sasso Pisano
description: A sacred Etruscan thermal site.

name: Sauve Pre-Roman oppidum
description: A pre-Roman and Gallo-Roman oppidum in the territory of the Volcae Arecomici.

name: Segontium: Roman Bath House
description: The Roman Bath House at Segontium.

name: Shadwell Roman Bathhouse
description: A second to fourth century A.D. Romano-British bathhouse that was excavated in 2002. Findspot of RIB 3012.

name: Siminina?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Siminina?

name: So-called Roman Baths in Apollonia
description: An architectural complex near the sea in Apollonia. It went through different stages and alterations between, roughly, the fourth century B.C. and the seventh century A.D. It was probably used as a private villa, then as public baths and, perhaps, as cisterns in later years.

name: Sokobanja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Sokobanja

name: Souk el Arba
description: A Roman camp with baths and associated settlement were identified at modern Souk El Arbaa in Morocco in the late 19th and 20th centuries on high ground occupied by a qubba called "Sidi Aïssa". Although ancient structures and settlement extent identified previously are not visible in modern satellite imagery, the site -- bisected by a rail line predating 1941 and occupied by a cemetery and more recent structures -- can still be correlated closely with previously published photographs and plans. Recent scholarly opinion diverges from that reflected in the Barrington Atlas to lean toward identifying this site with ancient (Vo)psiciana (q.v.), attested by Ptolemy, the Antonine Itinerary, and the Ravenna Cosmographer.

name: South-Western Baths
description: A bath complex at Herculaneum that was discovered in the excavations of the so-called "Insula Occidentalis". It was not in use as a bath at the time of the 79 CE eruption as it had been repurposed as a ship-shed.

name: Stabian Baths
description: The Stabian Baths (VII, 1, 8) and (VII,2) are located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and the Via dell'Abbondanza. They are the oldest baths in Pompeii.

name: Suburban Baths
description: A first century BC bath complex in Pompeii built against the walls near the Porta Marina

name: Suessatium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 C3 Suessatium

name: Termas Menores de Mura
description: A Roman bath complex, located in the northeast part of the modern town of Liria in Spain's Valencia province.

name: Termas romanas
description: One of the 19 Roman bath complexes identified in Mérida.

name: Termas romanas de las Bóvedas
description: The Roman Baths of Las Bóvedas are near the mouth of the Guadalmina River.

name: Termas romanas de Santa María
description: Remains of Roman baths at Antequera in Spain.

name: Termas Romanas do Alto da Cividade
description: A monumental public bath (thermae) at Braga, dating from the first to third centuries CE.

name: Terme Achilliane
description: Thermal baths at Catania dating to the fourth and fifth centuries CE. The archaeological remains are located near the present-day Piazza del Duomo of Catania.

name: Terme Aquae Passeris
description: A Roman bath located at Aquae Passeris.

name: Terme del Nuotatore
description: The so-called "Baths of the Swimmer" or "Terme del Nuotatore" (V,X,3) in region 5 of the ancient city of Ostia takes its modern name from a partially preserved black-and-white mosaic pavement. These baths were built in the late first century CE during the reign of Domitian. The complex was rebuilt during the Antonine period. The complex remained in use until ca. 250. The site was first excavated during 1938-1942. Andrea Carandini directed another campaign of excavation from 1966 to 1975.

name: Terme della Regina
description: Terme della Regina, Tuscania (Italy).

name: Terme della Trinacria
description: The so-called Terme della Trinacria in Regio III of Ostia Antica were built in the Hadrianic period at the same time as the Temple of Serapis (III,XVII,4). The modern toponym is derived from a triskelion included in a mosaic decoration.

name: Terme di Caronte
description: Mineral springs located at modern Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province. There is some archaeological evidence for exploitation of the springs in antiquity, but the current spa complex is completely modern in date. Some scholars have suggested that the Terme di Caronte may correspond to the "Aquae angae" mentioned on the Peutinger Map.

name: Terme di Cavascura
description: A site of ancient thermal baths.

name: Terme Erculee
description: The largest known bath complex of Roman Mediolanum, built in the late third to early fourth centuries CE.

name: Terme Romane
description: Roman baths of ancient Luceria

name: Terme romane (Regium)
description: The excavations carried out following the earthquake of 1908 revealed a number of baths dating to the early centuries of the Roman Imperial period.

name: Terme Romane con Mosaici
description: These Roman baths in modern Bevagna, Italy, date to the second century CE and were rediscovered in 1891.

name: Terme Romane, Ferento
description: The Roman baths of ancient Ferentium are located adjacent to the Roman theater.

name: Thermae Antoninianae
description: The Thermae Antoninianae, commonly known as the Baths of Caracalla, were built in Rome between AD 212 and 216.

name: Thermae Diocletiani
description: Public baths near the Viminal Hill in Rome constructed under Diocletian from 298-306 CE.

name: Thermae Helenianae
description: The Thermae Helenianae or Thermae Helenae was a thermal complex located in the Horti Spei Veteris, also referred to as the Horti Variani. The baths were situated near the arcus Neroniani adjacent to the Amphitheatrum Castrense. As Helena, mother of Constantine I, restored them following a fire in 323-326, they are named for her.

name: Thermae Novati
description: Thermae Novati, ancient baths of the Vicus Patricius that were located in the vicinity of S. Pudenziana.

name: Thermae of Hippo Regius
description: The northern bath complex of Hippo Regius.

name: Thermae Selinuntinae/Aquae Larodes
description: The site of thermal spings, founded as a sub-colony of Selinus in the seventh century B.C. Thought to be in the area of modern Sciacca.

name: Thermae Titi (Tïtianae)
description: Thermae Titi (Tïtianae), or Baths of Titus, were public baths built at Rome in 81 CE under the emperor Titus. Located at the foot of the Esquiline Hill, the baths occupy part of the Domus Aurea of Nero. The Baths of Trajan were constructed immediately adjacent to the Baths of Titus in the early second century.

name: Thermae Traiani
description: A monumental public bath of ancient Rome that was built between 104 and 109. The architect Apollodorus of Damascus built the baths for Trajan.

name: Thermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Thermai

name: Thermai Theseos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A4 Thermai Theseos

name: Thermes de Cluny
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex of the third century CE at Lutetia (modern Paris).

name: Thermes des Lutteurs
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex identified in 1991 at Saint-Romain-en-Galt that originally covered some 8 ha.

name: Thermes gallo-romains, Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: A Gallo-Roman bath complex at Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

name: Thermes romains de Cimiez
description: The ancient Roman thermal baths of Cemenelum.

name: Tomen y Mur Roman bath house
description: A bath complex associated with the first-century fort at Tomen y Mur.

name: Unnamed religious complex
description: Referred to today as "Gisacum", this vast Gallo-Roman religious complex (with major spa), is located largely in the French commune of Le Vieil Evreux.

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed bath (SW Siminina?)

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Untitled
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

name: Usays
description: Vogüé (1868 and 1877) assumed that the large, square fort and bath facilities at the extinct Jebel Seis volcano in Syria were Roman (whence Dussaud 1927 whence BAtlas); however, excavations in the 1960s demonstrated that the fortification and other structures constitute an Umayyad fortified palace and associated settlement built in the late 7th to early 8th centuries CE, incorporating some elements of a pre-existing settlement, fort and/or monastery dating to the 6th century CE. There is no evidence for activity at the site in the early Roman empire.

name: Varignano Vecchio
description: A villa maritima in eastern Liguria, inhabited from ca. 100 BC to the fifth century AD.

name: Vieil-Évreux Roman Baths
description: The Vieil-Évreux Roman Baths of the second and third centuries A.D. have four architectural phases that have been identified.

name: Villa dei Venulei - Terme Romane
description: A first-century A.D. Roman villa and bath complex located in Etruria.

name: Vrnjačka Banja
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 C6 Vrnjačka Banja

name: Welwyn
description: The remains of Roman baths are preserved at Welwyn, where a Romano-British roadside settlement was located near a crossing of the river Mimram.

name: "Ponte Sanguinario" at Spoletium
description: The "Ponte Sanguinario" at Spoleto is a late republican to early imperial Roman bridge. The remains of the bridge are now below street level in the Piazza della Vittoria, not far from the Porta San Gregorio.

name: "Trajan's Bridge" at Drobeta-Turnu Severin
description: A Trajanic bridge spanning the Danube River at Drobeta-Turnu Severin.

name: (H)Alesion Pedion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C2 (H)Alesion Pedion

name: (Iulia) Myrtilis
description: An ancient settlement at modern Mértola (Portugal) on the west bank of the Guadiana river (ancient Anas). The town is mentioned in Roman sources, but most of the monumental archaeology dates to the Muslim period or later (including a number of large piers previously misidentified as a Roman bridge).

name: A Ponte da Xira
description: A Roman bridge in the northwestern Iberian peninsula that crosses the río Valiñas.

name: Afrin bridge
description: An ancient bridge on the Afrin river.

name: Ain Diwar
description: A ruined Roman bridge near Ain Diwar, Syria, that once crossed the Tigris river.

name: Albarregas Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge with origins in the first century B.C. that crosses the Albarregas River, a tributary of the Guadiana River. The bridge was been declared a Site of Cultural Interest in 1912 and named a part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Mérida Archaeological Ensemble" in 1993.

name: Alcántara Bridge
description: A six-span stone bridge over the Tagus river, also known as the Puente Trajan.

name: Alebaece Reiorum Apollinarium (Bridge)
description: A Roman bridge at Alebaece Reiorum Apollinarium (modern Riez).

name: Algund
description: A bridge on the Via Claudia Augusta crossing the Adige river near Maiensis Statio.

name: Aluenda Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge in the modern Barranco de Aluenda (Aluenda Gorge), which runs down from the northwest to the modern village of Aluenda in Spain's Zaragoza province (Aragon). This bridge was east of ancient Bilbilis on the Roman road to Caesaraugusta.

name: Ancient bridge on Via Egnatia at modern Arisvi
description: An ancient bridge was reported at modern Arisvi, crossing the river Lissos, in documents in the general state archive of Komotini 1945-1961, which still seems to be known to local community and described as Roman. A modern bridge stands in its place today.

name: Arapsu Bridge
description: A Roman bridge near Antalya, Turkey.

name: Arkadiko Bridge
description: An arched Bronze Age bridge near the Mycenaean center of Tiryns. The bridge is located along an ancient highway connecting Tiryns to Epidauros.

name: Ascoli Satriano Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge crossing the Carapelle stream in the territory of Ascoli Satriano.

name: Band-e Kaisar
description: A third century AD Roman bridge located near Shushtar, Iran.

name: Becilla de Valderaduey Roman bridge
description: A bridge that crosses the Valderaduey river and that has traditionally been considered to be of Roman origin, although it may in fact be more recent in date. The ruins are found in the municipality of Becilla de Valderaduey in the Province of Valladolid, of the autonomous community of Castilla y León, Spain.

name: Bridge
description: Within the Villa Neronis

name: Bridge at Abodiacum
description: An ancient bridge over the Licca river, located at Abodiacum.

name: Bridge at Aspendos
description: An unnamed bridge over Eurymedon fl.

name: Bridge at Augusta Vindelicum
description: An ancient bridge over the Licca river, located at Augusta Vindelicum.

name: Bridge at Brugnato
description: A Medieval bridge of Roman origins on the Vara river.

name: Bridge at Eleonas
description: Excavations of the metro train line exposed piers of an ancient bridge across the Kephissos.

name: Bridge of Mantible
description: The Bridge of Mantible (also known as Puente de Mantible) crosses the Ebro river northwest of Vareia. It is a Roman segmental arch bridge that likely dates to the second century AD.

name: Bridge on Via Cassia near *Volsinii Veteres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C3

name: Bridge over Fosso di Acqua Rossa
description: The remains of a Roman bridge that once carried a road over the Fosso di Acqua Rossa. Ward-Perkins posits a date of the second or first century BCE based on comparanda in the vicinity.

name: Bridge over Gibralzo Arroyo
description: An ancient bridge located southwest of Turgalium, over Gibralzo Arroyo.

name: Calamocha Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the the modern Jiloca river at Calamocha, Teruel province, Aragon, Spain.

name: Caneiras do Roxo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B3 Caneiras do Roxo

name: Cangas de Onís
description: A Medieval bridge built by Alfonso XI of Castile and León that possibly rests on earlier Roman foundations.

name: Cáparra Roman bridge
description: An unnamed bridge over the river Caparra, just north of ancient Capera, which is west-southwest of modern Casablanca (Guijo de Granadilla, Caceres, Extremadura, Spain)

name: Capela de S. Águeda
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Capela de S. Águeda

name: Casal da Várzea
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Casal da Várzea

name: Casal do Mouchão
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Casal do Mouchão

name: Casas de Don Antonio Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge spanned the modern Río Ayuela at what is now the south side of the Spanish village of Casas de Don Antonio. Scholars believe that the Roman road station "Ad Sorores" was located somewhere immediately north of this bridge.

name: Castropodame
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 E2 Castropodame

name: Catribana Roman bridge
description: A restored (2019) bridge located near Sintra, Portugal.

name: Cinco Puentes
description: A Roman bridge

name: Cirauqui
description: Remains of a Roman bridge and paved road.

name: Cobos Roman bridge
description: A bridge with Roman origins across the río Adaja.

name: Constantine's Bridge (Mysia)
description: A Roman bridge crossing the Rhyndakos river that was built after AD 258.

name: Cyclopean Bridge of Agios Ioannis
description: The Cyclopean Bridge of Agios Ioannis is located one kilometer south of the citadel of Mycenae.

name: Damiya Roman Bridge
description: Sited at an ancient crossing-point over the river Jordan, between Neapolis and Philadelpheia, a Roman-era bridge is thought to have been one of many. The modern bridge and its antecedents are often referred to as the "Damia Bridge".

name: Decimomannu Roman bridge
description: The remains of a stone, arched Roman bridge at Decimomannu, to the west of Assemini, Sardinia. Three of the original six arches survive today. The bridge may originally have been as much as 160 meters in length. R.J. Rowland considered it to be of Iulio-Claudian date.

name: Dicle Bridge
description: An historic bridge built across the Tigris river by Sancaroğlu Ubeydoğlu Yusuf in AD 1065.

name: El Kantara Bridge
description: A restored Roman bridge at ancient Ad Calceum Herculis (modern el Kantara, Algeria)

name: Eleutherna Bridge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C2 unnamed bridge (Eleutherna)

name: Enza Roman Bridge
description: An unnamed Roman bridge on the Via Aemilia over the ancient Incia river. Located near modern S. Ilario d’Enza in Italy.

name: Euripos
description: The strait between Euboea and Boeotia, located where the modern Greek city of Chalkida now stands. A bridge connected the land masses in antiquity.

name: Firuzabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Firuzabad

name: Girsu Bridge
description: The archaeological site of the Girsu Bridge, located in modern Iraq's Dhi Qar governorate, was identified in 1920 and has been dated to the third millennium BCE.

name: Golmayo Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge at modern Golmayo in Spain's Soria province (Castile and León), crossing the modern river of the same name. The modern N-122 highway still makes use of this originally Roman bridge.

name: Handere bridge
description: A bridge on the road from to Egin and Harput.

name: Hannibal's Bridge (Rapallo)
description: A ruined bridge at Rapallo, Italy, that the local toponymastic tradition links to the third-century BCE military operations of the Carthaginian commander Hannibal.

name: Healam Bridge Roman Fort
description: The site of a Roman fort and an associated vicus along Dere Street, now buried and bisected by the modern A1.

name: Horta do Vinagre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Horta do Vinagre

name: Iustiniani Pons
description: The Bridge of Justinian or Sangarius Bridge is a Late Roman bridge built by the emperor Justinian I across the Sangarius river. The bridge's original span was ca. 430 m.

name: Iustiniani/S. Callinici Pons
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Iustiniani/S. Callinici Pons

name: Kalo Pegadi (bridge)
description: On Hiera Hodos at Kopros.

name: Karamagara Bridge
description: A Roman-era bridge spanning the Arabkir Çay south of Hastek Kale in Turkey. Now submerged and inaccessible.

name: Kemer bridge
description: A Roman segmental arch bridge over the Xanthos fl. near the ancient city of Xanthos in Lycia.

name: Kiz Bridge
description: A historical bridge near the Mianeh in East Azerbaijan partially destroyed in 1946.

name: Kleidi Roman bridge (Emathia)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 C3 unnamed bridge (Kleidi, between Methone and Thessalonica)

name: Le Pouzin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Le Pouzin

name: Limyra Bridge
description: A late-Roman bridge, ancient name unknown, located near the ancient city of Limyra in modern Turkey.

name: Luco de Jiloca Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the the modern Pancrudo river south of Luco de Jiloca in Teruel province, Aragon, Spain. The bridge crosses the river just below the modern Lechago dam, less than 100m before the Pancrudo's confluence with the Jiloca.

name: Lurs Roman Bridge
description: The Roman Bridge of Lurs is located to the northeast of Alaunium (modern Lurs, France). It was a bridge of the ancient Via Domitia.

name: Maalmetein Roman Bridge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C1 unnamed bridge (Maameltein, N Palaibyblos)

name: Magliana Vecchia
description: "Magliana Vecchia" is the name of the 40th urban administrative zone of the Comune di Roma. It is located to the southwest of the city center and straddles the Grande Raccordo Anulare (Ring Road) line that circles the city. The compilers of the Barrington Atlas noted that this place includes a "bridge" and a "settlement". In terms of archaeological remains, numerous sites have been identified in and around Magliana Vecchia, including a Roman bridge and several villas.

name: Malabadi Bridge
description: The Malabadi Bridge spans the Batman River in southeastern Turkey. Construction began ca. 1146/47 CE and continued until 1154.

name: Masseria Ponte
description: Roman bridge, Masseria Ponte

name: Mélas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Mélas

name: Nages Roman Bridge
description: An unnamed Roman bridge over the modern river Rhôny. It is located in the modern Nages-et-Solorgues commune in France's Gard department.

name: Negotin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Negotin

name: Niebla Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge at Ilipoula (modern Niebla), spanning the Iberos/Luxia river (modern Rio Tinto).

name: Oenoanda bridge
description: An Ottoman bridge at Oinoanda on the Xanthos river. A Latin inscription found nearby suggests an earlier Roman bridge existed at the site.

name: Pedroches Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the arroyo Pedroches near Corduba, Spain. The bridge lies along the ancient road to Augusta Emerita.

name: Penkalas Bridge
description: A second-century A.D. Roman bridge across the Penkalas river ( Kocaçay) in Aezani, Asia Minor.

name: Petra
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B4 Petra

name: Philippi Pons
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Philippi Pons

name: Piercebridge Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge that carried Dere Street across the River Tees in northern England.

name: Pietra Dell’Oglio (bridge)
description: A Roman bridge crossing the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river at the border between the modern Italian provinces of Campania and Basilicata. The bridge is one of two candidate identifications for the "Pons Aufidi" shown on the Peutinger map (the Ponte S. Venere), a possibility bolstered by recent comparative archaeological studies of the two bridges. The Pietra Dell'Oglio remains in use today for the Strada Comunale Fs di Monteverde, which connects to the Strada Statale 401 dir dell'Alto Ofanto e del Vulture at the southeast end of the bridge.

name: Pizões
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Pizões

name: Pol-e Dokhtar
description: Pol-e Dokhtar (also Romanized as Pol Dokhtar and Pul-i-Dukhtar) is a city in the Lorestan Province of Iran. The ruins of a Sasanian bridge over the Kashkan River are still visible today a few kilometers north of the city; that structure was part of a Sasanian Royal road that connected the Persian capitals Istakhr and Bishapur with towns in central and northern Mesopotamia. In 1992, a horde of ancient objects were found in the nearby Cave Kalmakareh (also referred to as Cave Kalmakarra).

name: Pons Aelius
description: The Pons Aelius or Aelian Bridge (now Ponte Sant'Angelo) was completed by Hadrian in AD 134 to span the Tiber opposite the new mausoleum of the emperor.

name: Pons Aemilius
description: The Pons Aemilius was the first stone bridge across the Tiber river at Rome (241 B.C.). Its remains are known today as the 'Ponte Rotto'.

name: Pons Apollinis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 A1 Pons Apollinis

name: Pons Aufidi
description: A Roman bridge (and possibly coincident road station) on the Via Appia over the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river in Italy, listed on the Peutinger map and in the Antonine Itinerary. Traditionally it has been identified with a bridge known today as Ponte S. Venere, but recently archaeological work has cast doubt on this view and some scholars now favor an identification with the Ponte romano Pietra dell'Oglio, which lies about 15 miles further south and can be securely dated to the Roman period.

name: Pons Aurelius
description: The Pons Aurelius is known only from late Roman documents (fourth and fifth centuries CE) and is most likely the same bridge known as pons Antoninus in the early Middle Ages. The present-day Ponte Sisto (built in the fifteenth century) reused some of the ancient Roman bridge piers.

name: Pons Auruncus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Auruncus

name: Pons Campanus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Campanus

name: Pons Cestius
description: A Roman stone bridge on the Tiber river, spanning the river channel from the Tiber Island westward to the Transtiberim (Trastevere). The original bridge dates to the first century BC.

name: Pons Chabinae
description: A Roman bridge across the Cendere Çayı that lies west of Arsameia pros Nymphaio (modern Eskikale in Turkey's Adıyaman Province).

name: Pons Constantini
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B5 Pons Constantini

name: Pons Dubris
description: An ancient settlement and bridge at the modern village of Pontoux (Saône-et-Loire department, France).

name: Pons Fabricius
description: The Pons Fabricius (sometimes referred to as the Ponte dei Quattro Capi) is a first-century BC bridge in Rome that spans part of the Tiber River from the area of the Circus Flaminius to the Tiber Island.

name: Pons Mulvius
description: Pons Mulvius (the Milvian Bridge) is a Roman bridge over the Tiber River. The original phase of the bridge dates to the consulship of Gaius Claudius Nero in 206 B.C. The bridge is most famous as the site of a decisive battle between Maxentius and Constantine I fought in A.D. 312.

name: Pons Neronianus
description: A bridge across the Tiber river built either by Caius or Nero in order to connect the western part of the Campus Martius with the Campus Vaticanus.

name: Pons Probi
description: The Pons Probi across the Tiber is attested in the Notitia and, according to Platner and Ashby, was likely built under Emperor Probus in the third century AD.

name: Pons Servili
description: A bridge (and associated mutatio or other road station) listed on the Peutinger Map. Hammond 1974 (whence BAtlas) localizes the bridge in the modern "Fusha e Kododeshit" plain in Albania, located west of modern Urakë, which he identifies with a mansio named Claudanon listed in the ItBurd. Bitrakova-Grozdanova 1994 would place the bridge at or very near Urakë and Claudanon in the vicinity of moden Radožda in Macedonia.

name: Pons Sublicius
description: The Pons Sublicius is traditionally held to be the earliest Roman bridge across the river Tiber. The textual tradition assigns its construction to the king Ancus Marcius in 642 B.C. It crossed the Tiber River near the Forum Boarium just downstream from Tiber Island.

name: Pons Tirenus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Tirenus

name: Pont d'Ambroix
description: A bridge over the Vidourle and road station on the Via Domitia.

name: Pont du Diable (Céret)
description: A Medieval bridge spanning the Tech river that dates between 1321 and 1341.

name: Pont du Roi
description: A Roman bridge in the modern French commune of Boucieu-le-Roi (Ardèche department).

name: Pont Flavien
description: A Roman bridge that carries the Via Julia Augusta across the Touloubre river.

name: Pont gaulois dit de Sainte-Catherine
description: The Gallic bridge of Sainte-Catherine is located at Kergonan, between the towns of Plounévézel (Finistère) and Treffrin (Côtes-d'Armor), in the Brittany region. It may date to the seventh century.

name: Pont Julien
description: A bridge that spans the river known as the Cavalon (French), once part of the Via Domitia.

name: Pont Romà
description: A Roman bridge on the island of Majorca (Pollença, Mallorca, Spain).

name: Pont Serme
description: A Roman bridge on the Via Domitia.

name: Pont-Saint-Martin (bridge)
description: A Roman segmental arch bridge in the Aosta Valley in Italy that dates to the first century BC. The bridge spans 31.4 m across the Betuctelum to the NNW of Eporedia.

name: Ponte Acque Alte
description: A Roman bridge along the Via Appia near Cisterna di Latina.

name: Ponte Altinate
description: A late Republican Roman segmented arch bridge in Padua, Italy.

name: Ponte Amato
description: The bridge known as Ponte Amato carries the Via Praenestina near Gallicano nel Lazio.

name: Ponte Apollosa
description: Ponte Apollosa, a bridge of the Via Appia that may have been restored by Septimius Severus in 198 CE.

name: Ponte Brabaciera
description: A second-century Roman bridge on the Riu Brabaciera.

name: Ponte Buriano
description: The bridge known as Ponte Buriano is located at the hamlet of Ponte a Buriano, Italy. The bridge spans the Arno River and dates initially from 1277.

name: Ponte Calamone
description: A Roman bridge near Narnia that was built over the river Nera ca. 27 B.C.

name: Ponte Caldaro
description: A Roman bridge of the Via Flaminia.

name: Ponte Camillario
description: A Roman bridge on the Via Cassia.

name: Ponte Ceccara
description: The Ponte Ceccara, also referred to as the ponte di Terra, is a Republican period bridge located on the fosso di Passerano along the Via Praenestina. It is located within the modern comune of Zagarolo, some 30.5 km southeast of the center of Rome.

name: Ponte Corvo
description: A Roman segmental arch bridge across the Bacchiglione river in Padua, Italy.

name: Ponte d'Arli
description: A Roman bridge along the Via Salaria near Ascoli Piceno.

name: Ponte d'Augusto
description: A Iulio-Claudian bridge located in Ariminum.

name: Ponte da Isna
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Ponte da Isna

name: Ponte da Ladeira dos Envendos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte da Ladeira dos Envendos

name: Ponte da Marinha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A2 Ponte da Marinha

name: Ponte da Ribeira das Razas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ponte da Ribeira das Razas

name: Ponte da Sancha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B1 Ponte da Sancha

name: Ponte da Senhora da Azenha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ponte da Senhora da Azenha

name: Ponte de Nossa Senhora da Guia
description: A first-century Roman bridge across the Rio Lima at Ponte de Lima that was rebuilt in part in the fourteenth century.

name: Ponte de Rubiaes
description: A Roman bridge located by the Rio Coura.

name: Ponte de Sor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte de Sor

name: Ponte de Tôr
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Ponte de Tôr

name: Ponte de Trajano
description: The Roman Bridge of Chaves is also referred to as the Bridge of Trajan. It is dated to the late first or early second century CE.

name: Ponte dei Saraceni, Adrano
description: A bridge over the Simeto River with ancient origins, the so-called Ponte dei Saraceni has been rebuilt several times, including after the 1693 Sicily earthquake.

name: Ponte del Diavolo
description: A bridge in the territory of Blera known as the "Devil's Bridge".

name: Ponte del Diavolo
description: Site of an ancient Roman bridge across the Raganello near Civita. The ancient bridge was largely rebuilt in 2005.

name: Ponte del Gran Caso
description: A Roman bridge across the Torrente Gran Caso located south of Ascoli Piceno.

name: Ponte del Toro
description: A Roman arched bridge on the Via Flaminia near modern Terni.

name: Ponte del Viceré de Ribera
description: The bridge known as the Ponte del Viceré de Ribera crosses the Fosso di Sant'Andrea on the line of the Via Appia. The viceroy of Naples, one Parefàn de Ribera, renovated the bridge in 1568. During the Second World War, the bridge was bombed and was eventually restored in 2005.

name: Ponte dell'Abbadia
description: An ancient bridge over the Fiora river (the ancient Armenta) near Vulci. Ancient name unknown.

name: Ponte dell’Acqua Nera
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 38 (bridge (Ponte dell’Acqua Nera))

name: Ponte dell'Ospedalato
description: A Roman bridge located near Praeneste.

name: Ponte della Concordia, Fossombrone
description: A bridge over the Metauro River built initially in the thirteenth century. The original bridge was destroyed in 1765 and rebuilt in 1782. The bridge was again destroyed in 1944 and was rebuilt in 1946.

name: Ponte della Maddalena
description: A Medieval bridge across the Serchio River. The bridge was built ca. 1080-1100 and was renovated ca. 1300. It is located along the Via Francigena.

name: Ponte della Rocca, Blera
description: The so-called Ponte della Rocca located on the Via Clodia at Blera is situated adjacent to the Necropolis of San Giovenale and Terrone. Some sources suggest an original date in the third or second centuries BCE.

name: Ponte delle Chianche, Buonalbergo
description: A Roman bridge of the second century A.D. along the Via Traiana that crosses the torrente Buonalbergo.

name: Ponte delle Voze
description: A Roman bridge with Augustan origins, known either as Ponte Muto or Ponte delle Vozze, associated with the Via Iulia Augusta and located in the Val Ponci in Liguria.

name: Ponte di Annibale
description: An arched Roman bridge across the Titerno near Cerreto Sannita in Campania.

name: Ponte di Annibale - Sant'Angelo
description: A Roman bridge known by a variety of local toponyms that crosses the Savuto river. It is located in the modern Italian municipality of Altilia, Calabria.

name: Ponte di Apollo, Punicum
description: A Roman bridge.

name: Ponte di Augusto
description: A Roman arched bridge located near Narni built originally in the Augustan period that remained in use until A.D. 1055.

name: Ponte di Capua
description: A Roman bridge crossed the Volturno river at Capua, some elements of which remain in the site of modern bridge.

name: Ponte di Diocleziano, San Lazzaro di Fossombrone
description: The so-called "Ponte di Diocleziano" in the local comune of San Lazzaro di Fossombrone was a Roman bridge along the Via Flaminia in the valley of the Metaurus river. The Roman bridge was completely destroyed during the Second World War.

name: Ponte di Malafede
description: A Roman bridge that is no longer extant. Quilici notes that the bridge was destroyed both by the Second World War and by urbanization that followed in the post-war period. Blake publishes a photo of E. B. van Deman and indicates that the bridge was built in peperino and of "indeterminate date". She compares the Ponte di Malafede with the construction of the Via Appia viaduct that lies just beyond Ariccia, a structure that was Augustan in its date.

name: Ponte di Mele
description: A Roman bridge along the Via Appia.

name: Ponte di Nona
description: A Roman-period bridge along the via Praenestina built of lapis Gabinus.

name: Ponte di Pietra
description: A Roman segmental stone arch bridge located near Augusta Praetoria.

name: Ponte di Pioraco
description: A single-arch Roman bridge built during the principate of Augustus.

name: Ponte di Pogi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 B2 unnamed bridge (over *Umbro fl., W Arretium at Pogi)

name: Ponte di Pratolungo
description: Remains of a Roman Republican bridge crossing the Fosso di Pratolungo along the line of the via Tiburtina.

name: Ponte di Quintodecimo
description: The Ponte di Quintodecimo is located near Acquasanta Terme.

name: Ponte di San Giovanni de Butris
description: The remains of a Roman bridge along the Via Flaminia that have been incorporated into the thirteenth-century church of San Giovanni de Butris.

name: Ponte di San Lorenzo
description: An early segmental arch bridge, built between 47 and 30 B.C.

name: Ponte di Santo Spirito
description: A Roman bridge over Torrente della Ginestra near Aequum Tuticum along the Via Traiana.

name: Ponte di Traiano
description: A Trajanic bridge along the Via Appia that crosses the fosso Striscia.

name: Ponte di Traiano at Tor Tre Ponti
description: A Roman bridge located on the Via Appia at modern Tor Tre Ponti.

name: Ponte di Vallerano
description: The Ponte di Vallerano is a second-century Roman bridge that carried the via Ostiensis across the fosso di Vallerano.

name: Ponte do Coadouro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte do Coadouro

name: Ponte Etrusco, Gallese
description: The so-called "Ponte Etrusco" is an ancient bridge located near Gallese in the province of Viterbo, Italy.

name: Ponte Ettori
description: A Roman bridge crossing the river Temo; the bridge is said to have originally had a five-arch span.

name: Ponte Ezzu
description: Ponte Ezzu or Pont'Ezzu is a Roman bridge of the first century A.D. that crosses the Rio Mannu near Ozieri, Sardinia.

name: Ponte Fabio Massimo
description: The so-called "Ponte Fabio Massimo" or "Ponte del Diavolo" originally dates to the third century BC with subsequent reworking and reuse continuing to the Middle Ages.

name: Ponte Fonnaia
description: The Roman bridge at Ponte Fonnaia carried a branch of the Via Flaminia over the river Naia. It is located north of modern Acquasparta in Umbria.

name: Ponte Fratto
description: The remains of a late Republican bridge near Benevento (in Località Serretelle).

name: Ponte Funicchio
description: A Roman bridge dating to the late second century B.C.

name: Ponte Gobbo
description: A Medieval bridge across the river Trebia that is said to have Roman origins.

name: Ponte Grosso
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D1-C4 unnamed bridge (on Via Flaminia (20))

name: Ponte Ladrone
description: The so-called Ponte Ladrone was a Roman bridge of the Via Ostiensis located at Acilia. According to L. Quilici, its remaining material vestiges were buried in 1982 by infrastructure works.

name: Ponte Latrone
description: A Roman bridge on the Volturno river located near Capriati.

name: Ponte Leproso
description: A bridge of Roman origins crossing the Sabato river on the outskirts of Benevento.

name: Ponte Loreto
description: A Roman bridge at Lanuvium.

name: Ponte Lucano Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the modern Aniene river adjacent to the Tomb of the Plautii, which is located north of the Villa of Hadrian at Tivoli.

name: Ponte Mammolo
description: Ponte Mammolo is the modern name of an ancient bridge over the Aniene river. It gives its name to a neighborhood and metro station in the northeast part of the modern city of Rome. Archaeological evidence of ancient quarrying and a settlement have also been found in the vicinity of the bridge.

name: Ponte Manlio
description: The Roman bridge known as "Ponte Manlio" carried the Via Flaminia across the Bosso torrent, whose main channel is now some hundreds of meters to the south. This is a well-preserved Roman vaulted arch bridge that dates to the second century B.C.

name: Ponte Molino
description: A Roman segmental arch bridge across the Bacchiglione river in Padua, Italy.

name: Ponte Mormanno
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 C2 unnamed bridge (NE Laos, at Mormanno)

name: Ponte Navea
description: A Roman bridge known as ‘Ponte Návea’ located near Puebla de Trives, over Rio Návea.

name: Ponte Nepesino
description: A bridge along the Via Amerina, just south of ancient Nepet (modern Nepi in Italy)

name: Ponte Nomentano
description: A Roman bridge over the Aniene river.

name: Ponte Papaloppe
description: A ruined bridge located near Nuoro in Sardinia.

name: Ponte romano – Ceggia
description: Excavated remains of a Roman bridge located on the modern Via Ponte Romano about 2km southest of Ceggia (Veneto), Italy. Aerial/satellite photographs often show crop marking corresponding to the associated Roman road and now-diverted watercourse spanned by the bridge.

name: Ponte Romano Di Largo Impero, Santa Marinella
description: The remains of a Roman bridge located in the comune of Santa Marinella.

name: Ponte Romano di Parco dei Medici
description: The ruins of a second-century A.D. Roman bridge at Magliana Vecchia.

name: Ponte romano di Santa Giusta
description: A Roman bridge dating between the first century BC and the first century AD.

name: Ponte Romano di Val d'Assano
description: An ancient bridge located to the east of ancient Teanum Sidicinum (modern Teano in Italy).

name: Ponte Romano di Viale Roma
description: The ruins of a Roman bridge located in the Viale Roma, Santa Marinella, Italy.

name: Ponte Romito
description: An ancient bridge, now known as "Ponte Romito" crosses the Arno River to the west of Ponte a Buriano.

name: Ponte Rotto
description: An ancient bridge over the Cervaro river between Aecae and Herdoniae on the Via Traiana.

name: Ponte S. Venere
description: A much-repaired medieval bridge crossing the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river. Long a traditional candidate location for the "Pons Aufidi" shown on the Peutinger map (thus BAtlas), recent survey work has found no evidence in the area consistent with a Roman-era bridge. The Pietra Dell'Oglio bridge (further south on the Ofanto) is of Roman date and now a more likely candidate. The Ponte S. Venere remains in use today for the Strada Statale 303.

name: Ponte Salario
description: The Ponte Salario is a Medieval bridge with Roman origins. It is located at the point where the Via Salaria crosses the Anio, about 3 km north of the Colline Gate.

name: Ponte San Cono, Buccino
description: A Roman bridge, perhaps originally dating to the first century BC, was substantially transformed to carry a provincial road in 1872.

name: Ponte San Stefano
description: A bridge that leads to the villa of Vibius Varius.

name: Ponte Sanguinario (Narni)
description: A Roman bridge located near Narni, Italy.

name: Ponte Scotonico
description: An ancient Roman bridge located along the Via Valeria near Roviano, Italy.

name: Ponte Sereno
description: A bridge near Ferentinum (modern Ferentino in Italy) that is traditionally dated to A.D. 260 or after. A substructure in polygonal masonry supports the bridge.

name: Ponte Sisto
description: The Ponte Sisto is a bridge that spans the Tiber River at Rome. In the present-day city, it links the Via dei Pettinari (Rione of Regola) to the Piazza Trilussa in Trastevere. The bridge was commissioned by Pope Sixtus IV and was built between 1473 and 1479. Its architect, Baccio Pontelli, reused the piers of the ancient Roman Pons Aurelius, which had come to be known as the Pons Antoninus in Late Antiquity. That bridge was at least partially destroyed in the 770s.

name: Ponte Spiano
description: The so-called Ponte Spiano is a Roman bridge located along the Via Flaminia in the modern comune of Sigillo.

name: Ponte sul Fosso Tre Ponti
description: A Roman bridge on the Via Amerina between ancient Nepet and Falerii.

name: Ponte Tufaro
description: A bridge of Roman origins located near Montesarchio.

name: Ponte Valentino
description: A Roman bridge located east of Benevento, Italy, and built by Flavius Iulius Valens.

name: Pontpierre
description: Remains of a Roman bridge over the modern Eyrieux river in the French commune of Saint-Fortunat-sur-Eyrieux.

name: Porto da Calçadinha
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Porto da Calçadinha

name: Puente de Cañete
description: The remains of a Roman bridge known locally as Puente Viejo or Puente de Cañete lying one mile west west of Obulco on the Arroyo Salado. The bridge may have been associated with a conjectured secondary Roman road or route between Corduba and Catulo because although unmentioned in the Antonine Itinerary, the bridge's location lies between two mentioned stations: Calpurniana and Vircao.

name: Puente de Génave Roman Bridge
description: Known locally as the Puente Romano or Puente Viejo, this single-arched bridge crosses the modern Río Guadalimar at Puente de Génave in Andalusia, Spain.

name: Puente de la Perdiz
description: A Roman bridge located near the hamelt of Nestar, over the Rio Rubagón.

name: Puente de Reparacea
description: A Roman bridge at the modern village of Oieregi in Spain's Navarre province. It spans the modern Baidasoa river.

name: Puente del Bibey
description: The Puente del Bibey or ‘Ponte Bibei’ crosses the river Bibei at Mendoya.

name: Puente del Chantre
description: A Medieval bridge spanning the Jucar stream.

name: Puente del Diablo (Martorell)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 unnamed bridge (Pont del Diable (Martorell) over R. Llobregat, SE (Ad) Fines)

name: Puente Mayor del Tormes
description: A Roman bridge over Rio Tormes in Salamanca, Spain.

name: Puente Mocho
description: A Roman bridge, known as the 'Puente Mocho', crossing the ribera de Cañedo.

name: Puente Mocho (Río Guadalimar)
description: A Roman bridge located between Chiclana and Beas de Segura, S Mariana.

name: Puente Romano (Talamanca del Jarama)
description: A five span stone bridge (Talamanca over R. Jarama, W Arriaca).

name: Puente romano de Alconetar
description: Relocated remains of a 300m-long Roman stone bridge originally located southwest of modern Tourmogon at Alconétar, over R. Tajo in Spain.

name: Puente Romano de los Ponlarrones (Villadoz)
description: Remains of ancient lime adjacent to the modern Huerva river at Villadoz in spain have been interpreted as a Roman bridge, likely associated with a road or route between Sermonae and Carae, two road stations attested in Roman itineraries.

name: Puente sobre el Arroyo Rabanales
description: A bridge, originally of Roman date, over the modern Arroyo Rabanales (a tributary of the Guadalquivir) just east of Cordoba, Spain. The bridge was in use as late as the 1920s.

name: Puentecillas
description: A bridge of the sixteenth century that rests on Roman foundations.

name: Puentecillas
description: A bridge of Roman origin in Palencia, Spain, that was remodeled in the fifteenth century.

name: Ribadiso Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge located in the Provincia da Coruña, Galicia, Spain.

name: Ribeira das Taliscas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ribeira das Taliscas

name: Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the river Platano, east of Volcei known alternately as Ponte del Diavolo/Ponte di Annibale.

name: Roman Bridge (Chaves)
description: A Roman bridge over the river Tâmega at Aquae Flaviae (modern Chaves, Portugal).

name: Roman Bridge (Paracuellos de Jíloca)
description: An unnamed Roman bridge just east of ancient Platea at modern Paracuellos de Jíloca over the river Jíloca in Spain.

name: Roman Bridge at Albertville
description: A hypothesized Roman bridge over the river Arly just above its confluence with the Isére at modern Albertville in France.

name: Roman Bridge at Aperlae
description: Remains of a bridge located near Aperlae.

name: Roman bridge at Ascain
description: A fifth-century Roman bridge over the river Nivelle at Ascain in France.

name: Roman bridge at Buonfornello
description: An unnamed ancient bridge, located in modern Buonfornello, Sicily.

name: Roman bridge at Clastidium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 E3 unnamed bridge (at Clastidium)

name: Roman bridge at Habitancum
description: Structural remains of a Roman bridge located on the River Rede in the vicinity of Habitancum Roman fort. The bridge lay along the ancient Watling Street.

name: Roman Bridge at Moulin de Boisseron
description: Ancient name unknown. Located in modern Boisseron, France.

name: Roman bridge at Palidoro
description: An ancient Roman bridge along the Via Aurelia.

name: Roman bridge at Valeriana
description: A bridge constructed across the Danube at the Roman fort of Valeriana.

name: Roman Bridge at Villevieille, Sommières
description: The so-called "Pont Tibère" in the modern French commune of Villevieille, in Sommières canton, France. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Roman Bridge of Châtillon
description: A Roman bridge over the Torrente Marmore at Châtillon in the Valle d'Aosta, Italy.

name: Roman bridge of Lugo
description: A bridge across the Minho River at Lugo with Roman origins. Heavily restored in modern times, the bridge now serves as a pedestrian walkway.

name: Roman bridge of Saint-Vincent
description: A Roman bridge built across the Cillian stream in the Valle d'Aosta, Italy.

name: Roman bridge of San Vito
description: The remains of a Roman bridge that once crossed the river known as Uso are located at San Vito, Italy, in the environs of Rimini.

name: Roman Bridge over Calycadnus at Seleucia ad Calycadnum
description: Epigraphic evidence, uncovered in the 19th century, indicates the presence of this bridge, built at the direction of the Emperor Vespasian and his two sons Titus and Domitian by the Cilician governor L. Octavius ​​Memor in 77 or 78 CE. The inscription, now lost, was reportedly found during construction of the current Taşköprü ("Stone Bridge") in the early 1870s. This 19th century bridge is often referred to as the "Roman Stone Bridge," despite its modernity.

name: Roman bridge over Rio S'Adde
description: Remains of a Roman bridge, north of Makopsisa (modern Macomer in Sardinia).

name: Roman bridge, Córdoba
description: A first century Roman bridge over the Guadalquivir river.

name: Roman bridge, Mérida
description: The Roman bridge (Puente romano) at Mérida spans the Guadiana River. It is included as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida".

name: Roman bridge, Pietralunga di Paternò
description: An unnamed bridge located west of Hybla Gereatis at Pietralunga di Paternò, over the river Simeto.

name: Roman Bridge: Le Vistre de la Fontaine Palaeochannel
description: Remains of a Roman bridge spanning a former channel of Le Vistre de la Fontaine south of Nîmes have been excavated. Work at the site also provided significant evidence for Roman-era waste and lead contamination transported down the river from the urban center of Nemausus.

name: Roman bridges at Tibur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 32 (bridges at Tibur (2))

name: S. Lidano
description: The so-called "Archi di San Lidano" are the remains of a Roman stone bridge in the Pontine plain, near the modern locality of Sezze Scalo. The bridge likely dates originally to the second century B.C. Evidence of an ancient sanctuary have also been detected in the vicinity.

name: Saint-Thibéry Roman Bridge
description: A segmental arch bridge on the Via Domitia in southern France, typically dated to the reign of Augustus.

name: Salone
description: Ancient tuff quarries and a bridge at Salone on the Anio River.

name: San Giuliano Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge crossing the Melfa river in Lazio, Italy.

name: Sarıcakaya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 H4 Sarıcakaya

name: Savignano sul Rubicone Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge on the Rubicon at Savignano sul Rubicone.

name: Segura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D2 Segura

name: Sextantio
description: Sextantio was a Celtic settlement at a ford on the Ledus fl. and later became a Gallo-Roman town.

name: Silves Roman Bridge

name: Solestà Roman bridge

name: Sôrinho

name: St-Sylvestre

name: Steinerner Steg - Ponte Romano

name: Susegana bridge
description: A Roman bridge along the Via Claudia Augusta.

name: Tarr Steps
description: Tarr Steps is a prehistoric bridge over the River Barle in England. The current phase dates to the Medieval period, although the original phase of the bridge is dated ca. 1000 BC.

name: Taşköprü
description: A large Roman bridge at Adana in the modern country of Turkey. It spans the Psaros/Sinarus/Koiranos river (modern Seyhan).

name: Tautaendia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 unlocated Tautaendia

name: Tenedo

name: Terrugem
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Terrugem

name: Theveste Roman Bridge
