description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C3 Torre del Padiglione

name: Tramatza Roman bridge
description: The remains of a Roman bridge at Tramatza cross the Riu di Mare Foghe. Gosner and Nowlin assert that this bridge likely dates to the first century BCE and that its construction may have allowed for better access to the Sardinian interior in the direction of Forum Traiani (Fordongianus). The bridge was repaired in the Middle Ages and again in 1826.

name: Unnamed Roman bridge at Mainz
description: Romans built a bridge over the Rhine river at Mogontiacum soon after 27 CE, based on dendrochronological evidence. This bridge likely replaced an earlier pontoon bridge. The first-century CE bridge spanned some 400 meters and was supported by 21 piers. The bridge facilitated an important road link and was repaired multiple times in antiquity. It may be the bridge pictured on a medallion issued by Constantius I Chlorus. The modern Theodor Heuss Bridge (Mainz-Wiesbaden) that was rebuilt following the Second World War stands at or near the point in the river once spanned by the Roman bridge.

name: Ussana Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge located northwest of modern Ussana in Sardinia.

name: Vaison-la-Romaine Roman Bridge
description: The Roman bridge at Vaison-la-Romaine is supposedly dated to the first century CE.

name: Via Ostiensis viaduct
description: A viaduct of the ancient Via Ostiensis that purportedly dates to the republican period.

name: Vila Formosa Roman Bridge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Vila Formosa

name: Vila Ruiva
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Vila Ruiva

name: Villa del Río Roman bridge
description: A Roman bridge along the Via Augusta.

name: Villa del Río Roman Bridge
description: A Roman bridge over the Arroyo Salado de Porcuna, located just east of modern Villa del Río in Spain's Cordoba province.

name: Villahermosa del Campo Roman bridge
description: The so-called Villahermosa del Campo Roman bridge crosses the River Huerva to the south of Ponlarrones in Spain.

name: Viviers Roman bridge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 unnamed bridge (Vivarium)

name: Yibna Bridge
description: The Mamluk arched bridge dating to the 1270s crosses the river Nahal Sorek near modern Yibna, Israel.

name: Zindan Bridge
description: A Roman-era bridge associated with the Zindan Mağarası sanctuary in Turkey's Isparta province. It spans the modern Aksu river.

name: Circus at Antinoopolis
description: A circus was identified at the site in the nineteenth century. John Humphrey dubbed this site "the most impressive circus in Egypt outside Alexandria".

name: Circus at Calagurris
description: The Circus at Calagurris is located to the northeast of the city and could have accommodated 20,000 spectators.

name: Circus at Lepcis Magna
description: The Circus at Lepcis Magna is located to the southeast of the city and measures ca. 100 x 450 m.

name: Circus at Mirobriga
description: The Roman circus at Mirobriga has been dated to the third century.

name: Circus at Segobriga
description: The area of the Roman Circus at Segobriga had been occupied in the earlier Roman period (at least since the Augustan period) by a cremation necropolis.

name: Circus Flaminius
description: Located in the southern Campus Martius, the Circus Flaminius is a large area built by C. Flaminius Nepos in 221 B.C. Various games, including the ludi plebeii, ludi Taurii, ludi saeculares, were celebrated here. The circus was also a place of popular assembly.

name: Circus Gaii et Neronis
description: Circus Gaii et Neronis was originally built as a private racecourse for the emperor Caius. In the fourth century A.D., the north side of the circus was destroyed in order to make room for the first basilica of St. Peter.

name: Circus Maximus
description: An ancient chariot-racing venue at Rome and a major location for public entertainments (ludi), the Circus Maximus is located between the Aventine and Palatine hills.

name: Circus of Col. Tarraco
description: The Roman circus of Tarragona dates from the reign of Domitian. The circus measures ca. 325 meters in length and ca. 100 to 115 meters in width.

name: Circus of Maxentius
description: The Circus of Maxentius is part of a complex of buildings built by emperor Maxentius on the Via Appia between A.D. 306 and 312.

name: Circus Varianus
description: A circus that was part of the Sessorium palace next to the Amphitheatrum Castrense. The obelisk currently situated on the Pincian Hill was discovered on the site in the sixteenth century. It had been moved to the spina of the circus during the reign of Elagabalus.

name: Hippodrome at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen
description: The Hippodrome at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen (Cyrenaica, Libya).

name: Hippodrome of Constantinople
description: The Hippodrome was the great racetrack of the city of Constantinople and was connected to the no longer extant imperial palace. At about 450 m in length, the Hippodrome once accommodated about 100,000 spectators.

name: Jerash Hippodrome
description: The Jerash Hippodrome dates after the middle of the second century. While among the smallest of the attested examples of Roman period hippodromes, it numbers among the best preserved.

name: Roman Circus (Bosra)
description: The Roman circus of Bosra dates to the third century.

name: Roman Circus at Mérida
description: The Roman Circus at Mérida dates ca. 20 B.C.

name: Roman circus of Carthage
description: The circus of Carthage could accommodate ca. 45,000 spectators and may date to the third century A.D.

name: Roman circus of Iruña-Veleia
description: A Roman circus has been identified at the site of Iruña-Veleia.

name: Tyre Hippodrome
description: The Tyre Hippodrome dates to the second century.

name: Villa Maxentii
description: A complex on the Via Appia constructed by Maxentius in the early fourth century A.D.

name: Agora at Kibyra
description: The agora of ancient Kibyra.

name: Agora at Malia
description: The so-called "agora" is a large open courtyard to the northwest of the Middle Minoan palace at Malia.

name: Agora of Athens
description: The marketplace of the ancient city of Athens in Greece.

name: Agora of Corinth
description: The Agora of ancient Corinth.

name: Agora of Dura-Europos
description: A group of ashlar buildings at the city center, identified by excavators as civic buildings.

name: Agora of Magnesia
description: Agora of Magnesia on the Maeander

name: Agora of Palmyra
description: Agora of the Ancient City of Palmyra.

name: Agora of Priene
description: The Agora of the ancient city of Priene.

name: Agora of the Competaliasts or Hermaists
description: Originally a commercial area of ancient Delos, built in the second century BCE on artificial infill adjacent to the city's port. The function of this area seems to have changed after the expansion and consolidation of Roman rule, with residential construction taking over the original commercial activity.

name: Ancient Agora of Argos
description: The Agora of ancient Argos.

name: Ancient forum of Beroe/Augusta Traiana
description: An architectural complex deemed to be the forum of the Roman city of Augusta Traiana.

name: Archaic agora of Thasos
description: The agora of the ancient city. The site was located near the center of the city's shoreline and was surrounded by stoas and public buildings.

name: Civic forum of Praeneste
description: The so-called civil forum of ancient Praeneste served as the center of the ancient city and was used across multiple periods. Its archaeological remains today correspond to the Piazza Regina Margherita and lie beneath the Sant'Agapito martire's cathedral. A large Etrusco-Italic temple of the fourth century BCE was situated here as well as a Roman-period basilica where the famous "Nile mosaic" was located in a flanking, apsidal room.

name: Commercial Market (Tetragonos Agora)
description: The Commercial Market or Tetragonos Agora was one of the main squares of ancient Ephesus.

name: Foro Romano
description: The Roman forum of Aquileia.

name: Forum Aemilianum
description: The Roman forum of Terracina, entered by a quadrifons arch, sits along the line of the Via Appia.

name: Forum Augusti
description: The Forum of Augustus at Rome was dedicated in 2 BCE, to the northeast of Julius Caesar's Forum. It contained the Temple of Mars Ultor.

name: Forum Boarium (at Rome)
description: Rome's so-called "cattle market", the Forum Boarium is a key area of commercial and ritual activity on the east bank of the river Tiber. It is flanked by the Capitoline, Palatine, and Aventine hills.

name: Forum Iulium
description: The Forum Iulium (or Forum of Caesar, Forum Caesaris) is a forum built by Iulius Caesar and dedicated ca. 46 B.C. The main cult of the forum is that of Venus Genetrix.

name: Forum of Carsulae
description: The Forum of Carsulae is supported by terraces and it includes the basilica and a twin sanctuary, in addition to tabernae.

name: Forum of Col. Augusta Raurica
description: The Forum of Augusta Raurica.

name: Forum of Conimbriga
description: The Roman forum of Conímbriga.

name: Forum of Cosa
description: The forum of Cosa functioned as the administrative center of the town and was the site of key administrative and ritual structures.

name: Forum of Hippo Regius
description: The Forum of Hippo Regius is one of the largest known Roman fora in north Africa.

name: Forum of Iuvanum
description: The Roman forum of Iuvanum.

name: Forum of Jublains
description: The remains of the ancient forum at modern Jublains.

name: Forum of Mactaris
description: One of two fora at Mactaris. This earlier square is adjacent to the Arch of Trajan.

name: Forum of Nerva
description: A forum complex at Rome begun under Domitian but completed under Nerva and dedicated in A.D. 97. Sometimes referred to as the Forum Transitorium, the complex was the next to last of the imperial fora at Rome.

name: Forum of Paestum
description: The forum of Roman Paestum stands north of the Hera sanctuary and measures ca. 57 x 150 meters. It occupies the site of the Greek agora and was surrounded with a peristyle of re-used Doric columns. Tabernae stood at its south side and baths funded by M. Tullius Venneianus stood to the southwest. A triple-podium structure - lararium? - stands on the western side.

name: Forum of Pompeii
description: Main forum of the Roman city of Pompeii (VII, 8).

name: Forum of Roman Corinth
description: The Roman forum of ancient Corinth.

name: Forum of Saepinum
description: The Forum of Saepinum assumes a trapezoidal shape and is aligned with the decumanus.

name: Forum of Scylletium/Scolacium
description: The Roman Forum of Scylletium/Scolacium.

name: Forum of Septimius Severus
description: The Forum of Septimius Severus at Lepcis Magna

name: Forum of Suasa
description: The Forum of Suasa measures 50 x 100 meters and is surrounded by a portico.

name: Forum of Theodosius
description: The Forum of Theodosius in Constantinople was founded by Constantine as the Forum Tauri. In 393, Theodosius I rebuilt and remodelled the forum to resemble the Forum of Trajan in Rome. The forum occupied the area of what is now Beyazıt Square in Istanbul.

name: Forum of Vulci
description: The forum of the ancient town, dating to the Roman period.

name: Forum Traiani
description: Imperial forum constructed at Rome under Trajan between 105-112 CE.

name: Helorus
description: An ancient settlement of Sicily that was likely a colonial foundation of Syracuse.

name: Methone (Pieria)
description: An ancient settlement in Macedonian Pieria on a hilltop named Koryphi, near modern Nea Agathoupolis in the Pydna-Kalindros municipality in Pieria, Greece. The site was occupied from the Late Neolithic period (ca. 4000 BCE) through 354 BCE, when it was destroyed by the armies of Philip II. It was succeeded by a new settlement approximately 1km to the northwest; known as Macedonian Methone, this later settlement was occupied in the Hellenistic and Roman periods.

name: North Agora
description: The Agora was the main civic square of the town. It was an enclosed space (ca. 202 x 72 m) surrounded by Ionic porticos on all sides. A fragmentary architrave inscription from the double north colonnade records its dedication by C. Julius Zoilos, the leading figure in the town in the 30s and 20s B.C.

name: Old Forum at Lepcis Magna
description: The original Roman forum at Lepcis Magna, in use prior to the construction of the Severan Forum in the early 3rd century CE.

name: Oval Forum (Jerash)
description: The Oval Forum at Jerash dates to the reign of Hadrian.

name: Roman Forum (Mérida)
description: The Roman Forum in Mérida, Spain, dates ca. 25 BCE. It was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1993.

name: Roman Forum at Syracuse
description: The area, roughly situated between the island of Ortygia and the district of Acradina, was used as an agora and, in later years, as the forum of the city of Syracuse. It is now a public park where ancient ruins are still visible.

name: Roman forum at Tergeste
description: The Roman forum of Tergeste.

name: Roman Forum at Thugga
description: The Roman Forum at Thugga is large, covering more than 900 square meters.

name: Roman Forum of Catania
description: The remains of a colonnaded square located near the Cortile di San Pantaleone in Catania are identified as those of the ancient Roman forum.

name: Roman forum of Col. Clunia (Sulpicia)
description: The Roman forum of Col. Clunia (Sulpicia) dates to the Imperial period. It is equipped with a rectangular basilica and a small tribunal in addition to shops. The paved decumanus enters the square through a gate.

name: Roman forum of London
description: A forum was constructed at London toward the end of the first century CE, and expanded between 90-120 CE.

name: Roman forum of Luna
description: The forum of Luna, founded as a colony in 177 B.C.

name: Roman forum of Sarsina
description: The Roman forum of Sarsina has been excavated in its northern reaches.

name: Römisches Forum Waldgirmes
description: The Roman Forum at Lahnau-Waldgirmes is the site of a fortified Roman trading outpost. It is located at the edge of the modern village of Waldgirmes.

name: South Agora
description: The so-called South Agora was the city's secondary public square. The complex dedicated by a local aristocrat Diogenes to the emperor Tiberius.

name: South Square
description: The South Square is the modern name for an enclosed area at the south end of the Athenian Agora, which was built in the mid-second century BC, incorporating some earlier structures. Parts of it were damaged in the Sullan Sack of 86 BC and demolished.

name: The Forum of Corporations
description: A key commercial center occupying the quadriporticus of the theater of Ostia, the Forum of Corporations dates initially to the Augustan period but is substantially rebuilt in the second century.

name: Tolmo de Minateda
description: Tolmo de Minateda was an early Roman hilltop oppidum located in the municipal territory of modern Helin in the Spanish autonomous community of Castilla-La Mancha. It was abandoned in the second century CE and then reoccupied in Late Antiquity (ca. 590 CE) as a Visigothic episcopal see during the Byzantine-Visigothic war. It was finally abandoned in the ninth century, and the population was resettled in Murcia, a new foundation. Tolmo de Minateda has been tentatively identified with the attested placename Ilounon.

name: ‘Stablo’
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 B2 ‘Stablo’

name: [Vicus Cruciniacum]
description: A Roman vicus on the Nahe river.

name: Abercyfor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 C3 Abercyfor

name: Abicada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Abicada

name: Acqua Claudia
description: A Roman villa at Anguillara Sabazia that may have been built in order to take advantage of mineral springs. The villa dates to the Late Republican period but has only been partially excavated. A large hemicycle with ambulatio is extant; this feature may have been coordinated with the villa's garden.

name: Acquoria
description: A basis villae near Tivoli in opus incertum on the river Aniene measuring 70 x 20 m. Two phases have been identified: second-first century B.C. and first century A.D.

name: Acropolis palace at Jebel Khalid
description: Hellenistic palace at the city of Jebel Khalid on the west bank of the Euphrates.

name: Acton Scott
description: Roman villa near Acton Scott, United Kingdom.

name: Ad Duas Lauros
description: The toponym 'Ad Duas Lauros' refers to a large imperial property at the third mile of the Via Labicana and today comprising the archaeological park of Centocelle. The site is mentioned in the Liber Pontificalis as that of a large imperial villa in which Helena, mother of Constantine I, lived. Archaeological work conducted in the late 1990s identified the remains of substantial Republican-period villas in the same area, with evidence of both elite dwellings and viticulture. The villas identified are the Villa della Piscina and the Villa Ad Duas Lauros.

name: Ad Villam Serviliana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 unlocated Ad Villam Serviliana

name: Adriano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 Adriano

name: Adro Vello
description: The remains of a Roman villa and necropolis.

name: Ag. Helene
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G4 Ag. Helene

name: Ag. Leontios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A3 Ag. Leontios

name: Agios Vasileios, Laconia
description: At Agios Vasileios is the archaeological site of the only Mycenaean palace thus far discovered in Lakonia. It is located near the village of Xerokambi in Lakonia, Greece.

name: Agnuli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 D1 Agnuli

name: Aguglia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Aguglia

name: Aiano-Torraccia di Chiusi Roman villa
description: The Villa Romana di Aiano-Torraccia di Chiusi, located near San Gimignano, is notable for a tri-apsidal courtyard.

name: Ailsworth
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Ailsworth

name: Ain-Taleb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 C4 Ain-Taleb

name: Akbou des Beni Chénacha
description: Noted by Gsell (AAA, whence BAtlas), modern Akbou des Beni Chénacha was the site of a large establishment of the Roman period, probably a villa. The location was approximately 2 kilometers to the southwest of colonial Camp-du-Maréchal (now Tadmaït in Algeria's Tizi Ouzou province.

name: Albinia Roman villa
description: The remains of a villa rustica near Orbetello, found to have construction in opus latericium.

name: Albuccione
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Albuccione

name: Allas-les-Mines
description: A Gallo-Roman villa.

name: Almoinhas Roman Villa
description: A Roman villa located near Loures, dating to the first through fifth centuries A.D.

name: Alresford
description: A Roman winged-corridor villa was discovered in a field adjacent to Alresford Lodge in 1884. Excavations were conducted under H. Laver in 1885.

name: Alto de Martim Vaz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Alto de Martim Vaz

name: Alto do Cidreira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A3 Alto do Cidreira

name: Alviano Scalo
description: Occupation of the Roman villa at Alviano Scalo begins in the first century BCE and continues until the fourth century CE.

description: Ancient name unknown.

description: A Gallo-Roman villa.

name: Apadana Palace
description: A monumental hypostyle building at Persepolis dating to the sixth century B.C.

name: Arbin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Arbin

name: Arbury Road
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Arbury Road

name: Archaeological area of Villa di Campetti
description: The site of a Roman villa complex covering ca. 10,000 square meters. The villa was built in the late republican period (second-first centuries B.C.) on the hillside adjacent to the Archaic Portonaccio sanctuary.

name: Arcinazzo
description: An imperial villa of the Trajanic and Hadrianic periods located on the Altipiani di Arcinazzo.

name: Ardara
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A2 Ardara

name: Arienzo
description: A villa of the second century AD that was destroyed by a flood in the fourth century.

name: Armira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 51 G2 Armira

name: Arnal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Arnal

name: Arnesp Roman villa, Valentine, Haute-Garonne
description: A luxurious Late Roman villa near the Garonne River.

name: Arrepiado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Arrepiado

name: Ashill
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Ashill

name: Ashtead Roman villa
description: The site of a Romano-British villa complex that included a bathhouse and tile works, occupied from ca. 67-79 CE until its abandonment ca. 200 CE.

name: Aspiran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A2 Aspiran

name: Assemini
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A3 Assemini

name: Astura
description: Astura was once a coastal island of Latium and is now a small peninsula at the southeastern edge of the Bay of Antium.

name: Atella
description: The site of a Roman villa in the locality of Magnone (Potenza) in South Italy. The archaeological site was first identified in 1979. Limited excavations identified a storage complex housing dolia. The presence of African Red Slip pottery suggests a date in the fourth century.

name: Barnsley Park
description: A Roman villa occupied between ca. A.D. 140 and the fifth century. It was discovered in 1950.

description: Roman villa at modern Benalmádena Costa on the Spanish Mediterranean coast, southwest of Malaga. The site is located on the north corner of the intersection of Avenida Antonio Machado and Avenida de las Palmeras. Most of the excavated portions of this large villa, which included a garden and water feature, are conserved or reburied under modern structures; however, a walled pit makes some portion of the extant remains visible to passerby.

name: Burnt Palace at Kalhu
description: The Burnt Palace, which was destroyed by fire when Kalhu fell to anti-Assyrian Babylonian and Median forces in 612 BC, has the longest building sequence yet identified at Nimrud (biblical Calah). This royal residence, which was constructed in the southeast quarter of the citadel, had at least nine phases of occupation between the Middle Assyrian and Hellenistic Periods. The so-called Burnt Palace was rebuilt/renovated three times during the ninth to seventh centuries BC and it might have temporarily served as a royal residence during the reign of Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC), while that Assyrian king was building his new capital Dūr-Šarrukīn. An immense collection of burnt ivories (the ‘Loftus ivories’) was discovered in and near the throne room in 1954–55; these are now in the British Museum (London).

name: Bussana
description: An early imperial villa along the coast of Liguria. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Buxio
description: Buxio is one of several villas mentioned in a list of "donatio ecclesiae" in the "territorio Carducino" in the Life of Saint Didier, who was bishop of Cahors in the 7th century CE. BAtlas places it with a hollow symbol and a question mark on map 25, offering "Le Buz, Vabre?" in the directory for modern location. Modern Vabre is a French commune in Occitania on the Gijou river. CAG reports that the remains of a villa had been reported in the 1940s in Vabre, but without further specificity.

description: Ashby describes a rich archaeological landscape that once included the remains of Roman-period tombs and villas, the latter appointed with mosaics. The modern toponym "Case Rosse" is said to derive from a well-preserved Roman tomb executed in brick that was purposefully destroyed in 1824.

name: Caserío
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 F4 Caserío

name: Casian
description: An unnamed settlement close to the modern village of Casian is also not far from the rock inscriptions from the Șeremet forest. The rock inscriptions mention the limits of the community of the Kasians and their caves. A house from the Roman period and some coins were found here. The relation between the settlement and the toponym Kasiana or Kasianon is not clear, because of the position of the inscriptions.

name: Casignana Roman Villa
description: The remains of a Roman villa in the modern Italian municipality of Casignana in Calabria. The site dates to the first through fifth centuries AD and is located near the Mediterranean coast, along the Strada Statale 106 between Bianco and Bovalino.

name: Cassiaco
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Cassiaco

name: Cassio
description: A villa in Tibur that has been incorrectly linked to Gaius Cassius Longinus. In the eighteenth century, the site produced a cache of Egyptianizing sculpture that likely dates to the reign of Hadrian and is now held in the Vatican Museums.

name: Castellaccio Europarco
description: A villa and a number of burials located along the Via Laurentina, dating from the fourth century BC to the second century AD.

name: Castellito di Ramacca
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Castellito di Ramacca

name: Castelo da Amendoeira

name: Castle Dykes

name: Castle Hill, Whitton
description: A large villa complex located at Castle Hill, Whitton. This site is considered to be one of the most important villa complexes known in Suffolk. Excavations were conducted in 1929-32, 1946-50 and 1988-9.

name: Castor

name: Centcelles Roman villa
description: A Roman villa located in the municipality of Constantí, in Catalonia. Among its finds are a dome mosaic with a Christian theme that numbers among the earliest known examples in the Roman world (4th century AD.)

name: Central Building at Kalhu
description: Near the middle of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud), the ninth-century-BC Assyrian king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) constructed a building with a grand, south-facing entrance. This building, constructed east of the southeast corner of the Northwest Palace, might have been one of the temples built by Ashurnasirpal II when he made Kalhu the administrative capital of the Assyrian Empire. Alternatively, the Central Building might have served as a palace.

name: Central Palace at Kalhu
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Tiglath-pileser III (r. 744–727 BC) built himself a grand, ornately-decorated royal residence at Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). This (large) palatial complex, which included a pillared-portico (a bīt-ḫilāni), was constructed south of the Northwest Palace (built by Ashurnasirpal II [r. 883–859 BC]). The “Cedar Palace” (as it is referred to in at least one contemporary Akkadian inscription) was largely dismantled by the seventh-century-BC ruler Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC), who had its (sculpted and inscribed) limestone slabs reused in his own royal residence (the Southwest Palace). The building is now generally referred to as the “Central Palace,” a modern designation given to it by its excavators since the palace was constructed in the central part of the Kalhu citadel.

name: Centro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 F4 Centro

name: Centroña

name: Centroni

name: Centum Celas

Thus far the site has produced archaeological evidence of suburban villas in Pompeii's hinterland. Early twentieth-century excavations were carried out here, only to be published in 1994. Clandestine looting at the site brought about a cooperative effort to resume excavations in the interests of heritage protection in 2017.

name: Contionacum

That house was constructed near the city wall in the southeastern part of city (city plan quadrants l8/l9), near the bank of the Tigris River. This building, which is known from numerous inscribed stone blocks, is often referred to as the “(Crown) Prince's Palace” or “Prinzenpalast.”

name: Cuarto Roble
description: A fortified Roman villa within the territory of Norba Caesarina. Re-occupation occurred during the Islamic period in Spain.

name: Cuevas de Soria

name: Cugno dei Vagni

The construction of later Medieval and Bourbon fortifications on the site significantly damaged the ancient remains. The Medieval Damecuta tower stands atop part of the Roman villa.

name: Dar Buk-Ammarah
description: A coastal villa known especially for its polychrome gladiatorial mosaics was excavated at Dar Buk-Ammarah, located on the coast some 4.5 km to the north of Zliten, Libya.

name: Dar Sied

name: Darenth

The villa measured ca. 370 by 414 feet upon excavations in 1894 and 1895. An assemblage of Roman coins (53 in number) range in date from Domitian to Gratian.

name: Daventry

name: Desenzano

Its decorative program includes polychrome mosaic pavements. It is noted for mosaic scenes featuring the Good Shepherd.

name: Domus Flavia

name: Domus Romana

name: Domus rustica

name: Egalgabarinutukua
description: The palace of the eighth-century-BC Assyrian Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) at Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), Egalgabarinutukua ("Palace That Has No Equal"), was constructed between 717 BC and 706 BC. This royal residence was constructed on a high, mud-brick terrace located within the city’s citadel, which was located against the northwestern stretch of the city wall. This large, ornately-decorated palace complex consisted of over two hundred rooms (including chapels to the deities Ea, Sîn, Ningal, Adad, Šamaš, and Ninurta) and courtyards, as well as a ziggurat.

name: Fingrinhoe Roman Site
description: A multi-acre site, now located within the bounds of the Fingringhoe Wick Nature Discovery Park in Colchester. The site may have been or included a Roman military supply base that was in use until ca. A.D. 120. The site included several structures, a ditched enclosure, and a possible villa.

name: Fishbourne

name: Flaviaco?

name: Fleury

name: Folkestone

name: Fons Timavi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F4 Fons Timavi

name: Fonte do Milho
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D3 Fonte do Milho

name: Formello villa and mausoleum
description: A Roman villa in the territory of Blera of which a cistern and a mausoleum remain.

name: Fórnea Roman villa
description: An imperial villa, occupied between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Fort Shalmaneser
description: The ninth-century-BC Assyrian king Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC) constructed an impressive palatial complex in the southeastern corner of the city Kalhu (biblical Calah). This armory, which the seventh-century-BC ruler Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) referred to as an ēkal māšarti (‘review palace’), was completed ca. 846 BC. Fort Shalmaneser, the name of building dubbed by its excavators, remained in use until the very end of the Assyrian Empire (612 BC), although its importance was significantly diminished after the Assyrian court moved away from Kalhu at the end of the eighth century BC, first to Dūr-Šarrukīn and then to Nineveh.

name: Forte Tiburtina
description: At Forte Tiburtina the remains of a Roman villa located on the northern edge of the Parco della Serenissima in the eastern suburbium of the city of Rome.

name: Fosso della Crescenza
description: John Ward-Perkins identified the archaeological site at Fosso della Crescenza during the South Etruria Survey. Excavations of a Iulio-Claudian mausoleum were undertaken in 1962. Additional excavation revealed portions of the Via Veientana. Post-excavation work did not occur until the 1980s, by which time Fentress 1983 notes that the archaeological landscape had been largely eradicated by the burgeoning growth of suburban Rome.

name: Fox-Amphoux
description: At Fox-Amphoux the remains of a Roman villa were discovered, adjacent to those of a sanctuary ad fines that may have been dedicated to the Capitoline triad. The site was in use from the time of Augustus until the third century AD.

name: Frampton
description: Samuel Lysons excavated a Roman villa at Frampton (Dorset) in 1796. Its unusual mosaics were a subject of keen interest, even attracting the attention of George III. The villa was thought to have been demolished, but Bournemouth University demonstrated that remains of the villa can still be identified in situ in 2019.

name: Franks Roman villa
description: The site of a Roman villa with two parallel ranges of rooms dating from the second half of the first century to the fifth century.

name: Gelleneuve Gallo-Roman villa
description: A fourth century villa was discovered at Gelleneuve in 1955 and 1956. The site later served as a Merovingian necropolis.

name: Gerace Roman Villa
description: A fourth century A.D. Roman villa with mosaic pavements. The villa may have extended to some 10,000 square meters in area.

name: Gestingthorpe
description: A Romano-British villa site located in Gestingthorpe parish, Essex, that has been sampled through survey, magnetometry, and partial excavation. Artifact evidence indicates occupation from the first to the fourth centuries.

name: Ghemines
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B2 Ghemines

name: Ghubayrā
description: Town in southern Iran built on a burial site used in the 3rd-4th millennia BCE and the Sasanian period. The town on the site was inhabited from from the 10th-14th century.

name: Gilly-sur-Isère
description: An archaeologically attested "agglomération romaine" (evidently equipped with port and warehouse facilities) at modern Gilly on the Isere river in France (about 4km downriver from Albertville), appears to have been an extension or adjunct of the Roman road station and toll house of Ad Publicanos, thought to have been located in the area.

name: Gleyzia d'Augreilh
description: A Late Roman villa.

name: Gorhambury Ancient Site
description: A multi-period site with evidence of construction and occupation ranging from the Neolithic to the Medieval periods. An iron age farmstead was replaced by a series of Roman-era constructs, including a late first-century CE masonry establishment, often characterized as a Roman villa (the site is located near the site of Verulanium, modern St. Albans). Renovated in the late second century, the farm declined in the third.

name: Governor's Palace at Kalhu
description: This beautifully-decorated Assyrian palace is located just north of the Nabû temple Ezida, in the southeast quarter of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). The building likely served as the office and/or residence of several important individuals in the administration of the Assyrian Empire, probably including the governor of Kalhu. Numerous cuneiform tablets were discovered in three rooms north of the central courtyard. Because many of those Akkadian texts relate to the activities of several governors, British excavators in the 1950s dubbed the building the "Governor's Palace."

A small portion of the mostly invisible site was excavated in the early 20th century, with a second (never published) excavation following in the 1950s. A 2019 geophysical survey and its publication (Maw 2021) have made the site more sensible and better documented.

name: Khorsabad Palace F
description: The armory (ekal māšarti) of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad) was located in the lower city, adjacent to the southern stretch of the southwest wall. No inscriptions of the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) record the construction of "Palace F," although several objects of that ruler were discovered there.

name: Khorsabad Residence J
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence J, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire. Since no inscriptions were discovered in the ruins of the building, the owner/principal occupant of the this Assyrian palace is not known.

name: Khorsabad Residence K
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence K, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire, possibly even Sargon’s heir designate Sennacherib.

name: Khorsabad Residence L
description: The residence of Sîn-aḫu-uṣur, brother of the Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) and grand vizier of the Assyrian Empire, was constructed in the citadel of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), near the royal palace Egalgabarinutukua ("Palace That Has No Equal"). "Residence L" was largest of the four royal residences (J, K, L, and M) built in Dūr-Šarrukīn's citadel.

name: Khorsabad Residence M
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had several royal residences constructed in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Residence M, which has constructed on the western side of the citadel, is presumed to have been for one of the highest officials of the Assyrian Empire. Since no inscriptions were discovered in the ruins of the building, the owner/principal occupant of the this Assyrian palace is not known.

Archaeological survey, published in 2023, reports that the significant surface presence of ancient ceramics (3rd - 5th centuries CE) surround the visible ruins of the structure, extending over 150,000 square meters, with highest concentrations immediately around and to the south and east where an ancient cistern has been documented.

The villa was inhabited from the second century B.C. to the seventh century A.D. The villa's archaeological remains are found in the locality of Barricelle in the territory of the community of Marsicovetere in the province of Potenza.

name: Molino San Vincenzo
description: Field surveys, geophysical prospection, as well as targeted research and training excavations, have revealed the remains of a mid- to late- imperial rural settlement with evidence for agricultural activity. However, the earliest finds (e.g., bucchero) date to the sixth/fifth century B.C., indicating the likelihood of Etruscan settlement activities at this site. The entire site seems to have undergone severe recycling and reuse processes in late antiquity and the remaining structures have broken down systematically in modern times. Ongoing intensive agricultural activities affect the few archaeological structures that remain, resulting in a poor state of preservation.

name: Moltone, S. Pietro

description: The Middle Assyrian king Tukultī-Ninurta I (1233–1197 BC) built himself a new royal residence at Assur. That palace, which went by the Sumerian name Elugalumunkurkurra (“House of the King, Lord of the Lands”), covered an area of about 27000 m². After Tukultī-Ninurta I’s reign, the so-called “New Palace” was no longer maintained and, thus, fell into ruins.

name: New Weir

name: North Palace at Babylon
description: The Neo-Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC) constructed a new royal residence for himself at Babylon. This grand palace was constructed north of the so-called “South Palace,” the traditional royal residence of the kings of Babylon. The building, which is referred to as the “new palace” (ēkallu eššu) in Achaemenid-period texts, is generally called the “North Palace,” “Nordburg,” and “New Palace.”

name: North Palace at Deir el-Ballas
description: Royal palace in the Upper Nile in use from the Second Intermediate Palace to the early New Kingdom.

name: North Wraxall
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 North Wraxall

name: Northchurch
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Northchurch

name: Northwest Palace at Kalhu
description: An impressive and ornately-decorated palace constructed at Kalḫu (modern Nimrud, biblical Calah) by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC), likely completed between 865 and 869 BC. This multi-winged palatial complex served as the primary residence and chief administrative center of the Assyrian Empire until Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) moved the imperial capital to Dūr-Šarrukīn in 706 BC. The Northwest Palace, the name given to it by its excavators (since it was built in the northwestern part of Kalḫu’s citadel), remained in use until the very end of the Assyrian Empire (612 BC). The southern half of the building also functioned as the burial place of prominent royal women, including Mullissu-mukannišat-Ninua, the wife of Ashurnasirpal and the mother of Shalmaneser III.

name: Norton Disney
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G1 Norton Disney

name: Nossa Senhora do Desterro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C4 Nossa Senhora do Desterro

name: Numituriana?/Saltus Caeliae Maximae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E4 Numituriana?/Saltus Caeliae Maximae

name: Oberentfelden
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 F2 Oberentfelden

name: Oberweis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Oberweis

name: Old Durham
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 9 F6 Old Durham

name: Old Palace at Assur
description: The Old Palace was the most important palace complex in the city of Assur. Although the oldest, securely-datable archaeological existence for the palace dates to the early Middle Assyrian period, this royal residence was probably founded in the Old Assyrian period, possibly during the reign of Šamšī-Adad I (ca. 1836–1776 BC). This important building was continuously until the sack and destruction of Assur in 614 BC. The Old Palace not only served as a residence for the Assyrian king and his family and an administrative center, but it was also a place of worship where the gods gathered on special occasions and the burial place of Assyrian kings.

name: Oldcoates
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F1 Oldcoates

name: Olivet d’en Pujol
description: An agricultural storage and processing facility including a large number of dolia, likely associated with a Roman villa or suburban fundus, Olivet d'en Pujol is located in the modern municipality of Vilademat, which is in the Catalonian comarca of Alt Empordà.

name: Opimiano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Opimiano

name: Oplontis
description: An ancient place (modern Torre Annunziata in Italy), lying between Pompeii and Herculaneum and buried by the eruption of Vesuvius in 79 CE. Its ancient name is known only from Peutinger's Roman Map.

name: Orbe Gallo-Roman Villa
description: The site of a palatial Roman villa dating to the first and second centuries A.D. Discovered in 1841, the site is noted for its extensive in situ mosaic pavements.

name: Ortomosaico di Giarratana
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Ortomosaico di Giarratana

name: Orton Longueville
description: The site of Orton Longueville has evidence for multi-period human activity. A large timber building is built in the second century A.D., followed by a Romano-British villa dating from the third to fifth centuries.

name: Ospedale di S. Camillo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B2 Ospedale di S. Camillo

name: Ossaia
description: Site of a Late Republican and Imperial Roman villa (first to fifth centuries A.D.)

name: Ouarville
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Ouarville

name: Oued Athmenia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E4 Oued Athmenia

name: Oued Bqader
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 B5 Oued Bqader

name: Oued Zariel
description: Immediately north of the Oued Zariel (Nador municipality, Algeria), remains of two ancient farms were identified, including an olive press.

name: Oukki
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G1 Oukki

name: Paduline villa
description: A Roman villa located on the Bruna river that was in use from the third century B.C. until the fifth century A.D.

name: Pagliuzza
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D3 Pagliuzza

name: Pago de Bruñel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 B4 Pago de Bruñel

name: Pago de los Fetisones
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 G3 Pago de los Fetisones

name: Pajares
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 E4 Pajares

name: Palace at Amathous
description: A large-scale building, tentatively identified as a palace, constructed during the 9th century BCE and in use until about 500 CE.

name: Palace of Adad-nārārī III
description: The Assyrian king Adad-nārārī III (r. 810-783 BC) constructed a palace for himself at Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). This royal residence, whose walls were covered with white plaster and painted with geometric and floral motifs, was built immediately to the south of the palace of his great-grandfather Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC).

name: Palatial complex at Pteria
description: Enclosed area at the Iron Age city of Pteria (modern Kerkenes Dağ) containing an audience hall and other elite structures. The complex is in an area of the city that was destroyed by fire, most likely in the mid-5th century BCE.

name: Palatiolum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Palatiolum

name: Palazzo a Mare
description: The site of one of the twelve villas of Tiberius, and perhaps the largest, on the island of Capreae.

name: Palazzo Pignano
description: Palazzo Pignano is known particularly for a large villa built in the Late Roman period (fourth to fifth centuries A.D.)

name: Pangbourne
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F3 Pangbourne

name: Parets Delgades
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Parets Delgades

name: Park Street
description: Park Street is the site of a Roman villa excavated in 1943-45 (dwelling) and 1954-57 (subsidiary buildings).

name: Parnaco
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Parnaco

name: Parndorf
description: Parndorf was the site of a Roman estate covering some 12 ha. The villa was situated near the Roman road leading from Savaria-Scarbantia to Carnuntum. The site was fortified by means of an enclosure wall ca. 1.5 km long.

name: Partizany
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 L2 Partizany

name: Passo Lombardo
description: A villa rustica in Rome's suburbium with evidence for domestic production in the form of vats made from peperino tuff.

name: Pattano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 B4 Pattano

name: Patti Marina
description: A third-century A.D. Roman villa near Messina in Sicily that was perhaps the center of an ancient latifundium.

name: Paturro Roman villa
description: A luxury terraced villa in Spain's Murcia region that was first built in the Republican period and then abandoned by the third century A.D.

name: Paulenca
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 B4 Paulenca

name: Pauli Putzu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A3 Pauli Putzu

name: Pavlikeni
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 C5 Pavlikeni

name: Pèbre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 A2 Pèbre

name: Pepiron villa rustica
description: Archaeological excavations at Pépiron in the commune of Saint-Just, near Marennes in Charente-Maritime, France, revealed the remains of a Roman villa rustica.

name: Petra/Fundus Petrensis
description: As attested by an inscription found on the site in 1901 and a passage in Ammianus, this fortified country estate was built by one Sammac, son of Nubel, whose brother Firmus assassinated him and raised a revolt against the Romans in the 370s CE. The modern placename associated with the site is M'lakou; it is located near Ighzar Amoqrane in Algeria's Kabylia province.

name: Petras Minoan Settlement
description: Minoan palatial settlement located on a hill 1.5km from Seteia, Crete.

name: Petregontio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H2 Petregontio

name: Petroliaco
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Petroliaco

name: Piddington
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Piddington

name: Pietrastretta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 C3 Pietrastretta

name: Pieve a Bozzone
description: The ruins of an ancient Roman villa were discovered in the Italian comune of Pieve a Bozzone (Siena) in 2023.

name: Pintens
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C2 Pintens

name: Pisões
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Pisões

name: Pitney
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Pitney

name: Pîtres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Pîtres

name: Place near Taparura
description: Unnamed minor settlement or villa in the territory of the Machounoi

name: Plailly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Plailly

name: Playa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C2 Playa

name: Pleshey
description: A possible Roman courtyard villa has been identified at Pleshey, based on the discovery of mosaic pavements, burials, and coin finds, most of which were recorded by ploughing in the 18th century.

name: Plinio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 E2 Plinio

name: Plinio
description: The so-called villa of Pliny the Younger, also known as the Villa della Palombara. The villa's first phases date to the Iulio-Claudian period. It continues in use, and continues to undergo modifications, through the second century A.D.

name: Ploaghe
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A2 Ploaghe

name: Poggio del Molino
description: A Roman villa overlooking the Tyrrhenian Sea located within the former territory of Populonia.

name: Poggio Gramignano Roman Villa
description: A Roman villa in the modern Lugnano commune of Italy's Terni province. Substantial architectural remains dating to the Roman imperial period have been excavated there, as well as a later (5th century CE?) collection of child and infant burials that gained significant media attention.

name: Poggio la Guardia
description: The site of a Roman villa.

name: Pompegiaco
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F4 Pompegiaco

name: Pompeo
description: A villa attributed to Cnaeus Pompeius.

name: Ponte Capo d’Acqua
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D4 Ponte Capo d’Acqua

name: Ponte de Lisboa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Ponte de Lisboa

name: Ponte di Tuscolo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Ponte di Tuscolo

name: Port-le-Grand
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B2 Port-le-Grand

name: Port-Sainte-Foy
description: At Le Canet, Cm. Port-Ste-Foy-et-Ponchapt, on the west bank of the Dordogne river, a Gallo-Roman villa was discovered in 1922. On the basis of its mosaic decorations, the villa is dated to the later imperial period.

name: Port-Sainte-Marie
description: A Gallo-Roman villa located on the right bank of the Garonne river.

name: Porto do Sabugueiro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Porto do Sabugueiro

name: Posta Crusta
description: The 'villa rustica' at Posta Crusta.

name: Posta Vecchia
description: Remains of a Roman imperial villa incorporated into a post office that later became a hotel.

name: Posto villa
description: The so-called "Posto villa" at Francolise was located in the ancient Ager Falernus of Campania. Its remains lie 500 meters from the so-called "San Rocco" villa. This was a small villa rustica whose incipit comes ca. 100 BC.

name: Prado de José Antonio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 G3 Prado de José Antonio

name: Praedia B(...)
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 Praedia B(...)

name: Prati Fiscali
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Prati Fiscali

name: Prato La Corte
description: A villa rustica was excavated near Licenza in 1913, the exact position of which is no longer known. This site included a possible granary room, as well as a torcular.

name: Priddy
description: A Roman building located approximately 270 meters north and east of Priddy Church. The site, excavated in 1964, has been interpreted as being part of a small villa site.

name: Prima Porta
description: Prima Porta, located to the north of Rome, allegedly derives its modern name from the remains of a Roman aqueduct whose presence signaled to travelers on the Via Flaminia that they had reached Rome. The site is best known for an Imperial villa that belonged to the family of Livia Drusilla.

name: Priorato
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 E4 Priorato

name: Prisciago
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Prisciago

name: Puente de la Olmilla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 C3 Puente de la Olmilla

name: Puente Melchor Roman villa
description: A Roman villa with mosaic decoration located in an archaeological area of Puente Melchor, in Puerto Real, Cádiz, Spain.

name: Pujol de Benicató
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 E2 Pujol de Benicató

name: Pulborough
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G4 Pulborough

name: Pully
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 D3 Pully

name: Punta Eolo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 D4 Punta Eolo

name: Puntone Vecchio
description: A Roman villa that was likely in use from the first century BC until the third century AD.

name: Puyloubier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 F2 Puyloubier

name: Qasr al-Abd at Tyrus
description: A Hellenistic palace constructed in the second century CE at the southern end of the Tyrus/'Iraq al-Amir site.

name: Quadraro
description: A Roman villa near the line of the Aqua Claudia located in the modern Via del Quadraro. The villa is dated to the first century B.C. or first century A.D. Following remodelling in the third century, the villa's occupation ends in the fourth century.

name: Quartu S. Elena
description: Quartu Sant'Elena is located along the line of the Roman road at a distance of four miles from Caralis.

name: Quinta da Bolacha
description: A Roman villa located near several productive sites.

name: Quinta da Ribeira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D3 Quinta da Ribeira

name: Quinta do Marim
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Quinta do Marim

name: Quintanilla de la Cueza
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 G2 Quintanilla de la Cueza

name: Quintilii
description: A suburban villa built by Sextus Quintilius Maximus and Sextus Quintilius Condianus (consuls in A.D. 151).

name: Quintilio Varo
description: A villa located near the sanctuary of Madonna di Quintiliolo (Tivoli) that is traditionally associated with Quintilius Varus. The remains of the basis villae may date as early as the third century B.C.

name: Rabaçal Roman villa
description: A fourth-century AD Roman villa located in Rabaçal, Portugal, near the site of Conimbriga.

name: Radford Semele
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F2 Radford Semele

name: Radlett
description: A Roman villa and bath building, dating between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Radwell Roman villa
description: A Roman villa site in Hertfordshire, England, Radwell Roman villa is located 500 meters west of Ermine Street and some 4 km north of the small Roman town at Baldock.

name: Ramla bay villa
description: A villa maritima discovered in 1910-11.

name: Ras el-Oued Gordab
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C1 Ras el-Oued Gordab

name: Ras Enghela
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 E2 Ras Enghela

name: Ras-el-Kasba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Ras-el-Kasba

name: Ratavoux
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 D1 Ratavoux

name: Reculan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 C2 Reculan

name: Reilla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G2 Reilla

name: Rekada
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 B3 Rekada

name: Remstecken
description: Site of a Roman villa rustica, dating from the first to third centuries AD.

name: Reontio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E4 Reontio

name: Ridgewell
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Ridgewell

name: Rienda Roman Villa
description: In the 1960s, agricultural activity unearthed evidence of a Roman-era villa at modern Rienda in Spain, which was then administratively associated with the municipality of La Artieda de Aragón (it is now part of the Sigüés municipality in Zaragosa province). Subsequent excavation revealed a large courtyard and other structures, as well as a number of mosaics.

name: Rijswijk
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 10 A4 Rijswijk

name: Rio Maior
description: A Roman villa in Rio Maior, Santarém, Portugal.

name: Rivenhall
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H3 Rivenhall

name: Rocca Stefano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 D4 Rocca Stefano

name: Rockbourne
description: A Roman-style courtyard villa occupied until the fifth century AD.

name: Roman villa
description: Remains of an early imperial period Roman villa, the so-called "Domus della Madonna delle Grazie".

name: Roman villa
description: Archaeological remains of modest Roman villas in the theater district of Colonia Iulia Carthago.

name: Roman Villa and Medieval Settlement at Piazza Armerina
description: The so-called "Villa Romana del Casale" (of Roman date) and the nearby settlement of Piazza Armerina, which seems to have been occupied by survivors from the villa site after it was destroyed in an earthquake in the early middle ages. The villa is listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, in part because of its famous mosaics.

name: Roman villa at Anagnina Metro station
description: The archaeological remains of a villa in the suburbium of the city of Rome located adjacent to what is now a station of the Rome Metro system at Anagnina.

name: Roman villa at Artena
description: A Roman villa built atop the terraced platform of the so-called "Civita di Artena".

name: Roman villa at Can Terrés
description: A Roman villa occupied from the first century B.C. until the fourth / fifth centuries A.D. The villa included a bath complex.

name: Roman Villa at El Faro (Torrox)
description: A Roman villa located at the modern lighthouse on Avenida del Faro, Torrox, 29770, Málaga in Spain.

name: Roman villa at Híjar en Las Gabias
description: A Roman villa, of which the pars fructuaria has been excavated.

name: Roman Villa at Lago di Nemi
description: A Roman Villa on the shores of Lago di Nemi.

name: Roman villa at Maasbracht
description: A Roman villa near the modern village of Maasbracht, Limburg, Netherlands, famous for its murals. The earliest structures on the site are dated to the second century CE, and the villa was abandoned by the middle of the third century.

name: Roman villa at Mill End
description: The remains of a Romano-British villa on the Buckinghamshire bank of the River Thames just south of the village of Mill End. The remains were first observed via parchmarks in 1921.

name: Roman villa at Positano
description: An opulent Roman villa maritima destroyed by the A.D. 79 eruption of Vesuvius. The remains of an opulent and well-decorated Roman villa have been known at Positano since at least 1758 when ruins were discovered during work on the bell tower of the church of S. Maria Assunta. Karl Weber conducted excavations there in the same year. Modern work, begun in 1999, has stabilized and consolidated the site.

name: Roman villa at Ramat Hanadiv
description: A Roman villa of the first century CE also known as "Horvat Akav" (חורבת עקב) in Hebrew.

name: Roman villa at Santo André de Almoçageme
description: A Roman villa located in Santo André de Almoçageme, Sintra, Portugal, and occupied from the second to the sixth centuries AD.

name: Roman villa at Tourmont
description: Remains of a large Roman villa and site of a significant coin hoard of the third century A.D.

name: Roman villa at Verige Bay
description: A Roman villa rustica located in the bay of Verige, dating from the first century B.C. through the sixth century A.D. The villa's ruins lie within Croatia's Brijuni National Park.

name: Roman Villa at Welschbillig
description: This extensive villa (ancient name unknown) was located at modern Welschbillig in Germany (in the ancient territory of the Treveri).

name: Roman villa at West Mersea
description: Remains of a tessellated floor pavement likely indicate the presence of a villa.

name: Roman villa near Praeneste
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 41 (villa near Praeneste)

name: Roman villa near Setia
description: The platform, including cryptoporticus, of a republican period villa originally dating to the first century B.C. with evidence for second century A.D. renovations.

name: Roman villa of Aia Nova
description: A Roman villa of the mid-first century B.C. connected with a Publius Anilius, identified by means of brick and tile stamps. Located in the modern commune of Scansano.

name: Roman villa of Almenara-Puras
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 G3 La Calzadilla

name: Roman villa of Ammaia
description: The Roman villa of Ammaia dates to the first century.

name: Roman villa of Capo di Massa
description: A villa at the Capo di Massa that has frequently been associated with Pollius Felix, a magistrate from Puteoli.

name: Roman villa of Cortijo Robledo
description: A Roman villa rustica dated between the first and fifth centuries. The site occupies a low hill located to the south of Cerro de Robledo, near the Roman settlement of Aratispi. Archaeological fieldwork has demonstrated that the site was involved in the production of olive oil until the later Roman period.

name: Roman Villa of San Giovanni Del Palco
description: The remains of a Roman villa located adjacent to the Convent of San Giovanni del Palco in Taurano, Italy. The convent, founded in 1383 at the Niccolò Orsini, utilizes elements of the villa's structural remains in its foundations.

name: Roman villa on viale Alfredo Ottaviani
description: The ruins of a Roman villa were discovered along viale Alfredo Ottaviani at Acilia Nord in 2011.

name: Roman villa, via Michelangelo Tilli
description: The remains of the Roman villa located in via Michelangelo Tilli are located close to the Aniene. The late republican villa remained in use until the fourth century.

name: Romano-British villa 250m south of Park Farm
description: A Romano-British villa and its associated features are located to the north-east of the River Ingol and along the ancient route known as the Icknield Way.

name: Rometta
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G2 Rometta

name: Römische Villa
description: A 'villa rustica' located to the west of Höflein, Austria, on the Danube frontier.

name: Römische Villa Otrang
description: This extensive rural villa of the Roman imperial period (ancient name unknown) was built in the territory of the Treveri. Located at modern Fliessem-Otrang in Germany.

name: Rostrata Villa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Rostrata Villa

name: Rothley
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F2 Rothley

name: Rougham
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H2 Rougham

name: Roussivau
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 C3 Roussivau

name: Roxby
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G1 Roxby

name: Royal Palace of Māri
description: Royal residence of the rulers of the kingdom of Mari. Constructed in the 24th century BCE, the site is also known as the Palace of Zimri-Lim, the ruler who completed extensive renovations of the structure around 1800 BCE.

name: Royal Palace V at Pergamum
description: The so-called Palace V has been interpreted as a royal residence at Pergamum containing a peristyle watered by cisterns. It is noted for its mosaic decoration.

name: Roye
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Roye

name: Rudston
description: A modern village in the East Riding of Yorkshire near which were found Neolithic cursus monuments and standing stones, two Iron Age Arras Culture cemeteries, and a fourth-century Romano-British villa.

name: Rudston Roman Villa
description: a fourth-century Romano-British villa west of the modern village of Rudston

name: Russi Roman Villa
description: The remains of an extensive Roman villa dating from the first century B.C. onward, located at modern Russi, Italy.

name: Rutland Roman Villa
description: A Roman villa, identified and initially excavated since 2018, located in the modern district of Rutland (England, United Kingdom), east of Foster's Bridge and southwest of Ketton.

name: S. Angiarxia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 48 A3 S. Angiarxia

name: S. Antonio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 S. Antonio

name: S. Carlo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 S. Carlo

name: S. Colomba
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 G3 S. Colomba

name: S. Croce
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 C1 S. Croce

name: S. Cruz
description: The Roman Villa of Baños de Valdearados, also known as the Villa de Santa Cruz, is located in Baños de Valdearados, Burgos region of Ribera del Duero, in Castilla y León (Spain). A Late Imperial Roman villa (fourth and fifth centuries CE) with polychrome mosaics is located at the site.

name: S. Cucufate
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 S. Cucufate

name: S. Giacomo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 G2 S. Giacomo

name: S. Giovanni
description: Canadian-led excavations between 1977 and 1984 uncovered a series of three Roman villas dating from the first to the sixth centuries AD at San Giovanni di Ruoti in southern Italy.

name: S. José

name: S. Nicolo di Carini
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 C2 S. Nicolo di Carini

name: S. Rocco
description: The San Rocco villa was built during the second quarter of the first century B.C. It was initially a simple villa rustica and it was later enlarged substantially.

name: S. Teresa di Longarini
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 G4 S. Teresa di Longarini

name: So-called Villa on the Lake
description: Lugli describes the remains of a sumptuous, porticoed villa in the Doric order facing onto the Alban lake. Marzano clarifies Lugli's earlier summary of the site, which was in turn based on observations made in 1912. The remains of the villa include a 200-m long portico on two levels. About 260 m from the portico are the remains of a cistern in opus reticulatum. Chiarucci documented a structure with five barrel-vaulted rooms of unclear function. Lugli's initial report suggests that this late republican villa was eventually incorporated into the Villa of Domitian.

name: Southwest Palace at Kalhu
description: The seventh-century-BC Assyrian king Esarhaddon (r. 680–669 BC) started building a royal residence in the southwest corner of the citadel of Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). Sculpted and inscribed limestone slabs from the Central Palace (constructed by Tiglath-pileser III [r. 744–727 BC]) and the Northwest Palace (built by Ashurnasirpal II [r. 883–859 BC]) were used as building materials for this royal residence, which very likely remained unfinished at the time of Esarhaddon’s death in 669 BC. Its excavators called it the “Southwest Palace” since it was located in the southwest corner of the citadel.

name: Thermae Constantinianae
description: The last of the great imperial baths of the ancient city of Rome, the Thermae Constantinianae were constructed in Regio VI, perhaps replacing the pre-existing Balineum Claudianum. They were located at the southern end of the Quirinal Hill, between the Vicus Longus and the Alta Semita. Substantial remains existed until the sixteenth century and they were sketched by A. Palladio and E. Du Pérac. The completion of the Palazzo Pallavicini-Rospigliosi in 1621 eliminated the majority of the ancient remains.

This archaeological landscape has been known and explored since at least the 19th century through chance finds, field walking, geophysical prospecting, targeted excavation, and aerial photography.

name: Untitled Villa on the island of Igilium
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 41 D5 unnamed villa (Igilium Ins.)

name: Upper Chambers at Kalhu
description: This ornately-decorated, elevated building might have been the residence of Sammu-ramat (the legendary Semiramis) in the palace of her son Adad-nārārī III (r. 810-783 BC) at Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). Because these rooms were built noticeably higher than the surrounding area, its first excavator Austen Henry Layard believed that he had uncovered part of an upper floor of an Assyrian palace and, therefore, referred to this area as the “Upper Chambers.”

name: Ussana
description: A Roman bath structure of the fourth century A.D. whose remains were reused to construct the Medieval church of San Lorenzo.

name: Vacone Roman villa
description: A Roman villa site near Rieti known initially from several cryptoporticus structures. More recent excavations have revealed a series of rooms, including some with mosaic pavement.

name: Vagnari
description: A villa, vicus, and necropolis site in modern Puglia, Italy, located near the modern city of Gravina. The site was active from the first to fourth centuries CE.

name: Valbandon Roman Villa(s)
description: Architectural remains consistent with a Roman-era villa maritima have been identified on both the north and south sides of a small bay at modern Valbandon in Croatia. Originally thought to represent a single establishment that wrapped the entire bay, more recent interpretations would see two separate architectural complexes.

name: Valdonega Roman villa
description: The Roman villa of Valdonega near Verona dates to the first century A.D.

name: Vale de Aguieiro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Vale de Aguieiro

name: Vale de Aguilhão
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Vale de Aguilhão

name: Vale de Mértola
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Vale de Mértola

name: Valerio Messala Corvino
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Valerio Messala Corvino

name: Variacum?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Variacum?

name: Vatsa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B5 Vatsa

name: Vendres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A3 Vendres

name: Vendresse
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Vendresse

name: Ventrelli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Ventrelli

name: Ver-lès-Chartres
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Ver-lès-Chartres

name: Veranes
description: A Roman villa.

name: Verneuil-en-Halatte
description: The Gallo-Roman villa is located at Bufosse in the northern part of the commune Verneuil-en-Halatte département Oise.

name: Veroli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Veroli

name: Vettianus Fundus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 I1 Vettianus Fundus

name: Vettweiss-Froitzheim
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Vettweiss-Froitzheim

name: Via delle Piagge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 Via delle Piagge

name: Via Pollenza Roman villa
description: A Roman villa complex that was occupied beginning in the first century BCE.

name: Vicarello
description: A large, Roman imperial estate covering ca. 2,000 ha on the shores of Lake Bracciano. The name may derive from Vicus Aurelius, suggesting a connection with Marcus Aurelius.

name: Vicus Antoniae Saturninae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E4 Vicus Antoniae Saturninae

name: Vieux-Rouen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Vieux-Rouen

name: Vigna Bevilacqua
description: In 1834, Edward Dodwell documented a substantial tract of polygonal masonry walling at Bevilacqua near Frascati that was thought to be associated with a Roman villa platform.

name: Vigna Spinabella
description: A Roman villa located at Vigna Spinabella and included in the Barrington Atlas. In the Barrington Atlas directory, this place is included as a "Numbered Feature" and not given a specific title. It is assigned number 44 on BAtlas map 43 C2.

name: Vignale
description: A Roman villa, mansio, and Late Antique settlement along the Via Aemilia Scauri.

name: Vila Cardílio
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Vila Cardílio

name: Vila Mogorjelo
description: A Roman villa located in Čapljina, Bosnia and Herzegovina.

name: Vila Verde
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A3 Vila Verde

name: Vilarenc
description: The villa dates from the first century B.C. to the third century A.D. when it was transformed into a bath complex. It was partially reclaimed as a villa in late Roman and Medieval times.

name: Vilauba Roman Villa
description: A Roman and Visigothic villa located in the modern town of Camós in Girona province, Cataluña, Spain.

name: Villa Aldobrandini
description: The Villa Aldobrandini is located on the outskirts of Frascati. The current villa of the site was built in 1550 by a Vatican prelate named Alessandro Rufini. Subsequently, Pope Clement VIII gave the villa to his nephew Cardinal Pietro Aldobrandini in 1598. The Papal villa and gardens occupy the site of a Roman terrace villa.

name: Villa Aniciorum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Villa Aniciorum

name: Villa Arcinelli
description: The remains of a Roman platform villa, the first phase of which belongs to the first century BCE.

name: Villa Arianna
description: The so-called "Villa Arianna" at Stabiae is typically considered one of the earliest villas at the site and is often dated to the second century B.C.

name: Villa at Foz de Lumbier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 no. 121 (Foz de Lumbier (Liédena))

name: Villa Augustea
description: The so-called “Villa of Augustus” at Somma Vesuviana was first discovered in the late nineteenth century. The villa has been shown to have survived the 79 CE eruption of Mt. Vesuvius but was eventually destroyed by a volcanic event in 472.

name: Villa Cardillio
description: Remains of a Roman villa located in the modern Torres Novas Municipality, Santarém District, Portugal.

name: Villa Carmiano
description: A villa rustica of about 400 square meters that was discovered at Stabiae in 1963. The site was reinterred in 1998.

name: Villa Collatino
description: Remains of a Roman villa discovered in the Collatino archaeological park in Via Grotta di Gregna consisting of 14 rooms, a cistern, and channelization for water works.

name: Villa Commedia
description: A villa on Lake Como described by Pliny the Younger. Scholars have long thought it must be located in the vicinity of modern Lierna, and 19th and 20th century finds of floor mosaics and other elements have led to conjectures that the villa must have been located either in the peninsular "Castello" neighborhood or in the core of the old town, which has long been known as "Villa" because of this conjecture. A mosaic from the latter area is now conserved in the Archaeological Museum of Lecco.

name: Villa Cuomo
description: The so-called Villa Cuomo in the modern Italian comune of Sant'Antonio Abate in greater metropolitan Naples is a Roman villa rustica of the first century CE.

name: Villa de Río Verde
description: A Roman villa with mosaic pavements located in the modern Spanish province of Malaga.

name: Villa de Santa Catarina de Sítimos
description: A Roman villa of the imperial period, in use from the first through fifth centuries A.D.

name: Villa degli Antonini
description: The so-called 'Villa of the Antonines', located near Genzano di Roma in the Alban Hills, lies just south of the Via Appia between the road's 18th and 19th milestones.

name: Villa dei Casoni
description: The Villa dei Casoni is sometimes referred to as the Villa di Varrone, based on some scholarly assumptions that it once belonged to Marcus Terentius Varro. This late republican to early imperial platform villa is located near Poggio Mirteto, Montopoli di Sabina, Italy.

name: Villa dei Quattro Venti
description: A monumental villa complex that is known as the “Villa dei Quattro Venti” is situated on a south-eastern offshoot of the Circeo promontory, approximately 100 meters southeast of the historic center of S. Felice Circeo.

name: villa del Cavalcavia di Salone
description: The so-called "Villa of the Jurist" was discovered in 2013 during salvage excavations connected with the expansion of Italy's A24 motorway. A significant villa of the Late Republican period was documented and attributed to the jurist Quintus Mucius Scaevola.

name: Villa del Cimitero Flaminio
description: A Roman villa located at the 1.6 km post of the via Tiberina, positioned on a tuff bank, today inside the Prima Porta cemetery.

name: Villa del Cortijo de los Vila at Alameda
description: Roman villa at Alameda, located near the modern town cemetery. It is also known as the “Finca del Moral”.

name: Villa del fondo Ippolito Zurlo
description: A Roman villa located in the contrada Giuliana at Boscoreale on a side street of via Grotta II. The site was excavated by Vincenzo De Prisco in 1897 and subsequently reinterred.

name: Villa del Pastore
description: A large villa at Stabiae covering more than 17,000 square meters.

name: Villa del Torraccio, Torrenova
description: The Forma Italiae notes the presence of drainage channels, foundations, and a cistern that are taken to be indicative of a Roman villa in the locale known today as Torrenova in the eastern portion of modern Rome.

name: Villa del Vergigno, Montelupo Fiorentino
description: A large villa rustica inhabited from ca. 100 B.C. to A.D. 500, located near Montelupo Fiorentino in the mid-Arno Valley of northern Tuscany, approximately twenty km west of Florence.

name: Villa dell'Incastro
description: An ancient villa in Guidonia Montecelio, Lazio.

name: Villa della Longarina
description: The ruins of a Roman villa rustica located near modern Giardino di Roma, just north of Via Cristoforo Colombo southwest of Rome.

name: Villa della Pisanella
description: A Roman villa rustica at Boscoreale that was first discovered in 1868 by Modestino Pulzella. The villa is best known as the findspot of the so-called "Boscoreale Treasure". Following excavation, the remains of the villa were reinterred.

name: Villa delle Colonne
description: A late Republican and early Imperial villa in the ager Cosanus noted for its turreted enclosure wall.

name: Villa delle Grotte at Grottarossa
description: A villa with beginnings in the early Republican period, continuing in use to the middle Imperial period.

name: Villa delle Terme degli Stucchi Dipinti
description: An imperial villa, perhaps abandoned in the late first century A.D., located near Tor Vergata.

name: Villa dels Munts
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 no. 110 (Els Munts (Altafulla))

name: Villa di Casale Ghella
description: A villa complex at the eighth mile of the Via Cassia, to which it was linked by means of a paved diverticulum. The first phase dates to the first century BCE and the villa remained in use until the Late Antique period.

name: Villa di Faonte a Vigne Nuove
description: The so-called the Villa of Faonte was a Roman villa in Rome located between Via delle Vigne Nuove and Via Passo del Turchino. There are remains of a cistern (approx. 29.5 by 14 meters) now set in a garden area.

name: Villa di Faragola
description: A Late Roman villa site near Ausculum.

name: Villa di Formello
description: A Roman villa dating ca. 50 B.C. located near Monte Gennaro.

name: Villa di Fralana
