description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Gasr el-Gezira

name: Gasr Zeitun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr Zeitun

name: Geran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Geran

name: Geronisos
description: A small island lying off the west coast of Cyprus where a sanctuary has been identified.

name: Ġgantija
description: Megalithic temple on the island of Gozo; named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980

name: Ghulbiyan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 F3 Ghulbiyan

name: Giado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Giado

name: Gilan Gharbi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 A2 Gilan Gharbi

name: Glale
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Glale

name: Gola di S. Andrea, Itri
description: Located along the course of the Via Appia between Fondi and Itri are the remains of a likely sanctuary of Apollo that dates to the fourth and third century BCE.

name: Görresburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Görresburg

name: Görtschach
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Görtschach

name: Great Temple of Hera
description: The Great Temple of Hera, or Polycrates Temple, is the third temple constructed at the Heraion of Samos between 540 and 530 BCE.

name: Great Temple of Petra
description: The Great Temple of Petra is one of the major archaeological and architectural components of central Petra. It is located to the south of the Colonnaded Street and southeast of the Temenos Gate and covers 7560 square meters.

name: Greek Temple at Taras
description: A Doric temple dating to the sixth century B.C., located in the modern piazza Castello in Taranto, Italy. It was initially attributed to Poseidon, but may have been dedicated to a different deity.

name: Gula temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Gula. Although its location is not known, the Gula temple at Kalhu is thought to have been part of the Ninurta temple complex (in the northwest corner of the citadel), east of the Šarrat-niphi temple and north of the Kidmuri temple.

name: Gur-i Dukhtar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Gur-i Dukhtar

name: Gura Dobrogei
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 F4 Gura Dobrogei

name: Gyroulas
description: A Late Archaic rural sanctuary of Demeter and Apollo at Gyroulas on Naxos.

name: Hajjiabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Hajjiabad

name: Hapsasieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hapsasieion

name: Harlow
description: Harlow is the site of a pre-Roman and Roman sanctuary.

name: Harran el-Awamid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 E3 Harran el-Awamid

name: Hathor Temple, Timna Valley
description: A shrine sacred to Hathor connected with the Egyptian mining settlement that was extracting copper in the Timna Valley.

name: Hecate, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hecate, T.

name: Hekateion
description: The Hekateion is a monumental temple for the goddess Hekate constructed in Lagina ca. 125 BCE.

name: Henchir Krebita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Krebita

name: Henchir Oum el-Abbas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Oum el-Abbas

name: Henchir Oum Iala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 Henchir Oum Iala

name: Henchir Snem
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Henchir Snem

name: Hephaistion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 D5 Hephaistion

name: Hera temple
description: The so-called 'Tavole Palatine' is the site of the ruins of a sixth century B.C. Doric temple sacred to Hera and associated with the city of Metapontum.

name: Herakles, T.
description: A sanctuary of Herakles located on the island of Lemnos, active from the Archaic period through to the time of the Roman empire.

name: Hermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 D3 Hermai

name: Hermel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Hermel

name: Hibbariye
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Hibbariye

name: Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.

name: Hochscheid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H3 Hochscheid

name: Hockwold
description: A Romano-Celtic temple site dating between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Hosion kai Dikaion, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C3 Hosion kai Dikaion, T.

name: Housesteads Mithraeum
description: Temple of Mithras at Roman fort of Housesteads (Vercovicium)

name: Husn Niha
description: Husn Niha is the site of four Roman temples that were constructed between the first and third centuries AD.

name: Husn Sfiri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Husn Sfiri

name: Hyperteleaton
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Hyperteleatas, partially excavated in 1885 and 1968. In ancient literature the sanctuary is only mentioned by Pausanias, in whose time it was called the Hyperteleaton and was dedicated to Asclepius. The site is located south of Phoiniki in the southeastern Peloponnese at a place known in the 19th century called Chasanaga.

name: Idomene Minor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Idomene Minor

name: Ionic stoa
description: An Ionic stoa with four dining rooms on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens, built ca. 420 BC. It may have been part of the Asklepieion complex.

name: Ishtar Temple at Mari
description: Temple dedicated to the goddess Ishtar located in the western section of the city of Mari.

name: Italic temple of Castel di Ieri
description: The remains of an Italic temple, in the locality known as "Pié di Francia". The sanctuary dates from the fourth to first centuries B.C.

name: Iton?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D2 Iton?

name: Iuppiter Urius, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Iuppiter Urius, T.

name: Jandial Temple
description: Jandial is the site of a semi-Classical temple of Ionic Greek design constructed in the first or second centuries BCE. It is the farthest east of any known Hellenic temple.

name: Jouima
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C2 Jouima

name: Jvari
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Jvari

name: Kai Ka'us
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Kai Ka'us

name: Kalabak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Kalabak

name: Kalhu ziggurat
description: The temple-tower of the god Ninurta, which was started by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) and completed by his son and successor Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC), is still the most prominent feature of Kalhu (modern Nimrud). This multi-staged ziggurat, whose remains are now a pyramid of eroding mud-brick, was constructed in the northwest corner of the citadel, next to Kalhu's principal sanctuary, the Ninurta temple.

name: Kalogeros
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Kalogeros

name: Karthaia, Athena Temple
description: A Doric-order temple, hexastyle in antis (23.20x11.10 m). The building utilizes stone from both Aegina and Paros.

name: Kasnakovo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 C6 Kasnakovo

name: Kástron Khóstion
description: A complex of structures dating to the Greek and Roman period, with ceramic evidence of prior occupation of the vicinity, on a spur of Palióvouno, overlooking a valley leading to the bay of Saránda on the Korinthian Gulf. The excavators associate this site with the attested classical city of Khorsiai on the basis of epigraphic evidence recovered at the site.

name: Kazerun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Kazerun

name: Kelisaye Espakho
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 97 B2 Kelisaye Espakho

name: Kerlescan alignments
description: A complex of 555 megaliths arranged in 13 rows.

name: Khayrabad Bala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Khayrabad Bala

name: Khentiamentiu, Temple of
description: An unexcavated Old Kingdom temple, attested by epigraphic finds at Abydos in Egypt. Its remains are presumed to underlie later Osiris sanctuary structures and part of the modern town.

name: Khirbet el-Bilaas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet el-Bilaas

name: Khirbet el-Knese
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Khirbet el-Knese

name: Khirbet et-Tannur
description: Located some 70 km from Petra, Khirbet et-Tannur is the site of a well-preserved, open-air Nabataean sanctuary.

name: Khirbet Semrin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet Semrin

name: Khontro Bigli
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Khontro Bigli

name: Khorsabad ziggurat
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple-tower built on the palace terrace of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). The ziggurat was located at the northern end of the temple sector, which was accessible from Sargon’s palace, through the Courtyard XV.

name: Khortata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Khortata

name: Khrysovitsa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Khrysovitsa

name: Khurramdasht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Khurramdasht

name: Kiboreia
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Kiboreia

name: Kidmuri Temple
description: The bīt Kidmuri is one of the temples located in the citadel of Nineveh. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. Although the temple's exact location remains unknown, it is generally believed to have been between the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš) and the Citadel Gate (East Gate).

name: Kidmuri temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Kidmuri (probably a collective name for a group of gods).

name: Kiosk of Qertassi
description: The so-called Kiosk of Qertassi is an unfinished Roman kiosk that may date to the reign of Trajan. Originally located at Qertassi, the remains of the temple were relocated to New Kalabsha in Southern Egypt as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia in the twentieth century.

name: Kiratah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Kiratah

name: Klaros
description: The site of a temple of Apollo administered by Colophonians and active from the seventh century B.C. to the fourth century A.D.

name: Kobosh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Kobosh

name: Koçak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Koçak

name: Koi Krylgan Kala
description: Koi Krylgan Kala is an archaeological site in Uzbekistan. At the site are the remains of a temple complex associated with the Chorasmian Dynasty, which was destroyed approximately 200 BC.

name: Kolona
description: An archaeological site on the island of Aegina that shows multiple settlement phases from the Bronze Age to the Classical period.

name: Kom el-Nana
description: Area south of the main city of Amarna that was the site of a temple during the Amarna period. The structure was overbuilt by a Christian monastery in late antiquity.

name: Komosarue
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 M2 Komosarue

name: Kordin III
description: Megalithic temple in Malta constructed during the Mġarr phase (ca. 3700 BCE) and abandoned ca. 2500 BCE.

name: Kordin Temples
description: A group of megalithic temples on Malta constructed during the Neolithic period.

name: Krounoi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B4 Krounoi

name: Ksar Djaria
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Ksar Djaria

name: Kubaniya
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 inset Kubaniya

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Kuh-i Ayyub

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Kuh-i Shahrak

name: Late Mithraeum
description: Final phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Laveyron

name: Lloret
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Lloret

name: London Mithraeum
description: A temple sacred to Mithras in Londinium that was discovered in 1954. The visible remains of the temple do not sit on the original site, but have been reassembled at Temple Court, Queen Victoria Street, London.

name: Los Cuadriales
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Los Cuadriales

name: Lousoi
description: A settlement in Azania, located near modern Kato Lusi, that has been under excavation since the late 19th century.

name: Luxor Temple
description: An ancient Egyptian temple that was initially built ca. 1400 BCE.

name: Lydney
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Lydney

name: Lydney Roman temple
description: A fourth century AD temple dedicated to Nodens.

name: Madhukeshwara temple
description: A Shiva temple at Banavasi in India whose foundation may date as early as the 7th century CE.

name: Magoula
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Magoula

name: Magoula Kastri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C1 Magoula Kastri

name: Mahabodhi Temple
description: One of the four main Buddhist pilgrimage sites, identified as the place where Gautama Buddha attained Enlightenment. The first temple on the site was built by Emperor Asoka in the third century B.C., and the present temple dates from the fifth or sixth centuries. It is one of the earliest Buddhist temples built entirely in brick, still standing in India, from the late Gupta period. The site was largely abandoned from the twelfth-eighteenth centuries. It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002.

name: Maimeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Maimeh

name: Maison Carrée
description: A well-preserved Roman temple built ca. 16 B.C. and dedicated to Caius and Lucius Caesar.

name: Malchittu nuragic temple
description: A sacred structure with an 'in antis' plan located near Arzachena in Sardinia.

name: Malla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Malla

name: Manglisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Manglisi

name: Maqam er-Rab
description: A peripteral temple built of basalt that includes an altar to Nemesis. Epigraphic data provides a chronology of late second to third centuries A.D.

name: Maqsudabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Maqsudabad

name: Marathos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A4 Marathos

name: Marino Mithraeum
description: A Mithraic shrine of unusual size discovered at modern Marino, Italy in 1963.

name: Markesi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Markesi

name: Mars, Aedes
description: A temple sacred to Mars located on the Via Appia. The shrine was located between the first and second milestones and was dedicated in the fourth century BC. According to Livy, Titus Quinctius dedicated the temple on 1 June 387 BC.

name: Mavrovounoi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Mavrovounoi

name: Mdoukha
description: Roman sanctuary with two small temples.

name: Mechel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D3 Mechel

name: Medinet Habu
description: The New Kingdom mortuary temple of Ramesses III at Medinet Habu, on the West bank of the Nile near Luxor.

name: Megaloi Theoi (sanctuary, Imbros)
description: A sanctuary of the "Great Gods" on the Aegean island of Imbros/Gökçeada. The remains of the sanctuary are located southwest of modern Kaleköy, to the west of the airport in the vicinity of a ruined monastery of Agios Konstantinos at a place formerly known as Roxado.

name: Menelaion
description: An ancient Greek sanctuary of Helen and Menelaos near ancient Therapnai in Greece.

name: Mercurius, Aedes
description: A temple dedicated to Mercury and Maia that was located on the slopes of the Aventine Hill overlooking the Circus Maximus.

name: Meroe
description: Meroë is an ancient city located on the east bank of the Nile river near Shendi, Sudan. The city was a center of the kingdom of Kush.

name: Meter Theon 1, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Meter Theon 1, T.

name: Meter Theon 2, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Meter Theon 2, T.

name: Meter Theon, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A5 Meter Theon, T.

name: Metokhi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C5 Metokhi

name: Metroon at Olympia
description: Temple at Olympia constructed in the late 4th or early 3rd century BCE

name: Mezgura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D3 Mezgura

name: Mézin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F1 Mézin

name: Middle Mithraeum
description: Second phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Middle Stoa
description: The Middle Stoa, the largest structure in the Agora of Athens, was built after 183 BC, as the north side of the South Square. In the Augustan Period, the Odeon of Agrippa was built on its north side. It was destroyed during the Herulian Sack of 267 AD and used as building material for the Post-Herulian Wall.

name: Military Zeus Temple
description: Small temple with pitched roof presumably dedicated to Zeus, located in the military campus zone Block A1 at Dura-Europos.

name: Minatiacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Minatiacum

name: Minerva temple at Punta della Campanella
description: An ancient temple of Minerva, located on the ancient cape that also was known by the goddess' name.

name: Minor Acropolis, Norba
description: The so-called Minor Acropolis is located on the hill to the south of the Porta Maggiore. This area is surrounded by the urban enclosure wall and is terraced in polygonal masonry. Two platform temples of unknown assignation are to be found here.

name: Mithraeum at Mundelsheim
description: A second-century Mithraeum is located in the area of the municipality of Mundelsheim in the Ludwigsburg district of Baden-Württemberg, Germany.

name: Mithraeum at Riegel
description: A temple once sacred to Mithras located in Riegel am Kaiserstuhl (Baden-Wuerttemberg, Germany).

name: Mithraeum at Segontium
description: A mithraeum was excavated at Segontium (modern Caernarfon) in 1959, ca. 137m from the east corner of the Roman auxiliary fort. The site was abandoned in the late fourth century A.D.

name: Mithraeum at Vindovala
description: A temple sacred to Mithras located at the Roman fort of Vindovala (Rudchester) on Hadrian's Wall.

name: Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander
description: The Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander located in ancient Ostia dates to the third century CE.

name: Mnajdra Megalithic Temple (Malta)
description: Megalithic temple complex of the fourth millennium BC.

name: Möhn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Möhn

name: Mollis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 B2 Mollis

name: Mont-Rivel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C3 Mont-Rivel

name: Monte Torre Maggiore
description: An Italic temple complex in Umbria of the first millennium B.C. that includes the so-called "Ara maior".

name: Montreuil-Bellay
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E1 Montreuil-Bellay

name: Mortuary Temple of Amenhotep III
description: Temple constructed in the Theban Necropolis during the 18th Dynasty.

name: Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut
description: The Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahari was built for the Eighteenth dynasty Pharaoh Hatshepsut.

name: Mortuary temple of Mentuhotep II
description: The Mortuary temple of the Eleventh Dynasty pharaoh Mentuhotep II at Deir el-Bahari would inspire the later mortuary complexes of Hatshepsut and Thutmose III that are also found at the site.

name: Mortuary Temple of Merenptah
description: Mortuary temple of the Nineteenth Dynasty in the Theban Necropolis.

name: Mortuary Temple of Senwosret III
description: Mortuary temple constructed for Senwosret III, a pharaoh of the 12th Dynasty in Egypt.

name: Msellata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Msellata

name: Muk
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Muk

name: Musawwarat
description: A Meroitic temple complex in modern Sudan, located 180 km northeast of Khartoum, 20 km north of Naqa and approximately 25 km south-east of the Nile. The complex dates to the early Meroitic period (third century B.C.). In 2011 the site was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Nabû temple (Khorsabad)
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple to the god Nabû, the god of scribes, built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Building H is the largest temple in Sargon’s newly-constructed city and it was built on a platform in the western side of the citadel, rather than on the palace terrace. The Nabû temple was connected to the temple district, which formed part of Sargon’s royal residence, by a stone bridge.

name: Nagos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C4 Nagos

name: Naisar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Naisar

name: Naqsh-i Bahram
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B3 Naqsh-i Bahram

name: Narmouthis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Narmouthis

name: Natanz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Natanz

name: Naudaran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Naudaran

name: Navish
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 F2 Navish

name: Nebi Safa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Nebi Safa

name: Nekromanteion
description: An ancient sanctuary of necromancy sacred to Hades and Persephone located on the banks of the Acheron River in Epirus, near the ancient city of Ephyra.

name: Nettleton
description: A significant cult center at Nettleton Shrub in Wiltshire was dedicated to Apollo Cunomaglus.

name: Neuvy-sur-Barangeon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H1 Neuvy-sur-Barangeon

name: New Year's Festival House at Assur
description: As part of his post-689-BC religious reforms, the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib (704–681 BC) had a new New Year’s festival house (akītu-house) constructed outside the western wall of Assur. This was an attempt to replicate the cultic topography of Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial names of that holy building and its cella are Eabbaugga ("House Where Tiāmat Is Put To Death") and Edubdubabba ("House That Makes Tiāmat Tremble”) respectively.

name: Neyrac-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Neyrac-les-Bains

name: Nihata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Nihata

name: Ningal temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the goddess Ningal.

name: Ninurta temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. The most important of those was dedicated to the god Ninurta, the patron deity of city, which Ashurnasirpal had constructed between his palace and the ziggurat, a building also consecrated to Ninurta.

name: Ninurta temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ninurta.

name: Nubian Lion Temple at Naqa
description: The Temple of Apedemak, also known as the Lion Temple, is located to the west of the temple of Amun.

name: Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon

name: Odry
description: The modern Polish city of Odry is the site of a major "stone circles" cemetery of the iron age Wielbark Culture.

name: Older Parthenon
description: A large temple on the Acropolis of Athens, begun in the 480s BC and destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, while still under construction. Parts of the structure were incorporated into the north wall of the Acropolis and other structures in the mid-fifth century BC and the Parthenon was built on the same site, using some of the older structure's foundations, beginning in 447 BC.

name: Orsingen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 Orsingen

name: Osireion
description: The Osireion at Abydos is located to the rear of the temple of Seti I. It is an integral part of Seti I's funeral complex and was built to resemble an 18th Dynasty Valley of the Kings tomb.

name: Pagans Hill Roman Temple
description: A Romano-British temple excavated on Pagans Hill at Chew Stoke, Somerset.

name: Palaio Pretorio
description: The ruins of a colonnaded structure in marble, perhaps a temple; a well / cistern is also observed by Sealy.

name: Panetelle
description: A sanctuary with votive deposits dating to the Hellenistic period at Panetelle di Mondragone.

name: Panóias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D3 Panóias

name: Paptoulis?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Paptoulis?

name: Parembole?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Parembole?

name: Parthenon
description: The temple of Athena Parthenos located on the Acropolis of Athens, which was constructed at the height of Athenian power between 447 and 432 BCE. It is the largest Doric temple in mainland Greece.

name: Passy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H2 Passy

name: Patriacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 Patriacum

name: Pecë
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B2 Pecë

name: Pelm
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Pelm

name: Per-Ptah
description: The site of Per-Ptah, meaning "House of Ptah", lies some 90 km south of Aswan and received a temple dedicated to pharaoh Ramesses II. Some of the architectural and sculptural remains of the temple were dismantled and relocated to the site of New Kalabsha in the 1960s during the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Pera Milous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Pera Milous

name: Pergamos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Pergamos

name: Pescara
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Pescara

name: Petit-Mars
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 D1 Petit-Mars

name: Phanai
description: A large ancient sanctuary of Apollo, located on the southwest coast of the island of Chios.

name: Philae
description: An ancient island located just above the First cataract of the Nile River that was flooded by the construction of the Aswan High Dam. The island was home to a multi-period temple complex, which was moved to the nearby island of Agilkia and declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the 1970s.

name: Phokikon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Phokikon

name: Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C4 Pithiviers-le-Vieil

name: Porto Cesareo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 G4 Porto Cesareo

name: Portonaccio sanctuary at Veii
description: The extraurban sanctuary of Minerva at Portonaccio is an important Etruscan cult center. The temple dates ca. 510 B.C. and was adorned with acroterial sculptures, notably the famous "Apollo of Veii".

name: Poseidon Phytalmios?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A4 Poseidon Phytalmios?, T.

name: Poseidon Porthmios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E4 Poseidon Porthmios, T.

name: Poseidon?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Poseidon?, T.

name: Poseidon?, T.
description: Poseidon?, T. a sanctuary of Poseidon on the island of Samos.

name: Potamos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F2 Potamos, T.

name: Pouzauges
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E2 Pouzauges

name: Prdejci
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B2 Prdejci

name: Pre-Erechtheion
description: A small temple on the Acropolis of Athens that might have existed from the 470s B.C. until replaced by the Erechtheion, perhaps in the 420s B.C. It might have hosted the cult of Athena Polias and/or Erechtheus.

name: Preah Khan
description: Temple at Angkor dedicated in 1191 CE under Jayavarman VII. The temple entered a period of decline beginning in the 15th century.

name: Preah Ko
description: Temple constructed in the city of Hariharalaya, dedicated in 880 CE by the Khmer king Indravarman I.

name: Prepelaion?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Prepelaion?

name: Prinias (Patela)
description: An Iron Age settlement on the Patela plateau north of the modern village of Prinias; its ancient name is uncertain. The site is notable for its occupation from the end of the Bronze Age through to the Archaic period, as well as for the monumental architecture and Orientalizing sculpture of its Buildings ('Temples') A and B.

name: Psaropyrgos
description: An ancient temple of Poseidon, known today as the "Tomb of Homer", located on a height at the northern end of the Aegean island of Ios. Some funerary epigraphy has also been noted at the site.

name: Punta della Vipera Etruscan temple
description: A coastal Etruscan sanctuary that dates ca. 540-530 B.C. with additional architectural phases dating to the fourth through second centuries B.C.

name: Pusht-i Arisht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Pusht-i Arisht

name: Pyra
description: Pyra is a place with preserved ruins of a Doric temple dating to the third century B.C. The sources connect Pyra with the pyre of the hero Herakles.

name: Pyramid-complex of Senusret III
description: Funerary complex of King Senusret III at Dahshur including a pyramid.

name: Pyrgi
description: Pyrgi is an ancient Etruscan port city on the Tyrrhenian coast of Italy. A Roman colony was established at the site by 191 B.C. In 1964, the bilingual 'Pyrgi Plaques' were discovered during the course of excavations.

name: Qadamgah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 D4 Qadamgah

name: Qadboun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B3 Qadboun

name: Qafr Zebad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Qafr Zebad

name: Qalaat Faqra
description: A Roman-era sanctuary complex in Lebanon with multiple altars and two temples. On the basis of epigraphic evidence, one of these temples seems to have been dedicated to Atargatis and the other possibly to Zeus Beelgalassos. There is some evidence for worship also of Zeus Heliopolis and for the Roman imperial cult. A Byzantine basilica was later built on the site.

name: Qanat-i Bagh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Qanat-i Bagh

name: Qasr el-Aguz
description: Site in Luxor (Egypt) associated with a Ptolemaic temple dedicated to the god Thoth.

name: Qasr el-Banat
description: The remains of an ancient temple at Qasr el Banat, Lebanon,

name: Qasr el-Sagha temple
description: Temple located north of Lake Qarun in the Fayyum Depression. Tentatively dated to the Old Kingdom period, the temple may have been dedicated to the god Sobek.

name: Qasr Naous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Qasr Naous

name: Qasr Neba
description: Qasr Neba is the site of a Roman temple in the Bekaa valley of Lebanon.

name: Qasr Nemrud
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Qasr Nemrud

name: Quarrush
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 Quarrush

name: Raiano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Raiano

name: Ramappa Temple
description: A thirteenth-century temple that was built during the period of the Kakatiya ruler Ganapati Deva.

name: Ramesseum
description: The funerary temple of Ramesses II, built within the area known as the Ipet-isut, "The Most Selected of Places", along the Nile River.

name: Ras il-Wardija
description: A Punic-Roman sanctuary site located on the western coast of Gozo (Gaulos), Malta.

name: Re-Buried Hellenistic Temple and Workshops
description: Excavations on site of post office at 49 Ben Ami St. revealed Hellenistic remains, including a dedication to Zeus Soter on behalf of Antiochos VII by a courtier (SEG XIX 9094). This inscription was associated with Hellenistic architecture of a temple-like character. Nearby workshops also uncovered.

name: Red Hill Roman Temple
description: Site of a Roman temple and possible small town near the confluence of the rivers Soar and Trent near Ratclifffe-on-Soar, Nottinghamshire. Findspot of at least three curse tablets (defixiones).

name: Resras
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Resras

name: Ribemont-sur-Ancre
description: Ribemont-sur-Ancre is the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman sanctuary located on the right bank of the Ancre river, a tributary of the Somme. Roger Agache discovered the site in 1962. The site is vast in its area, covering some 80 ha.

name: Rijelat Umm Kubar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 C4 Rijelat Umm Kubar

name: Rijsbergen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E1 Rijsbergen

name: Roda
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 A2 Roda

name: Rogeban
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Rogeban

name: Roman Double Stoa
description: A two-aisled Doric stoa ran along the south side of the Panathenaic Way from the Stoa Basileios towards the Dipylon Gate, which contained shops. It was constructed over a long period beginning in the early second century BC and repeatedly modified. The building was destroyed in the Herulian Sack of 267 AD, rebuilt, damaged in the Gothic Sack of 396 AD, rebuilt again, and was finally destroyed in the Slavic Sack of 582 AD.

name: Roman Temple at Maarab
description: Site of a Roman temple overlooking Jounieh Bay

name: Roman temple of Córdoba
description: A pseudodipteral, hexastyle, Corinthian order temple begun under Claudius and completed under Domitian. The temple is likely sacred to the imperial cult.

name: Roman Temple of Évora
description: The Roman Temple of Évora, also referred to as the Temple of Diana, is a Roman temple of the first century A.D.

name: Roman temple of Vic
description: The Roman temple of Vic dates to the second century A.D.

name: Roman temple, Capo Mulini
description: The remains of an early Imperial Roman temple on Sicily's Cape Mulini.

name: Roman temples at Chieti
description: Structures thought to be temples set within a sacred area that includes a sacred well.

name: Rotunda of Galerius
description: A brick rotunda of the fourth century AD ordered by the emperor Galerius for use as either a tomb or temple; later incorporated into the Christian Church of Agios Georgios. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988

name: Rouvroy-les-Merles
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Rouvroy-les-Merles

name: Royal Stoa
description: The Stoa Basileios or Royal Stoa was an important Doric-order stoa originating in the sixth century BCE and located at the northwest corner of the Athenian Agora. It was the administrative seat of the magistrate known as the archon basileus ("king archon").

name: Ruus el-Aslab
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Ruus el-Aslab

name: Ruwwafah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 83 B4 Ruwwafah

name: S. Cristina d’Aro
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 S. Cristina d’Aro

name: S. Eulalia de Bóveda
description: Located some 16 km from Lugo, S. Eulalia de Bóveda is the location of a vaulted, three-aisled, underground building, likely dating to the middle Imperial period and functioning as a nymphaeum.

name: S. Giovanni in Galdo
description: Site of a small Samnitic sanctuary atop Colle Rimontato (709 m. a.s.l.)

name: S. Jorge
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 S. Jorge

name: Sacellum of the Augustales
description: The so-called "Sacellum of the Augustales" at Misenum is an architectural complex located near the theater that was first built during the Iulio-Claudian period and dedicated to the Imperial cult. The complex was renovated during the second century, at which time one Cassia Victoria re-dedicated the shrine in honor of her husband, L. Laecanius Primitivus, who was a priest of the Augustales during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

name: Sacred Area of S. Omobono
description: An archaeological area in the Forum Boarium comprising an ancient ritual precinct with twin temples ranging in date from the Archaic period through the time of the Roman Empire. The Medieval church dedicated to S. Omobono stands atop the remains of one of the two ancient temples.

name: Sail-sur-Couzan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B2 Sail-sur-Couzan

name: Saint-Plancard
description: The Chapel of Saint Jean is built atop a temple once dedicated to Iuppiter Optimus Maximus.

name: Saint-Sernin-sur-Rance
description: Remains of a rustic temple and a menhir were discovered in the early 1960s near Boutaran.

name: Salins-les-Thermes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Salins-les-Thermes

name: Salt-en-Donzy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C2 Salt-en-Donzy

name: Šamaš temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Šamaš.

name: Sanctuary of Aphrodite/Isis
description: A sanctuary on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to Aphrodite in the Classical period and associated with Isis from the late first century BC. A temple was built in the Hadrianic period.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo at Cyrene
description: A sanctuary at Cyrene dedicated to Apollo Karneios. The sanctuary was in use between the seventh century BC and the second century AD, and included shrines to other gods, including Artemis and Isis.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo at Halieis
description: The Sanctuary of Apollo at Halieis is a temple complex dedicated primarily to Apollo, constructed outside of the city of Halieis and dating between the seventh and fourth centuries BCE.

name: Sanctuary of Apollo Phanaios
description: A partially excavated artificial platform and temple at modern Kato Phana on the Greek island of Chios (ancient Phanai).

name: Sanctuary of Artemis Aristoboule
description: A small temple of Artemis Aristoboule ("of best deliberation") erected by Themistokles

name: Sanctuary of Asklepios at Athens
description: A major sanctuary complex located west of the Theatre of Dionysus Eleuthereus on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis.

name: Sanctuary of Athena Itonia
description: A pan-thessaliote sanctuary, located at modern Philia Karditsis in Greece.

name: Sanctuary of Fortuna Primigenia
description: The Sanctuary of Fortuna Primigenia at Praeneste is a terraced sanctuary of monumental proportions. The phase visible today dates to the late second century B.C.

name: Sanctuary of Iuno Gabina
description: The Sanctuary of Iuno Gabina at Gabii.

name: Sanctuary of Ptoan Apollo
description: Described by Pausanias, this sanctuary was located near Akraiphiai in Boeotia.

name: Sanctuary of Themis
description: A sanctuary of Themis (order, righteousness), located on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens.

name: Santa Maria Sopra Minerva
description: A first century B.C. temple dedicated to Minerva by Gneus Caestius and Titus Caesius Priscus, who were two of the Assisi's quattuorviri. The temple was converted to the church of Santa Maria Sopra Minerva in 1539.

name: Santalla de Bóveda
description: The Roman sanctuary of Santalla de Bóveda dates to the second through fourth centuries A.D. It is located in the parish of Santalla de Bóveda de Meralocated near Lugo. Discovered in 1926, the site is a protected national monument.

name: Santuario di Pyrgi
description: A sanctuary with twin temples located at the Etruscan port. The Pyrgi sanctuary was initially excavated by Massimo Pallottino in 1957. The so-called Pyrgi Plaques were discovered here in 1964.

name: São Miguel de Mota
description: Site of a temple dedicated to the Celtiberian/Lusitanian god Endovelicus.

name: Sar-i Pul
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 F2 Sar-i Pul

name: Sarapieion A
description: The earliest of the three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.

name: Sarapieion C at Delos
description: One of three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.

name: Šarrat-niphi temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Šarrat-niphi (an aspect of Ištar).

name: Satyrou Mnema
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 L2 Satyrou Mnema

name: Scanno
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 Scanno

name: Schiavi
description: Twin Italic temples located atop the Colle della Torre.

name: Sebetti temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Sebetti. The location of this building is not known.

name: Sebetti temple (Khorsabad)
description: A temple dedicated to the Sebetti, the divine seven, was excavated by Iraqi archaeologists in 1957. This temple at Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad) is located just west of the citadel, near Citadel Gate A and City Gate 7.

name: Second Temple, Jerusalem
description: The so-called "Second Temple" in Jerusalem stood on the northern edge of the Dome of the Rock and was destroyed in A.D. 70 during the Roman siege.

name: Seh Pa Aivan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Seh Pa Aivan

name: Septeuil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Septeuil

name: Serapeion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B2 Serapeion

name: Serapeum
description: The Serapeum in Ostia's Regio III was built between 123 and 126 CE thanks to private patronage.

name: Serapieion B at Delos
description: One of three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.

name: Serapis, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Serapis, T.

name: Sghedeida
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Sghedeida

name: Shanhur
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Shanhur

name: Shara Temple at Umma
description: Large temple at Tell Jokha/Umma dedicated to the Sumerian god Shara. The temple was constructed by king Šu-Suen of the Third Dynasty of Ur (2035-2027 BCE), and was used during the Ur III-Old Babylonian periods.

name: Shore Temple of Mahabalipuram
description: The so-called "Shore Temple" of Mahabalipuram is a granite-built tomb at the "Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram" UNESCO World Heritage Site. It dates to the eighth century CE.

name: Sidi-Mechrig
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D2 Sidi-Mechrig

name: Sillingy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Sillingy

name: Sîn temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the moon-god Sîn. Although its location is not known, the Sîn temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Sîn temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Sîn.

name: Sîn-Šamaš Temple
description: One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to the moon-god Sîn and the sun-god Šamaš. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources and its exact location remains unknown. The temple is generally believed to have been east of the temples of Ištar (Emašmaš) and Nabû (Ezida) and south of the North Palace (palace of Ashurbanipal).

name: Sipahiler
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 C2 Sipahiler

name: Sirenae, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F4 Sirenae, T.

name: Skydra Hellenistic Temple
description: A Hellenistic temple, possibly of Zeus, the foundation of which was excavated on the southern outskirts of the residential area of modern Skydra in Greece.

name: Smintheion
description: Homer mentions the temple of Apollo Smintheus as the shrine where Chryses was priest. The second-century B.C. remains of an Ionic sanctuary with an adjacent town are located in the southwestern Troad near modern Gülpinar.

name: So-called "Temple of Augustus"
description: A temple dedicated to Octavian by a Lucius Calpurnius. It replaced a temple of ca. 194 B.C. that had in turn been restored by Sulla.

name: So-called Oratory of Phalaris
description: A small, rectangular Greek temple-type building dated to the first century B.C. and located in the upper agora of Agrigento, on the Poggetto di San Nicola. The name "Oratory" comes from its later use as a Christian chapel.

name: So-called Temple 1 at Hattusa
description: Temple 1, also referred to as the "Great Temple", is located in the lower city of Hattusa. It dates to the thirteenth century B.C.

name: So-called Temple of Aphrodite
description: A temple located on the acropolis of Cyrene.

name: So-called Temple of Cybele
description: A recently discovered temple on the acropolis of Cyrene.

name: So-called Temple of the Dioscuri
description: A temple on the acropolis of Cyrene, probably built around the seventh century BCE.

name: Sobek temple, Qaşr Qārūn
description: A Ptolemaic temple to the god Sobek.

name: Sofuentes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 Sofuentes

name: Soleb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 A5 Soleb

name: Souma el Kiata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Souma el Kiata

name: Souma n'Ikhetteben
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Souma n'Ikhetteben

name: Soumat el Kheneg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 B2 Soumat el Kheneg

name: South Stoa I
description: South Stoa I is the modern name for the stoa that bounded the southern edge of the Agora of Athens from the late 5th century BC until the mid-2nd century BC, when it was demolished and replaced with South Stoa II. It contained rooms for communal dining and was probably the headquarters of the metronomoi and other officials.

name: South Stoa II
description: South Stoa II is the modern name for the stoa that formed the south side of the South Square of the Agora of Athens. It was built in the mid-second century BC, on the site of South Stoa I using material from the Square Peristyle, and destroyed in the Sullan Sack of Athens in 86 BC.

name: Southeast Stoa
description: The Southeast Stoa lined the east side of the Panathenaic Way at the southeast corner of the Athenian Agora, between the Library of Pantainos and the City Eleusinion. It was built in the mid-second century AD and incorporated into the Post-Herulian Wall after 267 AD.

name: Southeast temple of the Athenian Agora
description: The modern name for a temple built in the south-eastern corner of the Athenian Agora next to the Panathenaic Way in the early second century AD, using material from the Temple of Athena at Sounion. A colossal cult statue of a female deity was located inside. It was demolished in the late third century AD and used as building material for the Post-Herulian Wall.

name: Southwest Temple in the Agora of Athens
description: A tetrastyle Doric temple in the Athenian agora, built in the Augustan period using material from a Classical stoa at Thorikos and unidentified Hellenistic structures.

name: Soyans
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E4 Soyans

name: Sparta
description: Sparta was a prominent city-state (polis) of ancient Greece.

name: Spathari (temple)
description: A temple in Acarnania, located between Stratos (modern Sourovigli) and modern Lepenou. The temple seems to have been constructed in the Greek Archaic period on the site of an earlier Helladic settlement. The temple continued in operation into the Roman period.

name: Sphinx Temple
description: The Old Kingdom temple associated with the Great Sphinx of Giza and assigned to the reign of Khafre.

name: Sri Ahobila Narasimha Swamy Temple
description: Main temple in Ahobilam dedicated to the man-lion form of the Hindu god Vishnu built or expanded in the 14th century, sacked by the Mughals in 1578, then restored and expanded at various times up to the present day.

name: St-Alban-les-Eaux
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B1 St-Alban-les-Eaux

name: St-Andéol
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I4 St-Andéol

name: St-Aubin-sur-Gaillon
description: St-Aubin-sur-Gaillon was the site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.

name: St-Galmier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C2 St-Galmier

name: St-Gervais
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 D2 St-Gervais

name: St-Goussard
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G2 St-Goussard

name: St-Laurent-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B4 St-Laurent-les-Bains

name: St-Maurice-d’Ibie
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C5 St-Maurice-d’Ibie

name: St-Victor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 St-Victor

name: Ste-Abeille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Ste-Abeille

name: Ste-Barbe-du-Tlélat
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 E1 Ste-Barbe-du-Tlélat

name: Ste-Ruffine
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Ste-Ruffine

name: Stoa at Brauron
description: The Stoa at Brauron, within the larger Artemis Brauronia complex, housed dining spaces as well as votive offerings to the goddess Artemis. The Stoa was Π-shaped, wrapping around a courtyard that separated it from the temple of Artemis at Brauron, which it faced. Worship at the cult site began ca. the eighth century BCE. The site was abandoned after a flood in the third century BCE.

name: Stoa at Lokroi Epizephyrioi
description: A large U-shaped stoa dating to the seventh and sixth centuries BCE.

name: Stoa in the sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios
description: A 62 m long stoa, probably of the fourth century BC.

name: Stoa of Attalos
description: The Stoa of Attalos, located on the eastern side of the ancient Athenian agora, is a long, rectangular, two-story building of limestone and marble, with an external and internal colonnade. It was originally constructed around 150 BCE and then reconstructed in the 1950s.

name: Stoa of Eumenes
description: Eumenes II, king of Pergamon, built this eponymous stoa on the South slope of the Athenian Acropolis ca. 160 BCE. Its position lies between the Theater of Dionysus and the later Odeon of Herodes Atticus.

name: Stoa of Zeus (Eleutherios)
description: The Stoa of Zeus Eleutherios was built on the west side of the Agora of Athens between ca. 430-425 BC, in honor of Zeus "of Freedom"; the stoa, Doric in its external order, housed the shields of Athenians who had died fighting for the city.

name: Stoa Poikile
description: The Stoa Poikile or Painted Stoa, named for a set of paintings inside depicting Athenian military victories, was located in the northwest corner of the Agora of Athens and was the namesake of the Stoic philosophical school. It was built around 460 BC and remained in use until it was spoliated in the sixth century AD. Archaeological excavation has uncovered the western and eastern ends of the building.

name: Sun Temple of Niuserre
description: Temple dedicated to the god Ra in the 5th dynasty by the pharaoh Niuserre (Nyuserre).

name: Surviving colonnade of the imperial temple at Mérida
description: The incorrectly so-called "Temple of Diana."

name: Synagogue at Dura-Europos
description: The Dura-Europos synagogue is one of the oldest known synagogues in the world. According to an Aramaic inscription, its final phase of construction took place in A.D. 244. It was uncovered during excavations in 1932.

name: Ta Prohm
description: Khmer temple at Angkor constructed in 1186 CE under Jayavarman VII.

name: Tabard Square Temple Complex
description: A Romano-Celtic temple complex located near the junction of Stane Street and Watling Street in modern Southwark.

name: Tabo
description: A Kushite site located on Argo Island in Nubia, the location of a large Amun temple.

name: Takht-i Bilqis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 90 C4 Takht-i Bilqis

name: Tal-i Hakavan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Tal-i Hakavan

name: Tang-i Qandil
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Tang-i Qandil

name: Tang-i Sarvak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Tang-i Sarvak

name: Taq-i Gunbad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Taq-i Gunbad

name: Tarxien (Malta)
description: A megalithic temple complex that was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980. This temple dates to the later Neolithic period on Malta and remained in use for several millennia. The first usage of the site occurs ca. 4100 BC and the temple complex develops across the fourth millennium.

name: Taxiarkhis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Taxiarkhis

name: Tchonemyris
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Tchonemyris

name: Tekmoreioi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F5 Tekmoreioi

name: Telesterion
description: The Telesterion (ca. 435 - 421 B.C.) was a sacred hall at Eleusis used for the annual initiation ceremonies for the cult of Demeter and Persephone known as the Eleusinian Mysteries.

name: Telfes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Telfes

name: Tell F6 at Failaka
description: Tell on the southwestern corner of Failaka Island containing the remains of two large buildings, tentatively identified as a palace and a temple, dated to 1900-1400 BCE.

name: Tempelbezirk Pesch
description: A Gallo-Roman temple complex dated to the 1st-4th centuries CE.

name: Tempio della Tosse
description: The so-called "Tempio della Tosse" is an ancient Roman building along the river Anio. Its dating is problematic, but it is likely a building of late antiquity that is built on the site of an earlier villa. It has been interpreted as a nymphaeum, a temple, and also as a tomb.

name: Tempio Grande at Vulci
description: A major urban sanctuary at Vulci with a long period of use, stretching from the archaic period into the Roman period.

name: Temple 200 at Naga
description: Temple structure located south of the main Amun Temple complex at Naga, constructed during the reign of King Nebmaatre Amanakhareqerema (1st century CE).

name: Temple A
description: So-called "Temple A" is a structure of the third century B.C. and is likely the Temple of Juturna that was vowed by Caius Lutatius Catulus following his victory against the Carthaginians in 241 B.C. It was later converted into a church, the apse of which remains visible.

name: Temple at Santa Maria in Canale
description: A temple of Tuscan type with cella and lateral alae built of ashlar masonry and dated to the 3rd century BCE, now built into a rural church and farmstead complex. The temple is situated along the Via Amerina between Ameria and Tuder, and marks the boundary between those two Umbrian communities. A fragmentary Umbrian bronze inscription (Vetter 229) of around 300 BCE mentioning a deity characterized as "Iovia" was discovered here in the 18th century CE.

name: Temple B
description: A temple vowed by Q. Lutatius Catulus at the battle of Vercellae (30 June, 101 B.C.). The sanctuary is a tholos-type temple and was sometimes referred to as 'aedes Catuli'.

name: Temple C
description: The so-called "Temple C" of the Largo Argentina sacred area may be dedicated to the Italic goddess Feronia.

name: Temple C at Selinus
description: The so-called Temple C at Selinus is a Doric temple that dates to the second half of the sixth century B.C.

name: Temple complex at Kommos
description: Multi-phase temple site in the city of Kommos in southern Crete. Temple A was constructed by the late 11th century CE. A larger structure, Temple B, was built over it in the 9th century CE. The temple complex reached its maximum extent when Temple C was built over the site and its surroundings in the 7th century CE.

name: Temple D
description: The temple referred to as "Temple D" in the Largo Argentina is likely the Aedes Lares Permarini, sacred to the lares who protect sailors. The largest of the temples in this sacred area, "Temple D" dates to the second century B.C. and remains largely unexcavated.

name: Temple d'Appolon
description: The ruins of a Roman-period temple dedicated to Apollo located at the Oppidum de Gaujac (Gard, France).

name: Temple E at Selinus
description: A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, sacred to perhaps Hera or Dionysus, located on the eastern hill at Selinus.

name: Temple East of Lavinium
description: From the 5th century BC.

name: Temple F at Selinus
description: Temple F at Selinus is a Doric temple located on the site's so-called "East Hill". Dedicated either to Athena or Dionysus, the temple dates to the middle of the sixth century B.C. The non-canonical ground plan of 6 x 14 columns gives the temple an elongated aspect.

name: Temple G at Selinus
description: Remains of an octastyle Doric temple begun in the sixth century B.C. but left unfinished.

name: Temple in Block X7
description: Small temple located to the northeast inside the military sector at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of "Artemis Epikrateia"
description: A classical/hellenistic temple located in the likely territory of Acarnanian Alyzeia. The temple site lies over 6km southeast from the ancient walled city (about a kilometer south of modern Paliovarka, Greece) at a site known in modern times as Drymonas Archontochoriou. It is perched on rising ground with a view westward to the channel and the island of Karnos (modern Kalamos). Inscriptions have been recovered from the site, and an unlooted portion of a votive deposit was scientifically excavated in the 1990s.

name: Temple of Adonis
description: Temple located in block L5 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Al-Lat at Palmyra
description: A temple sacred to the goddess Al-Lat in Palmyra. The earliest shrine on the site was constructed in the mid-first century BCE, and was greatly expanded during the second century CE. The temple was closed and its altar destroyed during the 380s CE.

name: Temple of Amada
description: The Temple of Amada is the oldest Egyptian temple known in Nubia. Founded by Pharaoh Thutmose III (18th dynasty), the temple was dedicated to Amun and Re-Horakhty. The decorative program is attributed to Amenhotep II, his son and successor. The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Derr.

name: Temple of Amenhotep II
description: Amenhotep II built a temple at Giza that he dedicated to the god Horemakhet, a sun-god identified with the Great Sphinx. It lies northeast of the Sphinx itself.

name: Temple of Amenhotep, son of Hapu
description: A mortuary temple located in the Theban Necropolis that was dedicated to one Amenhotep, son of Hapu, an official in the court of the 18th Dynasty pharaoh Amenhotep III. Numerous roles are attributed to Amenhotep, son of Hapu, including those of priest, scribe, and architect. Likely he received special dispensation for the construction of the mortuary temple.

name: Temple of Amon (Karnak)
description: Middle Kingdom temple of Amon at the heart of the Karnak Temple Complex.

name: Temple of Amphiaraus
description: The Temple of Amphiaraus is an oracular temple dedicated to the hero/god Amphiaraus, located within the sanctuary of the Amphiareion. The site was believed to have healing powers and was particularly important in the Late Classical and Hellenistic periods (fifth to third centuries BCE).

name: Temple of Amun at Jebel Barkal
description: Large temple dedicated to the Amun located at the base of Jebel Barkal. Designated temple B500, the structure was likely constructed by the Napatan king Piankhi in the 8th century BCE.

name: Temple of Anu and Adad at Assur
description: A distinctive feature of the city of Assur is the double-temple of the gods Anu and Adad. This building, together with its twin ziggurats, was constructed no later than the Middle Assyrian period; it underwent a major rebuilding in the reign of Tiglath-pileser I (1114–1076 BC). The existence of an (unnamed) Adad temple at Assur, however, is known from inscriptions of the Old Assyrian rulers Erišum I and Ikūnum. The various parts of this axisymmetric, double-temple was given Sumerian names: Anu’s temple and ziggurat were called Ešaan and Emelamanna; and Adad’s temple and ziggurat went by the names Ešuddegeštuku and Egišlamšaršar.

name: Temple of Aphaia on Aegina
description: An ancient sanctuary on the island of Aegina, dedicated to the autochthonous deity Aphaia.

name: Temple of Aphlad
description: Temple building located in the southwest corner of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Aphrodite (Thessalonica)
description: The remains of a marble temple executed in the Ionic order.

name: Temple of Aphrodite Euploia
description: The temple of Aphrodite Euploia is a tholos constructed in the fourth century BC in Knidos, a coastal city on the Datça Peninsula. It was built to house Praxiteles' famous nude statue of Aphrodite.

name: Temple of Apollo
description: The Temple of Apollo (VII,7,32) in Pompeii is one of the city's oldest cults, dating to the sixth century BC. The sanctuary assumed its present appearance in the second century BC.

name: Temple of Apollo at Corinth
description: The second temple of Apollo at Corinth whose reconstructed remains are visible on the site today. The structure dates ca. 540 B.C.

name: Temple of Apollo at Delphi
description: The Temple of Apollo situated in the center of the oracular sanctuary of that god at Delphi. Visible remains today are a 20th century reconstruction of the temple rebuilt in the fourth century BCE following a destructive landslide (repaired and altered multiple times thereafter). The date and cause of the temple's eventual destruction sometime not earlier than late antiquity remain undetermined.

name: Temple of Apollo at Hierapolis
description: The Temple of Apollo at Hierapolis, built during the Hellenistic period.

name: Temple of Apollo at Metropolis
description: A peripteral Doric temple built in the mid-6th c. BCE and destroyed by fire in the 2nd c. BCE. The temple faces east, and was built of mudbrick on foundations of local sandstone, with sandstone column drums and a terracotta roof. A 4th c. BCE dedicatory inscription found in the cella attests the worship of Apollo. The structure was discovered in 1994 following the activity of looters.

name: Temple of Apollo at Syracuse
description: Temple of Apollo at Syracuse on the island of Ortygia.

name: Temple of Apollo at Thermos
description: A temple to the Apollo was built in the late seventh century B.C. atop the ruins of the previous megaron in Thermos.

name: Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros
description: The Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros at Eretria dates originally to the late archaic period.

name: Temple of Apollo Patroos
description: The Temple of Apollo Patroos on the west side of the Agora of Athens was built at the end of the fourth century BC. It contained a famous statue of Apollo by Euphranor. The structure wraps around the tiny fourth-century BC shrine of Zeus Phratrios and Athena Phratrios. The existence of an earlier temple, destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, is disputed.

name: Temple of Apollo Pythios
description: The Hellenistic Temple of Apollo Pythios on the Acropolis of the city of Rhodes.

name: Temple of Apollo Zoster
description: The Temple of Apollo Zoster is a sanctuary of the god Apollo located in Vouliagmeni in Attica, Greece. The temple's foundations date to the sixth century BC.

name: Temple of Ares
description: The sanctuary with a temple dedicated to Ares is located south of the Altar of the Twelve Gods in the Athenian Agora. The temple was originally erected at Pallene ca. 430 BC and sacred to Athena Pallenis at Pallene. The structure was moved to the Agora in the early first century.

name: Temple of Artemis (Aulis)
description: Archaic temple to Artemis at Aulis, Boeotia. In mythology, said to be the place of death of Iphigenia. Mentioned in Euripides' 'Iphigenia at Aulis.'

name: Temple of Artemis (Thasos)
description: Temple of Artemis on Thasos Island with remains from the Archaic period to the 1st century CE.

name: Temple of Artemis at Ephesus
description: The Temple of Artemis, the central element of the extramural sanctuary of Artemis at Ephesus, was considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

name: Temple of Artemis at Sardis
description: A large temple dedicated to Artemis at the city of Sardis in western Asia Minor. A sanctuary to Artemis was constructed on the site during the Persian Era (ca. 547-334 BCE), and construction on a new temple on the site began during the third century BCE. The temple was never fully completed. A small church was built on part of the site in the 5th century CE, but the structure declined from the 7th century onwards, and many of its stones were reused or burned.

name: Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona
description: The temple of a syncretized incarnation of Artemis built at Dura-Europos in the Hellenistic period.

name: Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona Phase 1 (Dura-Europos)
description: Archaeological phase (c. 13-160 CE) of the Temple at Dura-Europos dedicated to Artemis Azzanathkona, probably the tutelary deity of the Euphrates island settlement Anath.

name: Temple of Artemis Azzanathkona Phase 2
description: Temple at Dura-Europos dedicated to Artemis Azzanathkona, tutelary deity of the Euphrates island settlement Anath; building complex later used for Roman military record keeping in addition to worship.

name: Temple of Artemis Nanaia
description: Temple located in block H4 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthasia
description: Remains of a sanctuary located on Mt. Kotilon northwest of the Bassai temple.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthia
description: An Archaic temple dedicated to Artemis Orthia within the sanctuary of this goddess at Sparta. The site contained rich deposits of votives and masks.

name: Temple of Artemis Orthosia
description: The Temple of Artemis Orthosia at Epidauros was founded in the Archaic period and continued in use in the Roman period. Pausanias mentions it as being on the city's temples.

name: Temple of Artemis, Jerash
description: The Temple of Artemis in Jerash.

name: Temple of Asclepius
description: The Temple of Asclepius (VIII, 7, 25) in Pompeii is located at the intersection of the Via del Tempio di Iside and the Via Stabiana. It is also referred to as the Temple of Jupiter Meilichius.

name: Temple of Aššur at Assur
description: From the second half of the third millennium BC until 614 BC, there was a temple in Assur dedicated to the city’s tutelary deity Aššur. The temple was excavated by W. Andrae in 1903–05 and 1909–12. The earliest and latest textual records for construction work on Aššur’s temple date respectively to the time of Šalim-aḫum, an early old Assyrian king, and the reign of Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC), Assyria’s last great king. According to the Middle Assyrian king Shalmaneser I (1263–1234 BC) and the Neo-Assyrian ruler Esarhaddon (680–669 BC), Ušpia, a tribal ancestor of the Old Assyrian king Šamšī-Adad I (ca. 1808–1776 BC), was the building’s founder. In 614 BC, when the city was captured by the Medes, the temple was destroyed.

name: Temple of Atargatis
description: Temple located in block H2 at Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Athena (Ilium)
description: The Temple of Athena at Ilion was a peripteral temple executed in the Doric order with a 12 x 6 columnar arrangement. Its creation is traditionally ascribed to Lysimachus (ca. 301-281 BCE), though it was later restored under Roman influence, likely during the principate of Augustus. Little of the temple, save its forecourt, remains in situ. It is mostly known from membra disiecta, including metopes uncovered during Schliemann's excavations.

name: Temple of Athena (Miletus)
description: The Temple of Athena in Miletos dates originally to the sixth century BCE, although the visible remains on site today belong to the Classical rebuilding of MIletos (ca. 479-450 BCE). The temple had a peripteral plan and was executed in the Ionic order. Unusually, its axis is oriented north-south.

name: Temple of Athena (Thasos)
description: An archaic temple of Athena on the acropolis of Thasos was dismantled in the 5th century BCE. Planning, procurement of materials, and construction of its replacement seem to have begun shortly thereafter, but were never finished.

name: Temple of Athena Alea at Tegea
description: An ancient sanctuary at Tegea dedicated to Athena Alea. The extant phase of the temple is attributed to Skopas who was responsible for rebuilding following a fire in 394 B.C.

name: Temple of Athena at Assos
description: A Late Archaic peripteral temple in the Doric order with a 6 x 13 columnar arrangement.

name: Temple of Athena at Paestum
description: A Late Archaic Doric Temple of Athena (ca. 500 B.C.) at Paestum.

name: Temple of Athena at Sounion
description: The Temple of Athena at Sounion (ca. 470 B.C.) with its L-shaped colonnade was built on the site of an earlier temple (sixth century B.C.) at the same location.

name: Temple of Athena Lindia
description: A Doric temple dating ca. 300 BC located on the acropolis of Lindos. The sanctuary stands above a grotto that likely housed the earlier phases of the cult. The amphiprostyle temple has an elongated floor plan and measures 7.75 x 21.65 m.

name: Temple of Athena Nike
description: A temple dedicated to Athena Nike, on a bastion at the southwest end of the Acropolis of Athens. A sanctuary existed from the early 6th century BC, a small shrine was built in the mid-5th century, and the temple was built between 449 and 420 BCE. It was dismantled by the Ottomans in 1686 and reassembled between 1835 and 1844.

name: Temple of Athena Pallenis
description: The Temple of Athena Pallenis was built in the mid-6th century BC and rebuilt on a larger scale in the late 5th century BC. In myth, the location was the site of King Eurystheus' tomb and Theseus' victory over the Pallantidai. In 546 BC, it was the site of the battle by which Peisistratos finally established his tyranny. At the end of the 1st century BC, the structure was disassembled and rebuilt in the Agora of Athens as the Temple of Ares.

name: Temple of Athena Polias
description: Pausanias describes the Temple of Athena Polias at Erythrai as containing a wooden image of the enthroned goddess.

name: Temple of Athena Pronaia
description: The Temple of Athena Pronaia, located at Delphi, is a peripteral Doric building (6 x 12 columns). It was built ca. 500 BC and is the largest building in the sanctuary of Athena Pronaia.

name: Temple of Athena Skiras at Phaleron
description: This sanctuary, reported in Phaleron by Pausanias, is thought to have been located near the modern "old town" of Phaleron in Attica. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras on the island of Salamis, reported by Herodotus.

name: Temple of Augustus
description: The Temple of Augustus in Pula is a Roman temple that dates to the time of Augustus. The tetrastyle Corinthian temple sites a high podium.

name: Temple of Augustus and Rome (Ankara)
description: The Temple of Augustus and Roma at Ankara likely dates ca. 20 BCE. It is also known as the "Monumentum Ancyranum", highlighting the structure's role in preserving the most complete copy of the inscription known as the "Res Gestae Divi Augusti", which was promulgated in both ancient Greek and Latin following the death of emperor Augustus in 14 CE.

name: Temple of Augustus et Livia
description: The so-called Temple of Augustus et Livia dates ca. 10 B.C. and was originally dedicated to Roma and Augustus. The temple was located on the ancient forum of the city. By the fifth century the temple had become a Christian church. It was extensively restored in the nineteenth century.

name: Temple of Augustus, Barcelona
description: The Temple of Augustus dates to the second century A.D.

name: Temple of Ba'al at Palmyra
description: Temple of Ba'al (or Bel) in the ancient city of Palmyra.

name: Temple of Baalshamin
description: Temple in Palmyra dedicated to the Canaanite god Baalshamin.

name: Temple of Bacchus
description: Part of the complex of Roman temples at Baalbek, the Temple of Bacchus is one of the best-preserved temples of the Roman world. It is conventionally dated to the second century CE.

name: Temple of Bêl
description: Temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Bel at Dura-Europos
description: A temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Castor and Pollux (Rome)
description: Located at the southeast corner of the Forum Romanum, the aedes Castoris is a temple of ancient Rome vowed by the dictator Postumius in 499 BCE.

name: Temple of Castor and Pollux, Cora
description: The Temple of Castor and Pollux at Cora was originally built between the fourth and the second centuries B.C. The partially extant architrave carries an inscription of the first century B.C.

name: Temple of Claudius
description: A large classical-style Roman temple built shortly after the conquest of Britannia in A.D. 43 by the emperor Claudius. Its remains lie beneath Colchester Castle.

name: Temple of Concordia, Agrigento
description: The Temple of Concord, located in the so-called "Valle dei Templi" of Agrigento, dates ca. 440-430 B.C.

name: Temple of Cybele
description: The Attalids built a temple at Pessinus to honor Cybele.

name: Temple of Dakka
description: An ancient temple relocated in the 1960s before the completion of the Aswan dam.

name: Temple of Dedwen
description: An ancient Egyptian temple that was dedicated to Dedwen, the Nubian serpent goddess. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979, as part of "Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae". It was relocated to its current location.

name: Temple of Demeter
description: A Doric temple ascribed to Demeter with origins in the fourth century B.C.

name: Temple of Demeter and Kore
description: A Punic and Roman temple sacred to Demeter and Kore located at Bagoi, Perdaxius, Sardinia.

name: Temple of Demeter at the Sanctuary of Demeter Malophoros, Selinus
description: The Temple of Demeter was used for the worship of Demeter (Malophoros), goddess of fertility, grain and agriculture. Built in the seventh century BC, the temple was used continuously until its abandonment ca. 250 BC

name: Temple of Derr
description: A rock-cut temple (speos) located in Lower Nubia, the Temple of Derr was built by Pharaoh Ramesses II (19th Dynasty). The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Amada.

name: Temple of Diana
description: The temple in the forum of Augusta Emerita is conventionally known by the misnomer "Temple of Diana", yet was actually dedicated to the Imperial cult.

name: Temple of Dionysos at Teos
description: A Temple of Dionysos in the Ionic order begun in the Hellenistic period.

name: Temple of Divus Augustus (Nola)
description: According to Cassius Dio, Tiberius dedicated a temple to the deified Augustus at a location marking the site of the princeps' death. Its precise location is not known.

name: Temple of Divus Iulius
description: The temple on the Sacra Via dedicated to the deified Iulius Caesar, authorized in 42 B.C. and built by Augustus, with its dedication occurring on 18th August, 29 B.C.

name: Temple of Ellesyia
description: A small rock-cut temple dedicated to Amun, Horus, and Satis dated to the 18th Dynasty. Originally located near Qasr Ibrim/Premis, the temple was relocated to the Museo Egizio in Turin, Italy, in the 1960s prior to the area's flooding beneath the surface of Lake Nasser.

name: Temple of Enodia
description: Pherai was the main cult center of the goddess Enodia. Today, the foundations of the temple of Enodia (or Ennodia) at found at the site.

name: Temple of Fortuna (Jublains)
description: Centerpoint of sanctuary at modern Jublains

name: Temple of Fortuna Augusta
description: A Corinthian order temple in Pompeii dedicated to Fortuna Redux and likely dating to the late first century BC (VII, 4, 1).

name: Temple of Hadrian
description: The Temple of Hadrian at Rome, also referred to as the Hadrianeum, was a temple located in the Campus Martius dedicated to the deified Hadrian by Antoninus Pius in 145 CE. Some of the temple's substantial remains were incorporated into a 17th-century building designed by architect Carlo Fontana in Rome's Piazza di Pietra.

name: Temple of Hadrian (Ephesus)
description: A small, prostyle Roman temple located on Curetes Street in ancient Ephesus, the Temple of Hadrian was dedicated to the emperor Hadrian in 138 CE by Publius Quintilius.

name: Temple of Hathor
description: The Temple of Hathor at Deir el-Medina dates to the reign of the Ptolemies. It was later repurposed as a Christian installation known as Deir el-Medina or "Monastery of the City", from which the site's modern toponym is derived.

name: Temple of Hathor Miskar
description: A Punic temple founded in the first century B.C.

name: Temple of Hera I (the "Basilica")
description: An Archaic Doric temple probably dedicated to Hera in the central urban sanctuary of Paestum/Poseidonia.

name: Temple of Hera II (Neptune)
description: A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, the so-called Temple of Hera II is also sometimes referred to as the Temple of Neptune.

name: Temple of Hera, Mon Repos
description: An archaic temple dating ca. 610 B.C.

name: Temple of Hera, Olympia
description: The Temple of Hera at Olympia, also known as the Heraion, is a peripteral temple located in the northwest corner of the Altis precinct, dedicated to Hera. Built ca. 600-590 B.C., the Heraion is the oldest temple at Olympia and one of the earliest known examples of the Doric order in Greece.

name: Temple of Heracles
description: The Temple of Heracles is one of the oldest temples in the Valley of the Temples in ancient Akragas, Sicily, dating from around the late 6th century BCE.

name: Temple of Herakles, Cora
description: The Temple of Herakles at Cora was built during the 80s B.C. The temple is tetrastyle in its plan and built following the Etrusco-Italic temple tradition.

name: Temple of Hercules
description: Remains of the fourth-century BCE Temple of Hercules are preserved in the edifice of San Marco d'Alunzio.

name: Temple of Hercules at Amman/Philadelpheia
